context.content:
Xiaolin, I wanted to talk to you about something.

I just got back from the hospital after finishing Mom’s check-up. On the way home, I couldn't stop thinking. I saw the look on your face today. And I know you were right there when Xinyu started crying.

You might think I’m being cold for not changing my mind to take her to the park. But the truth is, I kept thinking that if I didn't stay with Mom for this check-up and something actually went wrong with her health, I’d regret it even more. I was choosing between two things, and I chose the one I felt was more urgent.

But…

It wasn't until I got back from the hospital that I realized, no matter what I choose, someone ends up disappointed. You didn't say anything, but I could tell. When Xinyu was crying and saying, "You always say next time," it really hurt, but I still stuck to my decision. I told myself it was the right thing to do.

Now, I’m not so sure.

It feels like there’s just never enough time. Work just added new requirements, my father’s leg has been acting up more frequently, Mom’s blood sugar still hasn't fully stabilized, and I really wanted to help Xinyu reinforce what she’s learning in first grade... None of these things are a huge deal on their own, but added together, I feel like I’m running on empty.

I’m not trying to make excuses. I just… I want you to know where I’m at right now.

Have you been thinking about something lately, too? It’s been so long since we’ve had a real talk. Not the "who’s picking up the kids" kind of talk, but a real conversation.

I need to hear what’s on your mind.

# November 23, 2023, 9:30 PM

My daughter is asleep, and my parents have retired to their room. Li Xiaolin is in the bathroom. I’m sitting in the study; I haven’t turned on the computer, just staring out the window at the Hangzhou night skyline.

I still feel the need to write down tonight’s conversation. I don’t know why, but I have this feeling that if I don’t put it into words, these thoughts will slowly fade away—just like the last three months. I’ve made so many changes, yet I never truly stopped to ask myself: what was it all for?

After dinner, Li Xiaolin asked me, "What’s on your mind?"

It was a simple question. But I suddenly realized she had asked me that many times before. Every time, I’d say, "Nothing, just a bit tired from work." This time, I didn’t give that answer. I told her that the changes over these past three months were actually a response to her and our daughter’s needs. That they needed a more authentic Zhou Yu, not a perfect one.

After she heard that, she was silent for a long time. Then she said, "All we need is you."

Just that one sentence.

I finally understand what I’ve been doing all this time. I thought I was protecting my family—through perfect planning, precise execution, and never making a mistake. But in reality, I was hiding. Hiding from my true self, hiding from the things I couldn't control, and hiding from the very act of being present.

True care isn't like that. True care is being able to sit down and listen when my daughter says she’s afraid of an exam, instead of immediately drafting a study plan for her. It’s being able to actually talk to my father while we walk, instead of calculating next month’s household expenses in my head.

I used to think that change was a risk, a betrayal of stability. But now I see it clearly—excessive caution is the greatest risk of all. It made me miss out on so much.

When Li Xiaolin said, "All we need is you," I truly felt what it meant to be accepted. Not because of how well I performed, not because of my achievements, but just... me. The real me.

The feeling is unfamiliar, but it’s warm.

Tomorrow, work will go on, my daughter will still go to school, and my parents’ medical checkups still need to be done. Nothing has changed. But I have. Or rather, I’ve finally started to understand myself.

I’m not so afraid anymore.

October 28, 2022 – During my morning run

It’s not even light out yet, and I’m already downstairs. I haven't run in three days, and my body feels stiff.

Something my therapist said yesterday keeps looping in my head: "Perfection is the enemy; acceptance is the friend." I thought about it as I ran—it sounds simple enough, but truly grasping it is another story. I’ve spent so many years chasing the perfect project delivery, the perfect home life, the perfect father figure... and for what? Right now, I’m on the verge of blowing a routine project, I haven't been much help with my daughter’s schoolwork, and my mom’s blood sugar still isn't under control.

Around the third kilometer, I stopped and pulled out my phone to jot down a memo. I needed to get this thought down, otherwise I’ll forget again and spiral right back into that "I’m a failure" loop.

"Perfection is the enemy"—what does that actually mean? Maybe it means not every decision has to be the optimal one. Maybe it means my daughter doing poorly on one exam isn't a personal failure on my part. Maybe it means making a mistake at work doesn't make me a total failure as a human being.

I kept running. My breathing found a rhythm, and that heavy, suffocating feeling in my chest started to lift.

Last night, Xiaoxiao asked me what’s been on my mind. Instead of my usual brush-off—"Nothing, just tired from work"—I actually told her. I told her I was thinking about whether I should change my approach—not necessarily quitting, but maybe looking into other possibilities within the company. She didn't offer advice right away; she just squeezed my hand. In that moment, I felt a little less alone.

Maybe this is how change starts. Not with some grand decision, but just... being willing to say it out loud, being willing to figure it out with her.

Run finished. 5.2 kilometers. A bit less than usual, but I’m not angry about it. I accept where my body is today.

This feels a little different.

Xiaolin, I’m at the office right now, feeling a bit drained. I just wanted to talk to you.

The conversation I had with my parents at home earlier keeps playing over and over in my head. I put together a checklist for them regarding Mom’s blood sugar management—when to test, what to eat, how to exercise. I did so much research and laid out all the statistics. But Dad just said one thing: "You, you’re always trying to control everything."

I was speechless.

I know he was trying to tell me not to be so tense, but the way it came across was… like they don’t want my help? Or that they think I’m overstepping? I don’t know. I just don’t want Mom’s blood sugar to get any worse. Is there something wrong with that?

But think about it—I feel like I’m caught in the middle right now. At work, I have to keep up with the project schedule; I was even late this morning, which has never happened to me before. Then at home, I have to arrange Mom’s checkups, and Dad has that kind of attitude. You have work, too, and Xinyu needs someone to be there for her. Every day I’m calculating my time, trying to figure out which task is more urgent, only to realize… they all seem urgent, yet I can’t do everything well at once.

I’m not complaining. I’m just… a little tired.

Do you think I really am trying too hard to control everything? Sometimes I wonder if I should just loosen up and "let nature take its course" like Dad does. But I can't let it go. Mom’s numbers are right there—how can I just ignore them?

Anyway, maybe I’m just under a lot of pressure lately. What time are you coming back today? Let’s talk more over dinner tonight.

November 3, 2022, 8:15 PM

My finger stopped on the page when Xinyu asked, "Daddy, do you not want to read to me anymore?"

I had been reading the same paragraph over and over. It was the part about the rose in *The Little Prince*; I’d read it twice before Xinyu pointed it out. Her tone wasn't accusatory—it was just a direct question, as if she were asking something very simple. But in that moment, I felt a sharp sting.

I couldn't explain to her what was on my mind. I was mentally calculating my remaining vacation days. If Mom needs to be hospitalized, how much leave can I take? Who will pick Xinyu up from school? Li Xiaolin is on call this week, but what about next week? Can Dad take care of Mom, or do I need to be the one staying at the hospital?

I told Xinyu, "Daddy’s listening, let’s keep going." But we both knew that wasn't true.

I’ve been reading stories for years now—twenty minutes every night. It’s the one thing I’ve always stuck to. I’ve always felt this time was precious. But now, I’m spending this time thinking about my mother’s blood sugar levels, the doctor’s advice, and which tests need to be done. I’m using "problem-solving mode" to tackle a problem I can’t actually solve.

It’s ironic. I’ve spent so many years planning and controlling, making sure everything was under my thumb. Financial spreadsheets, health records, project timelines—I believed that with enough detail and enough caution, I could protect the people I care about. But my mother’s blood sugar is off the charts. I’ve done the research, contacted the hospitals, and drawn up a plan, but none of that changes anything.

And because of this, I am neglecting my daughter.

I was even late for work today. I’m never late. It has never happened in my entire career. I wonder if this is a sign—that I’m losing my grip on life.

Maybe I’ve been lying to myself all along. Maybe this "control" was always an illusion.

I don’t know.

Now Xinyu is asking me to read the same story again before she goes to sleep. I promised I would. This time, I will focus. I have to focus. But I’m not sure if I can.

I wonder, if I can’t even do this right, what else is left for me?

Xiaolin, I... there’s something I want to tell you.

Is our daughter asleep?

Actually, when I was at the tutoring center today, I was sitting in the waiting area watching those kids do their exercises... and I just started crying. It was so sudden, the tears just started falling. I didn’t even know what was happening.

I’m sorry, I know this sounds strange. A grown man crying there... I feel embarrassed too. But sitting there, watching Xinyu working on those problems, watching all those other kids, I just... I broke down.

I’ve been wondering if this means I’ve really failed. The teacher said she needs more foundational practice, so I thought about signing her up for classes and spending more time doing problems with her. But I’m already so exhausted when I get home from work. By the time I finish helping her with homework and reading her stories, my patience is gone, and I even get snappy with her. And then I have to go for walks with my parents, and take my mom for her checkups every month...

Mom’s blood sugar has been high again lately. Looking at her lab results last time, I was thinking I should learn more about how to help her manage her diet... but I can’t even manage my own time.

I know you’re taking care of our daughter and working too. I don’t want to give you more to worry about. But sitting there, I just thought, am I really failing at everything? I’m not a good son, I’m not a good father, and even things at work haven’t been going well lately...

I don’t know what else I can do. I really don’t.

I’m sorry for dumping this on you. I always told you I was fine, just a bit tired from work. But I think it might be more than just work. I think I might be... breaking down.

What do you think?

# Life Memoir - Project Framework

**Date Created**: 2023-12-08  
**Status**: In Progress  
**Priority**: High  

---

## Project Overview

I’ve decided to start documenting and organizing our family stories. This isn't just about putting together a simple timeline; it’s about truly understanding—how we got to where we are today, and what lay behind those seemingly ordinary decisions.

Lately, I’ve been thinking: if I’m gone one day, where will Zhou Xinyu go to learn about her grandparents? What did their generation go through? And how did I, step by step, become the person I am now? If these stories aren't written down, they’ll just scatter and fade.

---

## Main Chapter Framework

### Part I: Their Stories
- **My Father’s Trajectory** — From state-owned enterprise engineer to retirement
  - Why did he choose this path? What were the options back then?
  - Where did his understanding of "following the rules" come from?
  - Adjusting to life after retirement.

- **My Mother’s Story** — Thirty years as a primary school teacher
  - Which students did she teach? What left the deepest impression?
  - How does she view her career choice?
  - Her thoughts on her changing health lately.

### Part II: My Growth
- The clearest fragments from my childhood memories.
- The real reason I chose Computer Science (it wasn't just about "stability").
- My first ten years at this company — what was on my mind back then?
- Where did that sense of stagnation in 2021 come from?

### Part III: Moments of Transition
- Why my perspective started shifting this year.
- Takeaways from the new project.
- A redefined understanding of "stability."
- Changes in my relationships with Li Xiaolin and Zhou Xinyu.

### Part IV: Present & Future
- What I truly see at this moment.
- What kind of story I want to leave for my daughter.

---

## Information Gathering Checklist

**Questions for Dad**:
- [ ] What was your biggest challenge during those years at the state enterprise?
- [ ] Why have you always emphasized "following the rules"? Is it a personal belief or an influence of the era?
- [ ] Looking back, do you have any regrets about your choices?
- [ ] What do you think of the changes I’ve gone through lately?

**Questions for Mom**:
- [ ] After all these years of teaching, what was your proudest moment?
- [ ] What were your expectations for me? Are they still the same?
- [ ] Does the blood sugar issue worry you? How are you managing it?

**Things for me to sort out**:
- [ ] Which childhood moments stand out most? Why those specifically?
- [ ] Why did I decide to marry Li Xiaolin in 2018? What was I thinking then?
- [ ] How do I view the decision to buy the second apartment in 2020 now?
- [ ] What has been my biggest change this past year?

---

## Execution Plan

**December**:
- Organize thoughts and finalize the framework (Complete this week).
- Start conversations with my parents — keep it natural during weekend walks.
- Record things Zhou Xinyu has said or done recently.

**January**:
- In-depth interviews — may need formal sit-downs with recordings or notes.
- Organize the fragments of my own upbringing.

**February - March**:
- Shape the first draft.
- Ask Li Xiaolin to review and add her perspective.

---

## Why I’m Doing This

The initial idea was simple—just to record. But now I realize it’s more than that.

It’s a way of saying: I see your stories, I see my own story, and I am grateful. These stories deserve to be remembered; they deserve to be celebrated.

Moreover, as I write, I am re-understanding myself. Things I took for granted—my father’s teachings, my mother’s presence, every choice I made—are all starting to feel "warm" and meaningful.

This is important for Zhou Xinyu, too. It’s not about preaching to her; it’s about letting her know: where you come from, what your family went through, and that you didn't just appear out of thin air.

---

## Potential Risks & Reflections

- **Time Commitment**: This isn't a project that can be finished in a weekend. I’ll need to find space between work and family. But it’s worth it.
- **Difficulty of Dialogue**: I might discover things I didn't expect or hit on sensitive topics. I need to be patient.
- **Perfectionism**: It’s easy to fall into the trap of "needing to record it perfectly." I have to remind myself—authenticity is more important than perfection.

---

**Next Step**: During the walk with Dad this weekend, see if I can naturally bring up this idea. See how they react.

July 18, 2023, 2:47 AM

Li Xiaolin is asleep. I can hear her breathing; it’s steady.

Xinyu asked me today when Mom and Dad are coming back. I wasn't prepared for that question. I had prepared so much—medical records, medication lists, even a notebook on blood sugar management—but I hadn't prepared for that.

I told her, "They aren't coming back." Just like that. My voice was cold. I heard myself speak, but it sounded like it was coming from a long way off. Her eyes are so big, and she was looking at me. I couldn't bear it, so I walked out.

Li Xiaolin explained something to her later. I don't know what. I sat downstairs for an hour.

I remembered how I recorded my mother’s blood sugar data every month. A1C levels, fasting glucose, medication times. I made spreadsheets in Excel and set up reminders. I thought that was what care looked like. I thought if I just kept detailed enough records, I could... could what? Keep it under control?

In Mom’s diary, she wrote: "So tired." Just those two words.

I didn't see it.

When Xinyu leaned toward me, I just patted her head. That was it. I should have hugged her. I should have said something. But I couldn't say a word. The part of me that is a father seems to have... stopped working. Like a system crash.

I keep thinking, if I had... if I hadn't worked overtime that day... if I had more often...

But none of that matters now.

I’ve never truly taken care of anyone. I’ve just been keeping records. Recording physical exams, recording grades, recording deadlines. I turned life into a spreadsheet, thinking that was how to keep everything under control.

Now I’m sitting here in front of a blank document, not knowing what to write.

I can’t explain it to her.

I can’t explain it to anyone.

I can’t even explain it to myself.

—

Delete.

No, keep it.

Never mind.

Xiaolin, I wanted to talk to you about something.

I spent two hours this afternoon setting up a health tracking system for my mom. I organized her blood sugar levels, test results, and doctor’s advice into spreadsheets, and even added risk assessments and color-coding. At the time, it gave me a bit of a sense of control, but then I suddenly realized... this spreadsheet does absolutely nothing to change her attitude.

She still says the same thing: "At our age, why bother with all this fuss?" I can understand where she’s coming from, but I also know it’s dangerous to keep going like this. It’s just that, besides making spreadsheets and researching data, I don't know what else to do.

Also, Xinyu knocked on my door this afternoon wanting me to play with her, and I just brushed her off saying, "Daddy’s a bit busy right now." I’m still thinking about that.

I know this sounds a bit all over the place. I just... I don't know how to do everything well at the same time. Work, my mom’s health, spending time with Xinyu, taking care of the house... sometimes I wonder if the way I’ve been doing things all along is just fundamentally wrong.

Are you free after work? Can we talk?

## Notes

Spent two hours learning how to operate the blood glucose meter after buying it. Following the doctor's advice, I’m purchasing strips and needles weekly to ensure they stay fresh. The cold chain box is for Mom to carry her insulin when she goes out—she sometimes goes for walks in the park with Dad on weekends, and her injections can't be interrupted.

Looking at the bills, medical expenses this month have increased by nearly 1,400 yuan compared to last month. Including the regular check-ups, the cost of managing Mom's health is definitely rising. I need to readjust the family budget and list this as a separate category.

My daughter's tutoring fees, the mortgage, insurance... every item is squeezing our financial breathing room. Sometimes I think that if it weren't for these unexpected things, our financial planning would have been much more manageable.

---

**Next Steps**:
- Continue recording Mom's daily blood sugar data (Excel sheet created)
- Accompany Mom for her follow-up appointment next week; consult the doctor on whether to adjust the insulin dosage
- Recalculate this year's medical budget; may need to reallocate some funds from the education fund

[Appointment message with therapist]

Doctor, I’m sorry to bother you so late. I wanted to see if we could move up our next session, or rather... I’m in need of some advice right now.

Something unpleasant happened with Li Xiaolin last night. She asked me what I was avoiding, and I didn't give her a real answer—I just said I was tired from work. Then I turned my back to her and faced the wall. She ended up leaving the bedroom.

I know it might not sound like a big deal, but it’s the first time for us. We’ve never given each other the cold shoulder like that before. I can feel that she’s hurt.

The problem is... I *am* avoiding something. But I can’t quite put my finger on what it is. Is it my parents? Work? Or... myself?

Lately, I’ve been dwelling on one question, maybe thinking too much. I’ve always lived my life by the book, making every decision carefully and controlling every variable. But none of that has helped me solve anything. I’m thinking maybe I need to come in for sessions more frequently. Or... could you tell me, how does a person realize when they might be heading in the wrong direction?

Sorry for being so scattered. This is just where I am right now.

Stability does not mean stagnation. Change does not have to be grand or loud.

# MEMO

**Date**: December 18, 2023, 14:30  
**Subject**: Redefining Competitiveness — Meeting Notes and Follow-up Reflections

---

Something I said during this morning’s departmental meeting has been playing on a loop in my head ever since.

We were discussing the delivery cycle for the new project. When a colleague suggested compressing the timeline, I said it bluntly: "Competitiveness isn't about overtime hours; it’s about mental health and life satisfaction."

The room went silent for a second or two. I could feel people processing it. My supervisor didn't respond directly, but didn't push back either. In the end, we moved forward with a reasonable schedule.

I surprised even myself by saying it. It’s not that I haven’t thought this way before, but I’ve never voiced it so candidly in a formal setting. In the past, I would have wrapped the same sentiment in corporate jargon like "improving efficiency" or "optimizing workflows," essentially catering to that stale workplace logic: time input equals value output.

But over the past few months, I’ve come to see clearly that the logic itself is flawed.

I want to record why this perspective matters so much to me:

**First, it challenges my internalized beliefs.** My father’s generation—state-owned enterprise engineers—lived by the rule that "following the routine" was everything. Overtime was dedication; exhaustion was a badge of honor. I was steeped in these values from childhood and naturally carried them into my adult life. But what I’ve observed over the years is that the most effective people are often those with orderly lives and stable mindsets. It’s not that they don't work hard; it’s that their effort is sustainable. My current work output is actually better than it was during my peak overtime years. That is no coincidence.

**Second, it’s about organizational health.** Our company keeps saying "talent is our greatest asset," but if our culture encourages people to overextend themselves, that’s just empty talk. I believe a truly competitive organization shouldn't squeeze short-term output out of exhausted employees; it should gain long-term innovation and stability by respecting the wholeness of their lives. This might be a bigger idea I want to push within the company.

**Third, it is directly linked to my personal transformation.** Much of my previous anxiety stemmed from an internal conflict: I wanted to be a good project manager, a good father, and a good son, but my method was simply to "double down" on everything. The result was failing at every role. Now I understand that true competitiveness isn't about winning everywhere; it’s about knowing what matters most and having the courage to choose accordingly. I can be a project manager who doesn't work late into the night and a father who reads to his daughter every evening. These roles aren't in conflict—they support each other.

**Questions I want to explore further:**

- What is true competitiveness in our industry and in this era?
- What is the real relationship between organizational health and individual well-being?
- Can I drive concrete changes within the company, such as including "employee mental health metrics" as a success criterion in new project proposals?
- How does this viewpoint connect to my understanding of "thought leadership"? I don't want to be high-profile, but I want to be someone who asks meaningful questions.

This isn't a final answer; it’s just a beginning. But I feel the direction is right.

---

**Note**: I want to bring this up in my session with my therapist tomorrow. Perhaps we can explore it from the angle of "how to remain authentic in the workplace."

# Hospital Consultation Records & Comparative Analysis

**Date**: 2022.12.04  
**Location**: Hangzhou First People's Hospital / Zhejiang Provincial People's Hospital (Waiting Room)

---

## I. Summary of Test Results

**Mother’s Latest Metrics** (Dec 02 Checkup)
- Fasting Blood Glucose: 7.8 mmol/L (↑ up from 7.2 last month)
- HbA1c: 6.9% (Officially entered pre-diabetes range)
- ECG: No significant abnormalities, but the doctor recommended a 24-hour Holter monitor
- Blood Pressure: 138/82 (Mildly elevated)

**Advice from Hangzhou First People's Hospital**:
- Start glucose-lowering medication (Metformin) immediately
- Follow-up check every 3 months
- Emphasized that "early intervention is critical"

**Advice from Zhejiang Provincial People's Hospital**:
- Focus on lifestyle adjustments first; re-evaluate in 3 months
- Start medication only if blood glucose continues to rise
- "Avoid over-medicalization"

---

## II. My Analysis

The two doctors disagree. The First Hospital is more aggressive, while the Provincial Hospital is more conservative.

I’ve looked up some data:
- If left uncontrolled, there is a 60% probability of developing full-blown diabetes within 5 years.
- However, some studies show that lifestyle intervention has an efficacy rate of up to 58%.

My mother’s current stance: She doesn't want to take medicine and feels that her "health is still fine."

---

## III. Potential Persuasion Strategies

**Option A - The Data Pressure Method**:
- Show her the HbA1c data curve.
- Compare her results with checkup reports of friends her age (are there any examples?).
- Emphasize the success rate of "early intervention."

**Option B - Worst-Case Scenario Analysis**:
- Find a few cases where "refusal of treatment led to complications."
- Explain the severity of diabetic foot and kidney failure.
- (But would this be too terrifying?)

**Option C - The Compromise**:
- Agree to follow the Provincial Hospital’s advice on lifestyle changes for now.
- But require her cooperation in following a detailed diet plan I create.
- Re-test in 1 month (don't wait for 3).
- If there’s no improvement, start the medication.

---

## IV. To-Do List

- [ ] Compile a "Pre-diabetes Lifestyle Guide" for Mom.
- [ ] Accompany her for the 24-hour Holter monitor next week.
- [ ] Check the credentials of the doctor at the Provincial Hospital (is he reliable?).
- [ ] Talk to my wife and see what she thinks.

---

## V. Self-Reflection

I realize what I’m doing.

When I was on the subway searching for "failed cases of elderly patients refusing treatment," I knew something was off. I am trying to use worst-case scenarios to scare my parents into submission rather than truly helping them make a decision.

But I didn't stop. I kept recording data, comparing medical opinions, and drafting "persuasion strategies." It’s as if I believe that as long as I have enough information, I can control the situation.

Maybe that’s my problem lately. Every time my mother’s blood sugar rises by 0.1, I feel a sense of powerlessness. So, I use more data and more plans to fight that feeling.

But this doesn't seem to be actually solving the problem.

Maybe I should just sit down and have a real talk with her—not to persuade her, but to truly listen to what she thinks. I just don't know how to do that. Right now, every time I discuss this with her, I’m so rushed, as if I’m racing to complete a task.

My wife said yesterday, "You’ve been so busy lately, but you look more exhausted than ever."

I didn't answer. Because I didn't know what to say.

---

**Note**: This memo is just my thought process. Some ideas might not be entirely rational. But at least I’ve written them down.

I just sat there and listened without making any excuses. In the end, I promised to submit a detailed analysis proposal to them.

July 8, 2022, Night

I sat in the office for a while, but I’ve already left early. On the drive to pick up Xinyu, my mind is still racing.

What happened this afternoon keeps gnawing at me. During the risk assessment meeting, I actually overlooked the risk points for the third-party API integration. This rarely happens to me. My supervisor didn't point it out in front of everyone, but I saw that look in his eyes. I caught it myself after the meeting and rushed to update the report.

This shouldn't have happened. I’ve always made a living by being meticulous and reliable. "Dependable" is my label. And now?

Back at my desk, I opened that hidden Excel file. The financial model—I rarely look at it. It’s filled with various hypothetical scenarios: how long the family could last if I quit; what the mortgage pressure would be if we bought another apartment; what percentage my initial income would drop if I changed careers. I don't know when I started making this; maybe in the last few months.

Looking at those numbers, I wonder: maybe the problem isn't career development itself. Maybe it’s that I can no longer maintain being "dependable."

That thought is terrifying.

Have I failed? I don't mean failing to get a promotion, but rather—am I losing the very thing I’ve always trusted most about myself?

I left work early to pick up Xinyu, and she was so happy to see me at the school gate. Sitting in the car, she chattered away about school. I listened while driving, but a corner of my mind was still asking myself that same question.

She said the teacher praised her math today. I should be happy for her, and I was. But at the same time, I remembered the teacher saying she "needs to strengthen her basic training" at the last parent-teacher meeting. Am I failing my daughter, too?

While reading her a story tonight, I tried to be fully present, but my focus was still scattered. She asked, "Daddy, what are you thinking about?" I said, "Nothing, Daddy’s listening to your story."

It was a small lie, but it made me even more certain of one thing—something is not right with my current state.

Maybe I need to sort out my thoughts properly tomorrow. Or, I should talk to Xiaolin. She asked me recently if I was under a lot of pressure at work. I told her it was nothing, just that I was a bit tired.

But looking at it now, "a bit tired" might be an understatement.

I need to figure out whether all of this is just a temporary fluctuation or some kind of deeper signal.

# August 18, 2023, 11:45 PM

Insomnia again.

I’ve been lying in bed for three hours now. Li Xiaolin fell asleep long ago. I can hear her steady breathing. Next door is our daughter’s room—today she asked me again why Grandpa isn’t here anymore. I didn’t know how to answer. I told her, "Grandpa went somewhere far away," and she asked, "Then when is he coming back?"

I didn't answer.

I took out my notebook, my fingers lingering on the page for a long time. I want to write something, but I don’t know where to start. Or rather, there’s nothing left to start.

Why couldn't I stop it?

This question has looped in my head a hundred times. I did everything I was "supposed" to do—accompanied him to checkups every month, recorded every data point, controlled his diet, reminded him to exercise. I even listed all his health indicators in an Excel sheet, color-coding the risk levels. I managed it like a project. Meticulous, thorough, leaving nothing out.

And then? He still left.

All the plans, all the prevention, everything I thought I could control—it all turned into a joke in the face of death.

Li Xiaolin says I should see a therapist. Her tone is gentle, but I can see the worry in her eyes. I asked her, "Talk about what? Talk about how much of a failure I am?" She didn't answer; she just moved closer, trying to take my hand. I pulled away instinctively.

I don’t want her near me. It’s not that I dislike her, it’s just... I feel like something broken, something contagious. She deserved a better husband. A husband who wouldn't fall apart after his father died. A husband who could protect his family.

But I can’t protect anything.

My daughter needs "remedial training" at school. That’s what the teacher said. When I heard those words, I felt like the whole world was collapsing. I started messaging the teacher constantly, asking about every single detail. Li Xiaolin says I’m being "excessive." Maybe. But I can’t stop. If I can’t even manage my daughter’s studies, then what else can I do?

I’m still here. My father is gone, but I’m still here. I wake up at 6:30 every morning, go for a run, come back, work, manage projects, and make sure no one is inconvenienced. Everything is as usual. As if nothing ever happened.

But everything has happened.

Sometimes I wonder, if I also... no, I can’t think like that. My daughter still needs me. My wife still needs me. Even if I am a failure, at least I’m still here.

At least I’m still here.

...

I don’t know if that’s enough.

# Diary

**Tuesday Afternoon, November 28, 2023**

I just got back from the office, and there’s still a lingering warmth in my chest that won't dissipate.

It’s been three hours since the presentation ended, but I find myself replaying those moments over and over—not what I said, but the feeling of looking into the audience's eyes. It was strange. I always thought public speaking was just about delivering prepared content, but today was different. When I said, "True stability comes from embracing uncertainty," I saw people nodding; some frowned in thought, and others had a spark in their eyes—resonance, perhaps? I’m not sure, but in that moment, my nerves vanished.

A colleague approached me this afternoon and said, "You said exactly what I’ve been wanting to say but didn't dare to." I didn't know how to react at first. Were they waiting for a specific response? I was just telling the truth. Then others came by, and even managers from other departments asked if we could chat about my management philosophy.

It’s odd. I didn’t do anything special. I just stopped trying to control everything perfectly.

Last night, Li Xiaolin asked if I was nervous, and I said I was fine. Actually, I was a little scared, but not of saying the wrong thing. I was afraid that—once I spoke the truth—there would be no going back. No going back to being that "reliable but unremarkable" person. But thinking about it now, why would I want to go back?

My daughter came home from school today and said her teacher praised her for the progress she made on her homework this week. Instead of immediately checking every answer like I used to, I asked her where she felt she had improved. She thought for a moment and said, "I’m not as afraid of making mistakes anymore." Hearing that, something suddenly clicked for me.

I’ve been teaching her perfection, but in reality, I was teaching her fear.

While walking with my father this evening, he asked how I was doing. I told him there were some changes at work. He didn't press for details; he just nodded. He never asks too much. But I felt like he was listening. We just walked like that, not saying much, yet I felt he understood what I was going through. Maybe he had a moment like this when he was young—that moment when you realize that following the script isn't the only way to live.

I need to get one thing straight: this isn't me turning my back on stability. Quite the opposite. I understand now that true stability isn't about avoiding all risks; it’s about choosing which risks to take once you know what you want. Excessive caution is a risk in itself—the risk of missing out, of stagnation, of never truly being present.

What happened this month? Back in October, I was still a mix of excitement and fear. Now, the excitement remains, but the fear has faded significantly. In its place is a sense of certainty. Not the certainty of "knowing how things will turn out," but the certainty of "knowing what I am doing."

There’s a lot of work to handle tomorrow. But tonight, I just want to sit here for a while and let this feeling linger.

I’ve been sitting in the parking lot for almost twenty minutes.

My boss called me into the office earlier. He said there was an issue with the requirements assessment for the recent e-commerce project. A loophole that should have been caught in the second week didn't surface until now. He was very polite about it, but I could read between the lines—"performance has slipped."

I’ve never felt like this before. Not once in ten years.

Sitting in the car now, I have several unread messages on my phone. My mom’s check-up results came back, and her blood sugar is up again. My daughter’s teacher wants to call me at 3:00 PM because there are still issues with her recent math exercises. And then there’s a mountain of work to-dos.

I don’t really know how to tell you this.

Actually, I don’t even know what happened. Everything was fine a while ago, but now it feels like I can’t do anything right. I’m making mistakes at work; I’m distracted when I’m with my daughter—I even lost my temper with her last week just because she didn't finish her homework. I know it was wrong, but I couldn't control it. I haven't figured out how to handle my mom’s situation either; I need to catch up on the medical side of things, but I don't even have the time.

You asked me the other day if something was wrong at work, and I said it was nothing, just that I was tired. But now I feel like something is off. It’s not just about being tired.

I feel... a bit lost.

But maybe I’m just in a slump lately. Tomorrow should be better. I’ll fix the issues with the project, spend some quality time helping my daughter review, and then sort out my mom’s medical records. I just need to pull myself together.

Sorry, I shouldn’t be telling you all this at this hour. Are you at the hospital?

Let’s talk when I get home tonight.

Xiaolin, there’s something I need to tell you.

I’ve been thinking about what happened in our daughter’s room just now. I don’t want to keep using "being tired from work" as an excuse to brush you off anymore.

Today, I replaced Xinyu’s story time with math practice. There were a few problems she couldn't solve, and I... I used a really harsh tone and asked her, "How can you not know how to do something this simple?" You should have seen the look in her eyes. I saw it, but I didn't stop right away.

I stood in the bathroom for a long time afterward, asking myself over and over what kind of father I am.

I know this isn't your fault, and it's not hers. It’s mine.

Lately, I feel like I’m being pulled apart. At work, I messed up that module review, and now I feel this desperate need to prove I’m still reliable. With your mom’s blood sugar management, there’s so much I still need to learn; before every check-up, I’m terrified the numbers will be high again. And with Xinyu’s studies, her teacher said she needs to strengthen her fundamentals, which just makes me feel like I haven't spent enough time with her. All these things are weighing on me at once, and I just can't keep up with all of them.

I tried to just "push through" and handle things like I used to, but it’s not working anymore. I feel like I’m failing as an employee, failing as a son, and failing as a father. Every role demands something from me, but my time and energy...

I’m sorry. I shouldn't have treated Xinyu that way. And I shouldn't have kept hiding it from you by saying "it's nothing."

I honestly don't know what to do right now. But I wanted you to at least know what I’m going through.

— Zhou Yu

Xiaolin, just finished chatting with my boss.

I felt like sharing some thoughts with you. It’s nothing bad, just... a different kind of feeling.

He told me he can see that I’m already changing. I was a bit taken aback when he said that, because honestly, I’m still just trying to wrap my head around this transfer. But hearing him say it suddenly made me feel a lot less alone.

He also said something that I think echoes what we talked about before. He said, "We need you to be able to tolerate some imperfection." Do you remember when we were discussing life plans, and you said I always try to keep everything under perfect control? I didn't say much at the time, but I’ve been thinking about it ever since.

Now that my boss has said it too, it’s finally clicking—maybe this is exactly what I need to learn. It’s not about lowering my standards, but about giving myself permission to make mistakes while exploring. Allowing a new project to be less than perfect, and allowing myself to be something other than just the "reliable but uninspired" one.

To be honest, I’m still a bit nervous. It’s a new project, a new team, and I’m not entirely sure if what we’re building will work. But this nervousness feels different. Before, it was "I absolutely cannot mess up"; now, it feels more like "I want to see what I’m capable of creating."

I get the feeling you’ve been waiting for me to say all this. I know you’ve sensed my anxiety. Thank you for not pushing me, and for just being there.

Let’s talk more over dinner tonight? I’d love to hear your thoughts.

Saturday, November 18, 2023
Night

Today felt a little different.

I didn't follow the schedule this morning, which in itself made me feel a bit uneasy. Li Xiaolin said, "Let's forget the schedule today and just play with Xinyu." My first instinct was to bring up the Sunday homework checklist, but in the end, I kept it to myself.

In the garden downstairs this afternoon, Xinyu suddenly pointed at a butterfly. My initial thought was to snap a photo for the records and keep walking. But she grabbed my hand, her eyes following the butterfly as it fluttered about. So, I knelt down.

I stayed there for ten minutes. Just watching the butterfly, listening to Xinyu talk about where it might be going, and hearing her ask if it was looking for its home. I didn't explain the biology of butterflies, nor did I rush her by saying, "It's time to go home." I just... stayed with her.

My legs were a bit sore when I finally stood up. On the walk back, I said to Li Xiaolin, "I feel like I actually spent time with Xinyu today."

She just smiled and didn't say a word.

Tonight, while Dad was watching TV, I sat down beside him. He suddenly turned his head, smiled, and said, "You finally understand."

I didn't ask him what he meant. I knew exactly what he was talking about.

All these years, I’ve been busy making plans, checking boxes, and keeping records. My daughter’s grades, Mom’s blood sugar, the household bills, project deadlines. It was as if I believed that as long as I controlled everything well enough, nothing could go wrong. But sitting there today watching that butterfly, I realized—I’ve missed so much.

It’s not that planning isn’t important. I still have to do it. But I’ve been treating "doing it perfectly" as proof of my presence, as my way of showing love.

Xinyu doesn't need a perfect father. She just needs me to... be there. Truly be there.

I’m a little tired, but I also feel a sense of relief.

It feels like I’ve finally set down something very heavy.

# Work Log

**Date:** Friday, July 8, 2022  
**Project:** E-commerce Platform System Development - Q3 Phase  
**Recorded by:** Zhou Yu  

---

## Incident Record

During this afternoon’s risk assessment meeting, an oversight was identified—the integration risks for the third-party payment API were not included in the contingency plan. This is a rare occurrence in my professional record.

After the meeting, my supervisor didn't call me out in front of everyone, but I could feel the weight of that moment of silence. He simply said, "Zhou Yu, we need to supplement this section." I nodded, and another red line was marked in my notebook.

I couldn't stop thinking about this on the drive home.

---

## Root Cause Analysis

**Direct Cause:** Inadequate preparation. The risk checklist for the third-party API should have been completed last week, but I rushed to put it together only this morning.

**Deep-seated Reason (Honest Reflection):** My focus has indeed been scattered lately.

- Last week, I accompanied my mother for her blood sugar follow-up. The results were worse than expected, and I spent two days researching data and adjusting her diet logs.
- My daughter’s math quiz scores dropped this week. The teacher’s feedback made me anxious, and I spent Wednesday night discussing whether she needs extra tutoring with Li Xiaolin.
- I unconsciously pushed project documentation down the priority list.

I don’t want to make excuses for myself, but the fact remains: my energy has been divided across too many fronts.

---

## Remedial Measures

**Immediate Action (Tonight):**
- Conduct a comprehensive review of all risk points for third-party API integration (payment timeouts, exception returns, network fluctuations, version compatibility).
- Develop a detailed contingency plan and rollback strategy.
- Submit to my supervisor for review before 9:00 AM tomorrow.

**Process Improvement:**
- Reinstate my previous habit of "Thursday Afternoon Project Reviews" and stop letting ad-hoc tasks interrupt them.
- Complete risk assessment checklists three days in advance to allow time for internal review.
- Set earlier deadline reminders in the project management system.

---

## Reflection

This shouldn’t have happened.

For eight years, my professional reputation has been built on the word "reliable." No delays, no major omissions, and never making the team pay for my mistakes. This is the baseline I set for myself.

But today, I didn't do well enough.

Perhaps the pressure has been high lately. Perhaps I need to find a better balance between work and family. Perhaps I need to admit that there are some things I simply cannot do perfectly all at once.

However, admitting the problem is one thing; improving is another. Tomorrow, I will make this remedial plan even more solid than expected.

---

**Next Follow-up Date:** July 9, 2022  
**Target Status:** Risk assessment plan approved by supervisor; project proceeding as scheduled.

# Diary

**July 4, 2023, 10:30 PM**

Sitting in the study, my father’s engineering manuscripts are still piled high on the desk. I’ve been scanning them page by page; my fingers are starting to ache.

While sorting through his belongings this afternoon, I came across a family photo. It must have been taken when I was in elementary school. Mom is wearing that blue dress, smiling so happily, her eyes sparkling. I’ve never seen her quite like that—not as the "mother who needs care," but as a young woman full of life.

It suddenly hit me that at that age, she must have had so many dreams. Not just teaching, but other things too. But what happened later? Later, she became the person in my memory—eating meals on time, regular checkups, blood sugar monitoring, making sure she rested. In our last conversation before she got sick, she even told me, "Don't sacrifice your life to take care of us."

I didn't truly listen back then.

Sitting here now, I finally understand—I turned myself into a checklist. Checking her medical reports, recording blood sugar data, planning finances, managing everything. I thought that was what "taking care" meant. But in reality, I was just using "perfect management" to pretend I had control over something. I can't control anything. I couldn't even control her life.

Among the manuscripts, there’s a slip of paper in my father’s handwriting, with some engineering ideas and dates. I’ve decided to organize these and write a memoir for him. Not to complete another task on a list, but... to protect him. To protect the fact that he once existed, once thought, and once planned.

This thought makes me feel different. It’s not that tense feeling of needing to prove myself, but something gentle and necessary. It’s as if I finally understand that care isn't about control; it’s about remembering.

Mom is still at the rehabilitation center. The doctor says her condition is stabilizing. I think of the look in her eyes in that old photo, and I think of what she said to me.

Maybe I should stop turning life into a program. Maybe I should learn to allow myself some unplanned moments, just like she did when she was young.

I’ll talk to my therapist about this tomorrow. There’s still so much I can’t quite untangle yet.

But at least, tonight, I’ve decided one thing—I’m going to write that memoir for my father. Not out of obligation, but out of love.

It feels different.

Xiaolin, I’ve been sitting in the office for a while now.

I just got back from a meeting, and my mind is still racing with what you said last night. You said, "I see you changing"—and I’ve been thinking about what that actually means.

I think I understand now. It’s not that I’ve suddenly become a different person; it’s that you saw something in me that has always been there, something I just never allowed myself to look at. Those ideas, those things I wanted to try—they were always there, just buried under the weight of "stability" and "planning."

When I was discussing the project with my supervisor this afternoon, I didn't start by listing all the risks and constraints like I usually do. Instead, I led with the directions we could explore. He looked a bit surprised, but then he really listened. That wouldn't have happened before.

I’m not being reckless. I’m just doing the same things in a different way—still being thorough, but no longer bound by fear.

Do you have some time this afternoon? I looked over Xinyu’s homework; she’s made progress since last week. I want to ask her what’s been on her mind when I read with her tonight.

Thank you for not letting me off the hook last night.

August 10, 2023, past 10 PM

I don’t know how to write this. My fingers are shaking.

Father is sitting on the sofa, staring at the TV, but I know he isn’t actually watching anything. I brought him a glass of warm water, but he didn’t take it. I said, "Dad, have some water," and only then did he slowly turn his head. In that split second, I saw it—not exhaustion, but a kind of hollowness I’ve never seen before.

He said, "Don’t bother. Your mother is gone, and I don’t want to live anymore either."

Just like that. He said it so calmly. Like he was talking about the weather.

I stood there, glass in hand, completely at a loss for words. Did I want to say something? Yes. But nothing would come out. My mind was racing—should I try to talk him out of it? How? What could I even say? "Dad, you still have me"? "Dad, you still have Xinyu"? How hypocritical those words sound. Just like everything I’ve done over the past three months—the monthly check-up records, the blood sugar monitoring, the meticulously prepared meals—it was all just self-deception.

I thought I was taking care of them. I thought my plans, my caution, my "reliability" could change something. And the result? Mom is still gone. She wrote "I'm so tired" in her diary, and I didn't see it. I didn't see it. I was recording her data every single month, yet I failed to see how tired she was.

Now Father sits before me and tells me bluntly that he wants to die. And I—this project manager, this person who knows how to assess risks and create detailed plans—I am utterly powerless. I don't even know how to respond.

In the end, I just set the glass down and walked back to my room. I said nothing.

This is who I am. A failure of a son. A failure of a husband. A failure of a father. When I was reading a storybook to Xinyu, she asked me, "Why does Grandpa always look so sad?" I told her, "Grandpa is just tired." I was lying to her. I’m lying to everyone. Actually, I’m lying to myself.

If only I had noticed sooner... if only I could have...

No. It’s useless to think like that. Everything is useless.

Everything I’ve believed in for 39 years—following the steps, careful planning, risk control—has completely collapsed in the face of reality. No matter how careful I am, I can’t protect anyone. I can’t do anything.

Li Xiaolin is asleep. She’s been exhausted these past few days, too. I don’t want to burden her any further.

I sit here, staring at the dark screen, unable to figure out how to face tomorrow.

I wanted to tell you about what happened tonight.

Just now, after I finished reading a bedtime story to Xinyu, I pulled out my father’s diary. I’ve been reading it for the past two months, but tonight felt different.

Before, when I read it, my mind was always thinking—look, here he mentions my grades slipping in high school, that was my failure; here he says he’s worrying about my job, that was me letting him down. It was like I was looking for evidence to prove how unworthy I am.

But tonight, for some reason, I shifted my perspective. I started looking for his hopes for me, rather than my failures.

I turned to the very last page, where he wrote: "Our greatest happiness is seeing him live a stable and fulfilling life." The date was last November.

I stared at it for a long time, and then I wrote next to it: "My father’s expectations were simple. I have been meeting them all along."

The moment I finished writing that sentence, the tears started falling. There was no sound, they just kept falling.

I realized something strange—maybe all these years, my existence hasn't been a burden to my parents. Maybe my existence itself was a gift to them. They never needed me to be someone extraordinary; they just needed me to be alive, and to live well.

I’m very confused right now. This realization feels like it changes everything, yet nothing at all. But something has definitely loosened.

I want to talk about this in depth during our next session. I also want to understand—if this is how my parents saw me, then what about Li Xiaolin? What about Xinyu? Has my understanding of myself been wrong too?

I need help sorting through all of this.

Xiaolin... I’ve been sitting downstairs at the hospital for a while.

Just now, I was going through my mom’s bag in her ward, looking for her medical insurance card. And then... then I saw her diary fall out.

I shouldn’t have read it. But I did.

She wrote: "Yu’er is so tired, I can tell. Every time he comes to take me for my checkups, his eyes are bloodshot. I don’t want to be a burden to him anymore."

The date was from last month.

I... I just sat there and cried. The kind where no sound comes out. My shoulders wouldn't stop shaking.

You know, all these years, I thought I was the one taking care of her. The monthly checkups, logging her blood sugar levels, the weekend walks. I thought I was protecting her. But in reality... she’s been sensing my exhaustion all along. My "care" actually became a burden to her.

I’m a failure as a son.

I haven't even sat down and truly talked to her. I was just checking off a list. It was like managing a project—checklists, deadlines, risk assessments. But she’s my mom, not a project.

Now she’s lying in the ICU, and all I can think... the first thing that came to my mind wasn't "Mom, stay strong," it was "I didn't treat her right."

I’m sorry, I don’t know how to say this.

My head is a mess right now. The doctor said there will be a further assessment tomorrow morning. I... I don’t know what to hope for.

When do you get off work? I... I don’t want to be alone.

# 3:20 AM

I’ve been sitting in the kitchen for a long time now. I don’t know how long. My phone screen flickered to life—it’s Li Xiaolin’s alarm; she has work tomorrow. I didn’t feel the urge to turn it off, nor did I get up to go to the bedroom. I just sat here.

I haven’t changed my clothes in two days. This grey T-shirt. I can smell myself—a bit sour, a bit foreign. I wasn’t like this before. I used to wake up at 6:30 every morning, go for a run, shower, and put on clean clothes. Now... now those routines feel like someone else’s life.

Xinyu cried for a while, then stopped. I didn’t go to her. Li Xiaolin got up to soothe her, and now they’re both back to sleep. When I heard her crying, my mind was a total blank. Just... empty. I should feel guilty, shouldn't I? But I feel nothing. That scares me even more.

I’m still thinking about my father’s diary. That one sentence keeps circling in my head: "Zhou Yu is too much of a perfectionist; sometimes I wonder if this is my fault."

It is your fault.

It suddenly occurred to me that I never said those words to him. I never asked him why he raised me that way. I just... lived my life step by step. I did exactly what he taught me to do. And then I did the same thing to Xinyu. Checking her grades, making sure her foundation was solid, planning out her every move.

I am repeating him. And in his diary, he said he regretted it.

I cried at that moment. A silent cry. The tears just fell, and I didn't wipe them away.

Sitting here now, I wonder—everything I’ve done to Xinyu, am I protecting her or hurting her? I say I’m doing it for her own good, but what’s the truth? I’m imposing something on her that I inherited from my father—something I’m not even sure is right.

I didn't go to her when she cried. This is the real me. Not the "good father" who reads her bedtime stories every night. That’s me too, but right now... right now I can’t even manage that.

Why do I keep going?

I’ve asked this question a hundred times in my head. Why do I still work? Why do I still plan? Why do I still pretend that life has meaning? My father is dead. Everyone dies. Including me. Including Xinyu. So what is all of this—the mortgage, the education fund, the career path, the expectations for my daughter—what is it all for?

Is it a fight against the void? Or is it the void itself?

 the herbal tea has gone cold in the cup. It’s from yesterday. I didn’t drink it.

It’s still dark outside. It will be a long time before the sun comes up.

I don’t know what I’m supposed to do when it gets light.

It only clicked today—I’ve been "completing" this instead of "being there." Flipping pages fast, like I’m checking off a task. When she asks questions, I give perfunctory answers while my mind drifts to tomorrow’s projects.

That changes starting today.

Every night from 8:00 to 8:20. It can’t be less, and it can’t just be a formality. I need to truly listen. Her little childhood thoughts are probably more interesting than anything I’ve been thinking about lately.

During this window, phone down. Put work aside for a moment.

July 15, 2022, 9:00 PM

Li Xiaolin is already asleep. I’m sitting in the study; my daughter’s English textbook is still spread out on the desk, and I haven't cleared it away.

Just now, she asked me what was wrong. I told her it was nothing, just that I was a bit tired from work. But she didn't buy it. Instead, she said I’ve been trying to control everything lately—checking her work emails, checking Xinyu’s grades, checking Mom’s medical reports, checking the bills, checking the schedule. She said it’s as if I’m using these things to fill myself up, but that I’m actually terrified.

I didn't argue. Because she’s right.

I said something I would never usually say: I feel fragmented.

The moment I said it, I realized just how long I’ve been in this state. Professionally, I’m trapped—no way out, and no courage to find one. At home, I need to take care of my parents, be there for my daughter, and hold the family together. These are all the right things to do, all things I should do. But added together, it’s like being pulled in four different directions at once, and I am slowly being torn apart.

I’m not doing well enough. Not for my daughter, not for my parents, not for my wife, and not at my job.

She tried to hug me. My body went stiff. I don’t even know why I resisted. Maybe it’s because I feel I don’t deserve comfort, or maybe I’m afraid that if I let go for even a second, I’ll never be able to stand up again.

She said, "I’m sorry, I’m not blaming you." But I’m the one who should apologize. I told her, "I’m sorry. I’m probably not doing a good enough job." Saying it out loud, I sounded like a failure.

Now she’s asleep, and I’m still sitting here.

I wonder when the things I’ve always believed in—following the rules, prioritizing stability, not being a burden to others—turned into a trap? I’ve never taken a risk, never truly fought for anything; I’ve just been completing one task after another, like a machine. And now that the machine is starting to make noise, I realize it’s been exhausted for a long time.

Work tomorrow. Project review next week. Parents still need care. Xinyu still needs me.

None of that will change. The only thing that has changed is that I now know—I’m pretending everything is fine, but nothing is fine.

I don’t know where to go from here.

November 8, 2023 – Just past 11:00 PM

Sitting in the study. My daughter is asleep, my wife is watching a show, and my parents have retired for the night. It’s a rare moment of quiet.

The workshop from today is still spinning in my head.

This afternoon, when the colleagues from the design department proposed that "rapid iteration, learning through failure" plan, my first instinct was—it’s too risky. I could feel that familiar, almost reflexive vigilance kicking in. *This won't work; that direction is flawed; we need a more rigorous process...* The words were right on the tip of my tongue.

But I stopped myself.

I didn't shoot it down immediately. I listened as they continued, my mind racing. Then, I proposed a compromise—rapidly iterate and test non-core functions, but maintain strict control over the core issues. When I finished speaking, I wasn't actually sure if it was the right call.

And then... they clapped. My supervisor nodded.

I didn't expect that.

It makes me feel a bit uneasy. Not unhappy, just... a sense of unfamiliarity. It’s as if I did something I’ve never done before. I allowed some uncertainty to exist, yet I didn't completely relinquish control. It sounds simple, but for me... it feels like I’ve crossed some kind of threshold.

Sitting here and reflecting on it, I find myself asking: Is this real change? Or did I just happen to stumble upon a decent-looking answer in that specific moment?

But the applause was real. The fact that the team adopted the plan is real.

I think of my father’s saying: "Live life by the book." That phrase has stayed with me for so many years. It kept me stable, made me reliable, and ensured I never caused trouble for anyone. But this afternoon, for the first time, it felt like I deliberately made a crack in that framework. Not breaking it, just... opening it a little.

I don’t know if this is good or bad.

Maybe it’s both.

The project moves forward tomorrow. I’ll have to see if this compromise is actually feasible in practice. I know I’ll still be cautiously monitoring every step, but maybe... maybe this time, while sticking to my principles, I can try to trust in something I never dared to trust before.

I’m a bit tired. But also... I don’t know how to describe it. It’s not excitement; it’s something quieter than that. Like a form of confirmation.

Anyway, time for bed. There’s a mountain of work waiting tomorrow.

Xiaolin, I’ve been sitting in the office restroom for half an hour.

I’m okay, I just need a moment.

I signed the leave request—one month. My supervisor didn’t ask any questions; he just approved it immediately. I told him my mother passed away, and he offered his condolences. I don’t remember how I responded.

I don’t think I’ve cried.

It’s strange. I’m wondering if something inside me is broken. People are supposed to cry at a time like this, right? I just feel cold. Even in this heat.

I found a diary in her room. I don’t know when she started writing it. It’s filled with "so tired." Over and over again. I read about ten pages and put it down. I didn’t want to keep reading.

I’m sorry, I probably can’t be there for you right now. I know you’re hurting too. I can tell. But right now... I don’t know how to put it. I just can’t.

Xinyu asked me why Grandma didn’t come to pick her up from school. I told her Grandma is sick. She asked when she’ll get better. I didn’t answer. I just told her to do her homework first.

I don’t know how to tell her.

I keep wondering if I should have spent more time with my mother that day, or if I missed some kind of signal. The doctor said it was sudden. But I was tracking her blood sugar levels, all the time. I thought that was enough.

I need a rest. Truly.

Don’t wait up for me for dinner. I might be very late. Or I might just sleep at the office for a while.

I’m so sorry.

Xiaolin, there’s something I want to tell you.

I took the initiative to talk to my boss this morning. I told him that I’ve been dealing with some personal issues lately and that my performance might have slipped a bit. I was fully prepared to be reprimanded; I even stood outside his office for ten minutes before I worked up the courage to knock.

But his reaction wasn't at all what I expected. He said he’s always seen me as a reliable person and that everyone goes through difficult times. He even mentioned that if I need it, we could be more flexible with my workload to give me some breathing room.

I was a bit stunned while listening to him. I’ve always thought that in the workplace, you have to be perfect and hide all your problems. I even worried that if my performance continued to dip, I might be sidelined. But he didn't do that at all.

I don’t know if this will really change anything. Maybe the pressure will be back tomorrow. But at least for now, I don’t have to keep toughing it out on my own, and I don’t have to keep pretending to you that "it’s nothing, I’m just a bit tired from work."

You told me before that I needed to reach out for help. I think maybe this is the start.

I’ll be home early tonight. Let’s help Xinyu with her homework together.

Xiaolin, I’m downstairs picking up Xinyu right now.

Something went sideways at the meeting today. Another project change request came in, and I didn't manage the client's expectations very well. I feel a bit... like I couldn't keep up.

But it’s fine, just work stuff.

How was your day?

---

Actually, I was thinking...

(A long pause)

I might need to rethink some things. It’s not that things are bad right now, it’s just... I feel a bit stuck. Have you noticed anything off about me lately?

I mean, aside from just being tired from work.

---

Never mind, let’s talk when I get back. I’ll go get Xinyu first.

September 15, 2023, 9:00 PM

Tonight, after I finished reading Xinyu her bedtime story, I didn’t turn off the lights immediately like I usually do. I sat by her bed, watching her sleeping face, and was suddenly overcome by a strange sensation—not a sense of heaviness, but... I can’t quite put it into words.

After going downstairs, I didn’t turn on the TV or check my phone. I went into the study and pulled my father’s diary from the very back of the bookshelf. I’ve been flipping through it these past few days, but tonight felt different.

I found the page. In his usual neat handwriting, my father had written: "Our greatest happiness is seeing him live a stable and fulfilling life."

I’ve read this sentence many times before, but tonight... the tears just started falling. Silently, drop by drop.

I realized I’ve been looking for the wrong thing all along. All these years, I’ve been wondering where I failed, why I didn’t make a name for myself, why I couldn’t give my family a better life. I kept asking myself: Am I a failure? Am I a burden?

But my father never asked those questions. He was simply recording—every choice I made, every moment of stability. He found contentment in those things.

I picked up a pen and wrote in the margin of the diary: "My father’s expectations were simple. I have been meeting them all along."

My hand was trembling as I finished the sentence.

It wasn't out of despair. It was because... I suddenly understood that I’m not wasting my life. What I’m doing matters to someone. It mattered to my father. It matters to Li Xiaolin. And it matters to Xinyu.

I don’t know how long this feeling will last. But at least for tonight, I no longer feel like my existence is a mistake.

At least for tonight, I can breathe.

# October 12, 2023 | Sunny

6:45 AM. Just got back from my run.

I’m sitting in the study after my shower, still a bit out of breath. I ran a little fast today—probably because my mind kept racing over that project.

I used to just zone out during my runs. Five kilometers, forty-odd minutes, thinking about absolutely nothing. For the past three years, this has been my most cherished time—no worrying about my daughter’s grades, no staring at financial spreadsheets, no speaking up in meetings. Just running, breathing, and the sound of my footsteps.

But today was different.

I was thinking about the AI solution for user experience optimization. Thinking about how to pitch it to the team, how to convince my superiors that this isn't just some pipe dream. When I reached the riverside, I even stopped to jot down a few notes on my phone. That has never happened before.

My run turned into work hours.

I wonder: is this a good thing or a bad thing?

Then I realized—I wasn't escaping work; I was... genuinely interested in this. Not the "I have to do this well" kind of interest, but the "I want to see if I can make this happen" kind. The feeling is a bit foreign. A bit exciting. And a little bit scary.

When I got home, I originally planned to check the financial bills first—I need to review the progress on the mortgage and the education fund over the weekend. That’s my routine, set in stone. But Xinyu was already awake, and she asked me from the kitchen if we could make breakfast together.

In the past, I would have said, "Daddy needs to handle some work stuff first, I'll be there in a second."

Today, I put my phone down immediately and boiled eggs with her.

She stood on a stool while I watched her carefully lower the eggs into the pot. She asked me why eggs turn hard. I explained protein denaturation to her—in a way a child could understand, of course. She listened intently and followed up with three more "whys."

When Li Xiaolin came out of the bedroom and saw us together, she gave me a nod. Just a simple nod. She didn't say anything, but I knew she was saying, "Good. This is good."

In that moment, I felt permitted. Permitted to be imperfect, permitted to change plans, permitted to treat breakfast time as being just as important as financial data.

 the data is still there. It’s not going anywhere. But Xinyu’s childhood only happens once.

I’m sitting here now, still a bit uncertain. Uncertain if this career pivot is the right move, uncertain if I can truly pull off an innovation project, uncertain if I’ve actually changed or if this is just a side effect of a few months of therapy.

But.

I thought about work while running, I spent breakfast with my daughter, and I didn't feel guilty for breaking my routine.

Maybe this is the beginning.

Maybe I’m finally starting to believe that I can be different.

Maybe the identity of "the reliable project manager" can no longer contain who I am now.

I’m still walking; I haven't arrived yet. But at least, I’m moving.

# Initial Project Reflections - AI-Driven E-commerce UX Optimization

**Date**: 2023-10-12  
**Status**: Preliminary Ideas | Pending Validation  
**Priority**: High  

---

## Project Background Review

It’s been two weeks since I transferred to the Innovation Projects Department. To be honest, I’m still in the adjustment phase. But the direction of this AI project is genuinely compelling—optimizing user recommendation paths on e-commerce platforms using machine learning. This isn't just stacking technologies for the sake of it; there are real user pain points behind it.

I went over the whole logic again during my run this morning and felt the need to get it down on paper before the thoughts scattered.

---

## Preliminary Technical Direction (Requires Team Validation)

**Core Hypotheses**:
- Current recommendation algorithms rely too heavily on historical behavior and fail to capture "intent drift."
- User needs vary significantly across different time slots and scenarios, yet our system isn't fully leveraging this information.

**Initial Solution Ideas**:
1. Incorporate time-series features + user session context.
2. Implement a lightweight intent recognition model (no need for complex NLP; can be inferred from interaction behavior).
3. Use a canary release strategy with A/B testing to verify effectiveness.

**Pending Questions**:
- What is the data quality like? Is the labeling of historical data accurate enough?
- How high are the real-time requirements? This will dictate the model selection.
- Does anyone on the team currently have experience with the engineering implementation of this?

---

## Plan for Next Week

I’ll be attending the Design Thinking Workshop and want to run these ideas by everyone there. I know my thinking tends to be quite linear and I can easily get bogged down in details, so I need to hear other perspectives. Specifically, I want to understand:

- What is the Product side’s take on user pain points? Are my current hypotheses drifting away from actual needs?
- Are there similar cases or past failures we can learn from?
- Technical complexity assessment—is my current proposal over-engineered?

---

## Personal Reflections

These past two weeks since the transfer, I’ve felt like I’m tackling something completely foreign. In my previous project management roles, I could control every variable; now, this project is full of uncertainties. It’s a bit anxiety-inducing at times, but there’s also an indescribable sense of excitement.

It feels a bit like the first time I ran more than 5 kilometers—my body was resisting, but at the same time, I wanted to see how far I could go.

I need to find a new rhythm in this process. I can’t chase 100% controllability like I did when managing the e-commerce platform, but I can’t just let things drift either. Perhaps the key is working in this state of being "planned yet flexible."

---

**Next Steps**: Organize the data source list; prepare the presentation draft for the workshop by Thursday (keep it informal, just focus on getting the ideas across clearly).

Xiaolin, there was something I wanted to share with you.

While I was with Xinyu during her drawing session today, she suddenly asked if I wanted to join her. My initial thought was to teach her about composition, color usage, and things like that, but the moment I sat down, I stopped myself. I realized I had been so focused on how to "teach" her, when in fact, she didn't need that at all.

As she drew, she kept talking, telling me stories from her dreams—about castles and flying deer. I put down my brush and just listened. I asked her what her dreams were like and what color the deer was. She was so happy while she talked; her eyes were literally glowing.

When she finished, she looked at me and said, "Daddy, thank you for being with me." I told her, "Thank you for letting me be part of your world." It was only after saying that out loud that I understood what had been on my mind lately.

I’ve been so worried about whether her foundation is solid enough, checking her grades, and thinking about how to help her do better. But the most important thing, really, is that she’s willing to let me in—into her dreams and her imagination. That’s what companionship actually is.

I used to feel like I had to be perfect and plan everything out. Now I realize that "perfect" companionship doesn't exist. What exists is simply being together in that moment.

I feel like I’ve had a bit of a breakthrough these past few days. Not everything can be planned or controlled. Some of the most precious things only appear when you let go of that control.

Xiaolin, are you asleep?

I wanted to tell you something. It’s not about work, so don't worry.

Just now, while I was with my dad at the hospital, a thought suddenly came to me—I want to write something for him. A memoir of his life, starting from when he was young. All those stories he’s told, everything he’s been through. I want to get it all down.

I know this sounds a bit sudden. But these past two days at the hospital, looking at my mom and dad, I realized that some things we assume will always be there, actually...

I’m not trying to control or plan anything. I just want to protect something. To protect their memories.

Thinking about it this way makes me feel different. It’s not that feeling of being overwhelmed by a schedule, but... it’s a bit warm. Like I’ve finally found something worth doing.

You said before that I needed to think things through, that I needed to adjust. Maybe this is the start.

No need to reply, get some rest. I should head to sleep too.

It’s the weekend tomorrow. I think I’ll start with my dad’s stories.

February 20, 2023, 9:00 PM

Xinyu is already asleep. I’m sitting in the study; outside the window is the Hangzhou winter night, a bit cold.

I talked with Xiaolin for a long time tonight. A really long time. We’ve been talking since we finished dinner.

I don’t know how to start talking about this. Maybe it’s just that… I finally stopped saying, "It’s nothing, just a bit tired from work." I don’t know when I started learning to use that phrase to shut down every conversation. It’s like a button—press it, and all the problems disappear. But in reality, nothing disappears.

Xiaolin asked me, "Are you always afraid of something?"

I didn’t answer right away. I sat on the sofa, looking at her, thinking for a long time. Then I said it—"I’ve been making decisions for the sake of security. But I’ve never asked myself if this kind of security actually makes us happy."

The moment I said those words, it felt like I was admitting a truth I had known for a long time.

We started discussing some "what-ifs." What if we don’t buy the second apartment? What if I stay at the company not because I love it, but just because I’m afraid of change? What if my focus on Xinyu is actually just a way to deflect my own anxiety?

These questions hurt. But they are necessary.

Xiaolin didn’t try to comfort me or say things like "you’re doing great." She just sat beside me and asked, "So, what now? What do you want?"

I don’t know. That’s the most honest answer. I really don’t know.

But at least… at least I’m not alone in the dark anymore.

It’s a strange feeling. I always thought I had to have all the answers, that I had to be the one making the decisions, the one taking care of everyone. But maybe… maybe I don’t have to be that person.

I’m still scared. Scared of losing control, scared of failure, scared of change. But now, this fear seems to have an outlet. It hasn’t disappeared, but… someone is willing to face it with me.

I don’t know what happens next. Maybe this is just the beginning. Maybe nothing will change. But at least tonight, I spoke the truth.

That’s enough. For tonight, at least, that’s enough.
