August 7, 2022  2:17 AM

I can’t sleep. I’ve been lying here for three hours.

I just got up and turned on my laptop. For some reason, I opened Excel. I originally meant to check my daughter’s education fund, but my fingers took on a life of their own and created a new spreadsheet. The title: "Risk Assessment."

I spent twenty minutes listing every fixed expense. The mortgage, insurance, my parents' medical bills, my daughter’s tuition... then I calculated our liquid assets. I ended up with a single number: 14 months.

If I lose my job, we can hold out for 14 months.

14 months. I keep staring at that number like it’s a countdown.

Why am I doing this calculation at 2:00 AM? I ask myself. My hands are shaking slightly.

Maybe it was that meeting this afternoon. I messed up during the project review—a bug that should have been caught two weeks ago, and I somehow missed it. My boss asked me what happened in front of everyone. I said, "I’ll fix it immediately," but I saw the disappointment in his eyes. It’s been eight years, and I’ve never dropped the ball on something like this.

Or maybe it was last night when I was helping Xinyu with her math. She got the same word problem wrong three times, and I finally snapped, "Why still don't you get it?" Her eyes welled up. Li Xiaolin heard it from the kitchen; I could feel her silence.

Or maybe it was taking Mom to the hospital last week. The doctor said her blood sugar still isn't under control and we need to adjust her medication. I took a full page of notes, but I know I didn't really understand the medical jargon. I was just pretending everything was under control.

Now I’m sitting by the window, looking out at the lights of Hangzhou. I’m thinking about a very strange question—what is my value?

Not my professional value. I mean... if one day I lose my job, if one day I can no longer provide a steady income, if one day I’m no longer that "reliable project manager," then what am I? What can I give my daughter? What can I give my parents? What can I give Li Xiaolin?

I always thought I was just "living life by the book"—that’s what my father taught me. Do your job well, don't be a burden to others. But now I realize my entire identity is built on two things: "not making mistakes" and "being a provider." What happens if those two things crumble?

When I messed up in the meeting today, I felt true fear for the first time. Not fear of the job, but fear of myself.

I added a row to the Excel sheet and calculated what would happen if my salary dropped by 20%. Then 30%. Finally, I shut down the computer because I realized what I was doing—I was preparing for my own failure.

My hands are still shaking.

Maybe I should go for a run tomorrow. But I’m not sure if running helps me clear my head anymore. Lately, when I run, my mind only races faster.

I should go to sleep. But I know I’ll stay awake.

December 10, 2022  23:47

I’ve been lying here for nearly two hours. My eyes are wide open, watching the shadows flicker across the ceiling. My heart is racing a bit, and my breathing is uneven. Xiaolin is fast asleep; I can hear her steady, rhythmic breathing. I don’t want to wake her.

Mom’s blood sugar was 7.8 today. That number has been looping in my head all day. According to the research I’ve done, if this trend continues, it could exceed 8.5 in three months. If it goes over 8.5, the risks during her cardiac function test will increase. If there’s an issue with the cardiac test, then…

I stopped myself. There’s no point in thinking like this.

But I still opened the Excel sheet on my phone. *Mother’s Health Records.* I started this file over a month ago, logging every single physical exam result. I even calculated a trend line. If the slope stays like this… no, I shouldn’t keep calculating. It only makes me more anxious.

But if I don’t calculate it, what am I supposed to do?

Xiaolin asked me at work today if I’ve been under too much pressure lately. I said no, just a bit tired from work. That wasn't entirely a lie. Work *is* exhausting—I was late once on that project’s timeline, and now I’m playing catch-up. But that’s not the real reason I can’t sleep.

The real reason is… I don’t know how to put it.

I can’t do everything at once. This is something I’ve only recently truly realized. I used to think that as long as I planned in enough detail and allocated my time rationally, I could keep everything in order. Project management, family finances, my daughter’s education, my parents’ health… everything had its place, its deadline. I noted them in my journal and tracked progress in Excel. That method always worked.

But it’s different now.

Mom’s blood sugar doesn’t follow my plans. She says she doesn’t need such frequent checks, that I’m being too tense. Dad says the same; they both think I’m overthinking it. But if I don’t think about it, if I don’t record it, if I don’t track it—what do I do when the worst-case scenario happens? How could I ever forgive myself?

I’ve even wondered what I’d do if Mom’s condition worsened. Who would manage the project? Who would look after our daughter? Xiaolin is busy with her own work. I can’t let her take on any more. I have to be the one. But how can I carry it all at once?

There is no answer to this question.

I know I’m over-planning. I can feel myself doing it. But I can’t stop. I need this sense of control. Without it, I feel nothing but fear.

At 1:45 AM, I got up and looked over Mom’s lab reports from last week again. I know it’s pointless. The reports aren't going to change. But I looked anyway. I keep wondering if I missed some crucial detail.

My heart is still racing.

Maybe I should go for a run. But it’s the middle of the night; I can’t go out. Maybe I should do something productive instead of lying here spiraling. But I don’t want to wake Xiaolin.

So, I’m writing this.

I don’t know if this changes anything. But at least by writing these thoughts down, they stop looping infinitely in my head. Maybe tomorrow morning I’ll look at these words and realize I was overcomplicating things. Maybe I’ll remember that I’ve always been reliable. I’ve always been able to handle things.

But right now, in this moment, I’m not so sure.

Yeah.

I'm fine, just didn't sleep well.

Go ahead and eat first, I'll eat later.

Xiaolin, I just stepped out of the conference room.

Sitting here in my office, it still feels a bit surreal.

At the management meeting this morning, I formally proposed that "Caregiver Support Program." You know how long I’ve been preparing for this—actually, it’s only been two weeks, but it feels like a lifetime.

I remember saying, "Our competitiveness isn't measured by overtime hours, but by mental health and life satisfaction." There was a moment of silence in the room after I said it. For a second, I thought I’d said something wrong.

But then, the department manager spoke up and said we should move forward with the idea. This afternoon, several colleagues even came up to me to say they’d been thinking about the same thing. One veteran employee, who usually keeps to himself, even asked if I could help him apply for flexible hours because he needs to look after his sick mother.

I realized then that this wasn't just my problem.

Do you remember? Last month, when you asked why I was always preoccupied with work, I just said, "It’s nothing, I’m just a bit tired." In reality, I was already thinking about all of this, but I couldn't explain it to you. I hadn't even figured it out myself.

It was you being there for me that allowed me to see it clearly—that I wasn't running away, but searching for a more authentic way to live. You didn't rush me or tell me what I "should" do; you were just there, thinking with me, trying things out with me.

My colleagues' reactions today made me feel understood. But more importantly, you understood all along. You understood why I had to do this and what it meant to me.

I’m certain now that this is the right direction. Not because the meeting was a success, but because I finally stopped feeling like "stability" and "change" are at odds. I can remain the reliable person I’ve always been while doing something meaningful and different. This isn't a risk; it’s what I’m supposed to do.

Let’s talk more over dinner tonight? I want to tell you the whole story.

And, thank you.

# November 21, 2023 Morning Notes

6:30 AM, by the West Lake. The air is freezing. I stopped running at the third kilometer.

It wasn't that I was tired; I just suddenly felt like stopping. I stood there for a long time, staring at the water.

One question kept circling in my head: Have I really changed? Or have I just found a different way to do the same old things?

It’s a nagging question. Not the kind of anxiety that keeps you up at night, but... how should I put it? It’s like a splinter. It doesn't exactly hurt, but you know it's there.

What has happened over the past three months? I took on the AI project, started managing the team with more flexibility, and suggested keeping weekends unplanned. These all sound like good things. But this morning I’m wondering—are these changes real? Or have I just taken my old logic of "control" and given it a new skin called "flexibility"?

For example, I no longer set detailed study plans for my daughter, but have I just swapped one form of anxiety for another? I’ve moved from "checking grades" to "observing interests," but at its core, am I still just trying to ensure everything stays within the bounds of my understanding?

Or... am I truly responding to my own authentic needs, rather than running away from something?

The thought makes me a little tense. Not a fearful tension, though. It’s more like—the feeling of needing to tidy something up.

This afternoon, I need to organize my notes and prepare for the company presentation. I originally thought this was just a professional task, but now I realize this speech might mean something else to me. It’s an opportunity to understand what has actually happened to me over these last three months.

It’s not about explaining it to others. It’s about seeing it clearly for myself.

Maybe the authenticity of change doesn't lie in what I do, but in why I do it.

I need to figure this out.

Back to the run. Two kilometers to go.

December 21, 2022, 11:45 PM

Li Xiaolin is already asleep. I can hear her steady breathing coming from the next room. She’s angry. No, disappointed would be more accurate.

I don’t know when I started becoming this person—the kind who responds to every gesture of care with a "nothing’s wrong." I’m like a wall. When she asked me what I was running from, I just turned toward the wall and said coldly, "I told you, I’m fine." The moment I saw her face fall, I knew exactly what I’d done wrong. But I just couldn't bring myself to open up.

Why?

Maybe it’s because I truly don’t know how to explain it. How can I tell her that I, the "perfect decision-maker," actually have no control over anything? My parents won't listen to my advice, my daughter’s grades still haven't improved, and that aggressive proposal at work didn't get the reaction I expected. The logic I spent twenty years building—data, planning, rational analysis—it turns out it’s all failing me.

I’ve been dealing with insomnia for over a month now. Sometimes I lie in bed, the same questions looping in my head: Was I too conservative? If I hadn't been so cautious, so by-the-book, would things be different now? But then I think, if I give all that up, what do I have left?

I’ve even started to wonder if I truly love this family. It’s not that I don’t, but rather that I only seem to know how to express love through "management"—checking the bills, logging my mother’s blood sugar, reviewing my daughter’s homework. When these things stop working, I’m like a machine with its parts stripped away, no longer knowing how to function.

My wife tries to get close to me, and I push her away. This is a first. In the past, whenever we had disagreements, I could always resolve them with reason. But I have no reason left now. Or rather, my rationality has turned into a defense mechanism—using indifference to protect myself from further failure.

I hear her tossing and turning in the next room. She isn't sleeping well either.

I have work tomorrow. I need to check my daughter’s math one more time. My mother’s check-up for next week hasn't been confirmed yet. There’s so much to do, yet I feel myself slowly sinking.

I shouldn't have spoken to her like that. But right now, I have no idea how to make it right.

New Year's Eve. I didn't make a bucket list this time; instead, I feel a sense of relief.

Watching the fireworks out the window with my daughter, she asked, "Daddy, do you have any goals for the new year?" I told her, "Let’s just go and experience the new year together." She didn't seem to quite get it and went back to pointing at the sky.

This past year has been a journey. Moving from initial anxiety to where I am now, I’ve slowly come to realize one thing: being real is more precious than being perfect. Not everything needs to be under control; sometimes, simply being there is enough.

While walking with my father, I brought this up. He said, "You’re finally not so tense anymore." I smiled. Perhaps those who are stable need to learn to embrace change, and those who are constantly changing need to find an anchor. The two aren't mutually exclusive.

The new year doesn't need grand plans. It just needs us to show up for the important moments, listen to family stories, and allow life to have its surprises.

2024, let’s do this.

# Diary

**Thursday Night, January 12, 2023**

I just finished reading Xinyu a bedtime story, and she’s already fast asleep. I’ve been sitting here by her bed, watching her for a long time.

At the parent-teacher meeting this afternoon, the teacher mentioned that Xinyu’s fundamentals still need strengthening. Right then and there, I decided—I’m going to sign her up for tutoring classes. I’ve been mulling over this decision ever since, but the more I think about it, the more it feels wrong.

The teacher’s parting words were: "Learning happily is more important than anything else." I listened and nodded at the time. But the moment I stepped out of the school, I brushed it aside. I told myself that was just the teacher’s "idealistic" view; in a world this competitive, how could "happiness" alone be enough?

But now, sitting by my daughter’s bed and looking at her sleeping face, I suddenly find myself asking—what am I doing?

I’ve already drafted a schedule for the tutoring. Math, reading comprehension, English. I’ve even calculated the costs and time commitments in my head. But is all of this really for Xinyu?

To be honest, I don’t know.

I’m wondering if this is about my mother. All those things I can’t control. She won't listen to my advice, she refuses to follow her treatment properly, and all those perfect health management plans I drew up are useless. I feel a sense of failure—as if, as a son and the head of the household, I can’t even protect my own mother.

Then Xinyu’s teacher says she "needs more practice," and I immediately think—this, I can control. This, I can do right. I can get her the best tutors, create the most detailed study plan, and ensure her grades never falter.

I am using my daughter’s education to compensate for my failures with my mother.

I’m only realizing this now, as I’m writing this down.

The teacher spoke of "learning happily," yet here I am planning how to pile pressure onto a six-year-old. This isn't the kind of father I want to be. I’ve never been that person. And yet, that’s exactly what I’m doing.

Why am I so obsessed? Why can’t I be like everyone else—listen to the teacher’s advice and just let it go?

Maybe it’s because I feel like I’ve lost control. There’s no hope for a promotion at work, my parents won’t listen to me at home, and now I’m not even sure if I’m doing the right thing for my daughter’s education. I feel like I need to prove, somewhere, that I’m still capable.

But it’s not fair to Xinyu.

I need to talk to the teacher again tomorrow. Maybe I should actually listen to her. Maybe I should just let my daughter learn happily, instead of crushing her under the weight of my own anxiety.

But I still don't know what to do.

I need to sleep. I haven't been sleeping well lately, and my head is a mess.

# Work Log - 2023.11.23

While organizing my thoughts for the speech tonight, a conversation I had with Xiaolin today came to mind. She asked me why I’ve changed so much over the past three months. Without thinking much, I just blurted it out—it’s actually a response to what you all need. You need a more authentic Zhou Yu, not a perfect one.

After saying it, I caught myself off guard.

That sentence seems to explain a lot. It’s not just about why I’ve started experimenting with more flexible management styles on projects; it also explains why my communication with the team feels different now.

Before, I was always trying to maintain this "reliable" image—everything had to be planned out, every risk anticipated, and every problem solved before it even surfaced. The upside was that projects rarely ran late, and my colleagues found me dependable. But at what cost? It meant that in front of the team, I was always the person with the answers, rather than a real person.

The changes I’ve made this month on the AI project are actually an attempt to break that habit. When I started admitting, "I’m not entirely sure about this direction," the conversations with the team actually became deeper. They started truly thinking, rather than just executing. I also started seeing their genuine thoughts, instead of just the answers they thought I wanted to hear.

It’s the same thing that happened at home. My approach to my daughter’s education used to follow this same logic—perfect plans, strict execution, and preemptively preventing every possible problem. The result? She became very cautious around me, terrified of making a mistake. Now that I’ve started to loosen up, I’m finally seeing who she really is—she’s actually very imaginative, it was just suppressed by my "expectations of perfection."

I think this might be why work feels different lately. It’s not because the projects themselves have changed, but because my understanding of "what makes a good leader" has shifted.

A good leader isn't a perfect executor. It’s someone who can still think sincerely alongside their team when facing uncertainty. It’s being able to say "I don't know" instead of pretending to know. It’s seeing team members for who they really are, rather than the roles they are performing.

I’ll continue working on the speech tomorrow. I want to weave this idea into it—not as some management theory, but simply as a genuine observation from these past three months.

Xiaolin, are you asleep?

I had some thoughts before heading to bed tonight. Lately, my sleep has improved quite a bit—I’m getting five or six hours now. You might not have noticed, but I can feel the difference.

My running has also returned to a normal rhythm, unlike before when I was... forcing myself. While I was out today, it suddenly hit me that maybe I’ve been looking for answers in all the wrong places.

I used to feel like I had to keep everything under control, to have every plan be flawless, just to protect you all. But lately, I’ve realized that the tighter I grip, the easier it is for things to fall apart.

Before bed, I told myself something—maybe tomorrow will be better. Not because everything will go according to plan, but because I’ve learned how to truly live.

I know I haven’t been very good to you lately, always pushing your care away. I think you should know that I’m feeling a change now.

Thank you for always being there.

# February 28, 11:30 PM

I got six hours of sleep yesterday. Same today.

The number seems small, but I find myself silently counting—5 hours, 5.5 hours, 6 hours. It feels like tracking a slow recovery. Two months ago, I could only manage two hours a night, lying in bed staring at the ceiling, thinking I’d lose my mind if it kept up. Now, at least I can sleep until an hour or two before dawn.

I still got up at 6:30 this morning and ran 5 kilometers. I didn't push myself until my legs gave out like before, and it didn't feel compulsive—I just wanted to move; my body needed it. While running, I wasn't thinking about work or my daughter’s grades; I was just running. The wind was cold, but it felt good.

Tonight, while reading to Xinyu, she asked, "Daddy, what's been going on with you lately?" For a moment, I didn't know how to answer. She said, "You used to frown all the time." I stroked her hair and told her it was nothing, that Daddy was just a little tired. It wasn't a lie, but it wasn't the whole truth either.

Li Xiaolin was in the kitchen; I could hear her washing the dishes. She hasn't asked me "Are you okay?" lately, which makes me feel a bit guilty. But maybe it’s better this way—I don’t have to keep repeating the phrase, "I'm fine, just a bit tired from work."

Lying in bed, a thought suddenly came to me. I don't know where it came from. I said to myself: "Maybe tomorrow will be better. Not because everything will go according to plan, but because I’ve learned how to actually live."

I almost laughed after saying it. It doesn't sound like something I would say. And yet, it’s exactly what I’m thinking right now.

I used to always be planning, controlling, making sure nothing went wrong. Now I realize that’s not how life works at all. My mother’s blood sugar will still spike, my daughter’s grades won’t improve just because I check her work one more time, and I won’t solve my problems at work just by running myself to the point of exhaustion.

These facts used to terrify me. Now... I don't know how to describe it. Acceptance, maybe?

I have work tomorrow. There are still problems waiting for me on the project. But I’m not that afraid anymore.

My sleep is improving. That much is real.

Maybe things really will get better.

I’m starting to wonder if I’m the one doing something wrong.

The tears started falling instantly.

I’ve never spoken to her like that before. I know it. She knows it too.

I apologized immediately and held her, but the guilt is relentless. I told her I wasn't angry with her, that Mommy was just a little tired. She nodded, but I could see the lingering fear in her eyes. I stayed with her until she fell asleep before coming out here.

No.

I know it’s not.

I’ve been preoccupied with work for the past few days. The results of the annual review were the same as always: "Reliable but not outstanding." Eight years. The mortgage, the education fund, my parents' medical bills... the numbers spin around in my head, and they always lead to the same conclusion: I can’t move. I’m trapped.

And somehow, I projected this sense of powerlessness onto a six-year-old child.

I stared at myself in the mirror for a long time. The person in the reflection looks the same—stable, calm, organized. But I know that inside, things are starting to crack. I can’t find an exit in my career, and I’m starting to lose control at home. This isn't right. This is all wrong.

But I knew that in that moment, I had failed her.

It’s been three years. Twenty minutes every night—that was my promise to her. It wasn't about teaching her anything; it was just so that time belonged to the two of us. Now, I can’t even manage that.

Li Xiaolin asked me what was going wrong at work. I could hear the worry in her voice, but I couldn't say it. "Nothing, just a bit tired from work." Even as I said it, I felt like a hypocrite. She didn't push further, but I could sense her disappointment. She was waiting for me to open a door, and I chose to shut it. It feels like there’s a layer of something between us now.

My supervisor’s words keep spinning in my head: "Management positions are limited." I understand it’s a statement of fact, but it broke something. For eight years, I’ve played by the rules, made no major mistakes, delivered projects on time, and my colleagues call me "reliable." Reliable. That word sounds like a euphemism for "mediocre" now. I’m starting to wonder: did I head in the wrong direction from the very beginning? Or did I have no direction at all, just moving forward out of sheer inertia?

I’ve looked over my mother’s recent check-up report. Her blood sugar levels are fluctuating. It’s not a major issue, but being a rational person, I’ve already started mapping out the chain of potential risks in my head. If her blood sugar isn't controlled, what will it affect? What preparations do I need to make? Meanwhile, the teacher says Xinyu needs more practice with her foundational math. I want to spend more time practicing with her, but I don't get home until after 7:00 PM every day.

I use Excel spreadsheets to manage family finances, plan education funds, and calculate mortgage pressure. All the numbers are clear; every item has a corresponding goal and timeline. But now I’m realizing there are things a spreadsheet can’t manage. For instance, how am I supposed to be a good son, father, husband, and employee all at once? When the demands of these identities conflict, which one should I prioritize?

I don't know.

Perhaps that is my most honest state right now—not knowing. Not knowing which direction my career should take, not knowing how to allocate my time, and not knowing why a person who follows the rules is suddenly feeling this sense of panic.

The study is very quiet. From downstairs, I can hear the sound of Xiaolin clearing the dishes; Xinyu should be asleep by now. I should go down and help, or at least sit with her for a while. But I sit here, drawing the same lines over and over again on the paper with my pen.

Am I avoiding something? Or do I simply have no idea how to face it?

# Diary

November 22, 2023 | Night

Just got back from the office. My daughter is already asleep. Sitting here in the study, I still can’t quite settle down.

That sharing session this afternoon... to be honest, I’m still processing it. Speaking in front of so many people is never comfortable for me. But what was even more unsettling was realizing that as I spoke, it felt like I was having a conversation with myself.

"True stability isn't about avoiding all risks; it’s about choosing which risks to take after clarifying your priorities."

I revised that sentence so many times before settling on it. The initial version was more absolute, but then I felt it was too dogmatic. Yet, it wasn't until I said it out loud that I truly understood what it meant for me. I wasn't talking about project management principles; I was talking about... myself.

When I shared that story—about the night I missed reading with my daughter just to perfect a project plan—I could feel the room go quiet. It wasn't the effect I was aiming for, but it happened. My voice cracked a little at that point. I didn't expect that.

Afterward, a colleague came up to me and said, "What you said made me rethink the way I work." I didn't know how to respond. It felt like something had been seen. It wasn't the feeling of being seen itself that made me uncomfortable, but rather... I realized just how long I’ve been used to being labeled as "reliable but uninspired." That label has been stuck on me for so long that I’d almost forgotten that being "inspired" might just mean having the courage to speak your truth.

I mentioned it to Xiaolin tonight. She said, "You look different." I asked her what she meant, and she couldn't quite put it into words—just that it felt like I was slowly becoming someone else. I said, maybe so. I am changing.

But I’m still a little scared. This new sense of identity is still fragile. What if I mess up? What if this AI project fails in the end? What if everyone realizes I’m not actually that insightful?

...But then again, I don't seem to believe in these "what-ifs" as much anymore. Or rather, I’m starting to believe that some "what-ifs" are worth the risk.

Onward to tomorrow.

Zhou Yu

Xiaolin, I felt like talking to you today.

I was sitting in the office for a while just now, and my mind was a bit of a mess. I started thinking back to last weekend.

Do you remember? We didn't end up going to that children's park as planned; instead, we just stayed home and watched butterflies with Xinyu. At first, I actually felt a bit uneasy—like I was wasting the weekend and not "making the most of my time." But later, out on the balcony, I realized something strange: I’ve been rushing through everything, even rushing through being present.

It’s like when I’m reading a storybook to Xinyu, my mind is actually still on tomorrow’s project, my mother’s check-up results, and the family budget. I thought that was being "efficient," but in reality, I was just constantly running away.

That day, while watching the butterflies, I didn't check my phone. When Xinyu asked me why the butterfly stopped on the flower, I actually stopped to really think about it. We just watched for a long time, doing nothing at all. That was the first time I was truly there for her.

Do you remember what Dad said to me later? "You finally understand." It didn't click at the time, but I get it now. He’s probably been waiting for me to let go of that "step-by-step, perfect execution" mindset. He’s the one who taught it to me, but maybe he realized later that it ended up trapping me.

I want to say thank you. You were the one who kept pushing me to see a therapist, even though I was a bit resistant at first. But it’s because of those sessions that I’m slowly starting to see myself clearly. I’ve realized that I don’t need to plan everything perfectly—and I can’t, anyway.

I still get anxious, and I still have that urge to control everything. But at least I’m starting to understand that what really matters isn’t how much I get done, but whether I’m actually *there*.

This weekend, I want to try again. No plans—just playing with Xinyu. And being with you.

What do you think?

February 1, 2023, 9:30 PM

Lying in bed. My daughter is already asleep. Li Xiaolin is in the living room watching TV; I can hear the sound of it, sounding so far, far away.

I don’t know how to write this. I keep picking up my phone and putting it back down.

Just now, in the kitchen, she made a list. I didn’t argue. Compulsive running, excessive searching for medical information, over-analyzing Xinyu’s grades… she listed them one by one, and I just listened. I wanted to say "no," but I couldn't get the word out. Because it’s all true.

She said, "I won't just stand by and watch you continue like this."

In that moment, I turned my face toward the wall.

It’s been three months. It started with difficulty falling asleep, and now I’m lying here until two or three in the morning every night. I thought it was just work stress, or maybe just getting older. I even thought that maybe I needed to exercise harder, or maybe I needed to control my diet more strictly. I looked up so much information. Insomnia, palpitations, dizziness… I checked every single box.

But this isn't an insomnia problem.

I know. I’ve always known.

She said I should see a therapist. My first instinct was to refuse. But I didn't have the strength to refuse. I didn't even have the strength to defend myself. So I just turned toward the wall, like a child.

I’m scared. Scared that a doctor will tell me I’ve had a mental breakdown. Scared that it means I’ve lost control of myself. Scared that it means I’ve failed—as a father, a son, a husband, a man.

I always thought I was stable. My life was clear—university, work, marriage, buying a house, raising a daughter, taking care of my parents. Every step was planned. I never worked late into the night, I never let a project miss a deadline, I ran three or four times a week, I logged my mother’s blood sugar data, I read storybooks to Xinyu…

I did everything I was "supposed to do."

But now I find I can’t control my sleep. I can’t control my anxiety. I can’t stop myself from checking medical info over and over, from asking the teacher about Xinyu’s grades over and over, from doing everything… over and over.

I can’t even control my own breathing.

Li Xiaolin said she’s noticed my insomnia for three months now. I didn’t realize it had been that long. Or rather, I realized it, but I was in denial. I was telling myself, "It’s nothing, just a bit tired from work."

But this isn't a work problem.

I don’t know what the problem is.

I’m afraid that seeing a therapist will confirm that I am "sick." But I’m even more afraid of not going. Because now, I can no longer keep denying it. She saw right through me. She listed it all out. I have nowhere left to hide.

Maybe… maybe it’s okay to go.

Maybe I need someone to tell me that I haven't had a breakdown.

Maybe I need someone to tell me that I can still be saved.

But right now, I don’t want to think about anything. I just want to lie here. I just want to face this wall.

I don’t want to think. I don’t want to plan. I don’t want to control.

I’m just so tired.

# Saturday, November 12, 2022, 15:45

I just sent Xinyu away. She knocked on the door and said she wanted to play with me, but I told her, "Daddy’s a bit busy right now." She left obediently, but I could hear the disappointment in her voice from the living room.

I’m sitting in the study now, staring at the Excel spreadsheet that took me two hours to build. My mother’s blood sugar data, check-up dates, doctors' advice, potential follow-up tests... I’ve categorized everything, color-coded the risk levels, and even calculated a "health score." It looks comprehensive, professional.

But I know I’m just lying to myself.

This spreadsheet changes nothing. When my mother sees it, she’ll just say, "You’re overcomplicating things again," and my father will keep insisting that "at her age, we should just let nature take its course." I’ve built a perfect system, yet it does absolutely nothing to change their attitudes. I’m using data to hide from my fear. That’s all this is.

I always thought that as long as I was cautious enough and planned meticulously enough, I could protect everything I care about. Work wouldn't go wrong because I’d make detailed plans. Family finances wouldn't go wrong because I’d calculated every cent in Excel. My daughter’s education wouldn't go wrong because I’d check every grade and every piece of teacher feedback.

But then my mother’s blood sugar spiked. I researched everything, did everything I could, and it spiked anyway.

I can’t control it. And I’ve realized that I can neither control it nor escape the responsibility for it.

It’s 15:45. Xinyu is in the living room, my wife is looking after my mother, and my father is watching TV. None of them know what I’m thinking in here. I should be with my daughter, but I’m sitting here staring at a meaningless spreadsheet. I should be working, but I’m wondering how many days of leave I’ll need to take if my mother has to be hospitalized. I’m supposed to be the one with the answers, but right now, I only have questions.

Maybe I’ve been lying to myself all along. Maybe "living by the book" isn't a way to stay safe, but a way to hide—hiding from the things that can't be planned, from the variables that fall outside my control.

But now the variables have arrived. And I don’t know what to do.

I hear Xinyu singing. She’s a bit out of tune, but she sounds happy. I should go be with her.

But I’m still sitting here.

December 12, 2023, 8:30 PM

Just now in the kitchen, while Li Xiaolin was clearing the dishes, I leaned against the doorframe and the words just came out. I told her about how I’ve over-planned everything these past few years—how I packed our daughter’s weekends to the brim, and how I even scheduled my parents’ walks into my calendar. She stopped what she was doing and looked at me, so I kept going. I told her that by doing all that, I’ve actually missed out on so much.

She smiled and asked if I was "reflecting" again. I said it wasn’t reflection, it was… finally understanding.

Then I told her: "I haven't changed. I’ve just become more real."

The moment I said it, even I was a bit taken aback. But that is exactly how I feel right now. It’s not that I’ve turned into a different person, or that I’ve suddenly become romantic or impulsive. I’m still the same Zhou Yu—I still make plans, I still check the data, and I still worry about my mother’s blood sugar. It’s just that now I know what truly matters behind all those plans and worries.

Li Xiaolin reached out and squeezed my hand. She said she had seen it coming for a long time.

We stood there in the kitchen—she still in her nurse’s uniform, me in my work clothes from today—just holding hands in silence. In that moment, so many things crossed my mind. I thought about my recent walks with my father; when he told stories of his youth, I was actually listening, not thinking about the next task. I thought about reading to Xinyu last night; she sat on my lap and asked why the little bunny in the story had to work so hard, and I actually paused to think and give her a real answer, instead of just rushing to flip the page.

These subtle shifts, it seems, are the only changes that matter.

I always thought that "change" meant making some grand decision, breaking something down, or taking a huge risk. But it’s not. Real change is doing the same things, but with a different heart. It’s truly being present when you are with someone. It’s remembering that the point of planning is to create space for life, not to crowd life out.

Tomorrow, I’ll still go to the office, I’ll still have meetings, and I’ll still do all that project management work. But I know now that it isn't everything. It’s just one part of life.

Holding Li Xiaolin’s hand at that moment, I felt a sense of deep contentment. Not because of any success, not because I had reached some goal, but simply because—right now, I am real.

# September 18, 2022, 10:30 PM

My conversation with Xiaolin is still looping in my head.

She asked me what was wrong, saying I’ve looked exhausted lately. I was going to brush it off like I always do—"Nothing, just busy at work"—but this time, I didn't. I told her the truth: that as a son, a father, and an employee, I am failing. I failed to catch my mother’s blood sugar issues in time; I can’t seem to help Xinyu with her schoolwork properly; and I made a mistake on the project last week. With all three hitting me at once, I... I just can't handle it.

She said, "Don't overthink it."

The moment I heard those words, a flash of anger surged inside me. I wasn't angry at her, exactly; it was more... the feeling of being misunderstood. She thought I was just tired and needed to relax. But that’s not what I meant. I meant that I am genuinely failing. Her attempt at comfort made me feel like she was only supporting me on the surface, without actually understanding.

I said, "Maybe I’m just too weak."

I stunned myself the moment the words left my mouth. It didn't sound like something I would say. But it is exactly what I’m thinking.

She didn't say anything else. Neither did I. We just sat there, separated by a strange kind of distance.

Later, I stood on the balcony alone for a long time. One by one, the streetlights below flickered on. I started thinking—maybe this family doesn't need me at all. Xiaolin can take care of Xinyu, manage the household, and even look after our parents. What is the point of my existence? I thought I was the one supporting this home, but now it feels like I’m just here taking up space.

The mistakes at work, the helplessness in life, the impatience with my daughter... these are all real. It’s not just "in my head."

I need to do something. But I don't know what.

Tomorrow goes on regardless. Xinyu still needs help with her homework, Mom has her check-up next week, and the project issues still need to be resolved. There isn't enough time. There isn't enough energy.

I’m tired.

But I can’t stop.

# Morning Run Notes - Oct 12

Headed out at 6:30 AM. The weather was great, and the air felt incredibly crisp. Around the third kilometer, an idea suddenly flashed into my mind. I ended up sitting down on a park bench and pulling out my phone to jot it down. It’s a strange feeling—running used to be just running, a way to clear my head, but now I’m actually thinking about work.

**A few thoughts on the AI project:**

1. **Entry points for UX optimization**
   - Current recommendation algorithms mainly look at browsing and purchase history, but we could actually incorporate "dwell time." A longer stay suggests genuine interest rather than just an accidental swipe.
   - If we can identify patterns where a user repeatedly lingers on a category without purchasing, we could push targeted discounts or new arrivals. The conversion rate should be much higher than blind recommendations.

2. **Technical Feasibility**
   - Backend modification costs shouldn't be too high; it’s mostly about redesigning data tags.
   - The frontend display logic will need adjustments, but our current framework should be able to support it.
   - The key is ensuring real-time performance; the recommendations shouldn't feel laggy to the user.

3. **Risk Factors**
   - If recommendations are *too* precise, users might feel "watched." We need to find the right balance.
   - We need A/B testing; we can’t just roll this out to everyone at once.
   - Data privacy compliance needs to be confirmed upfront.

**To be honest, I’m a bit excited.**

This idea didn't just appear out of thin air; it’s been swirling around for a few days. Something a facilitator said during that Design Thinking workshop kept sticking with me: "The best innovations often come from the meticulous observation of user behavior, not the technology itself." In the past, I approached projects based on requirement docs—as long as there were no bugs and we delivered on time, I considered it a success. Looking at it from this new angle actually makes things more interesting.

Thinking about this while running is a first for me. Running used to be pure headspace, but now it’s when my thoughts are clearest. Li Xiaolin says I haven't been "acting like myself" lately, but I actually feel more like myself than ever—at least I’m listening to my own ideas instead of just being pushed along by a schedule.

**Next Steps:**
- Carve out some time this weekend to flesh this out into a simple proposal document.
- Chat with my lead about feasibility first and get his feedback.
- If the direction looks good, pull 1 or 2 colleagues in for a brainstorm.

Energy levels are good. The morning sun is filtering through the leaves, feeling quite warm. My daughter is still asleep, and my father is probably still watching the morning news. I need to head home and get breakfast ready for Xinyu.

# 20 Minutes in a Coffee Shop

**Time: December 11, Morning**  
**Location: The coffee shop downstairs**

---

## Observation Fragments

Caught sight of the billboard for that new residential development across the street—yet another "Urban Renewal Project." Glass curtain walls, smart communities, and so on.

It occurred to me that when I joined the company eight years ago, this area was still a cluster of old factories. Now, it changes every year. Hangzhou is redefining itself.

**This got me thinking: I am also redefining myself.**

---

## Keyword Fragments

- Stability ≠ Stagnation (I finally, truly understand this now)
- Change isn't betrayal; it’s maturity
- The city tears down old buildings to build new ones; I am letting go of my identity as a "perfect planner" and learning how to simply *be there*
- Both processes take time, and both require embracing "uncertainty"

---

## Small Realizations

I used to fear change because I mistook "stability" for being "fixed."

Now I see: stability can be a foundation, not a shackle.

Just like this city—its stability comes from its willingness to change. The infrastructure is solid, but the buildings are renewed. The economic structure is steady, but the industries are upgrading.

The same goes for me. My core hasn't changed—I’m still that reliable person, still someone who values family. But I’ve learned:
- At work, I can speak my mind instead of always saying, "It’s nothing, just a bit tired."
- In my relationships, I can let go of the obsession with "perfect caretaking" and just show up authentically.
- In life, I can admit "I don't know the next step yet" instead of being consumed by a packed schedule.

---

## Actionable Insights

No need to decide anything right now.

But remember: unplanned time is still time. These 20 minutes in the coffee shop aren't on the calendar, but they might be more important than many "productive" moments.

Reading a storybook to my daughter tonight, a weekend walk with my father, a real conversation with my wife—none of these are in the "five-year plan," but they *are* life.

**The city is changing, and so am I. This is normal.**

Sometimes, the best plan is no plan at all.

Spent the weekend crouching in the garden with Xinyu watching butterflies. She asked me, "Daddy, why do butterflies fly around like that?" I couldn't find the words to answer. So we just sat there for a long time, saying nothing, just watching.

She said, "Is it looking for its home?"

I smiled. Maybe.

I’ve only recently realized that companionship isn't about having everything scheduled. Sometimes, it’s just being there, watching butterflies together.

[Image: A blurred silhouette of a butterfly, backlit]

Xiaolin, I’m late.

I took Mom to the hospital for her follow-up this morning, and her blood sugar was even higher than last time. The doctor said she needs further testing and might need to adjust her medication. I really tried to make it back for the 9 o'clock meeting, but I still ended up being 15 minutes late.

This is a first for me.

I didn't perform well in the meeting either; my mind was all over the place. It wasn't until my supervisor asked, "Are you okay?" that I realized how obvious it must have been. I’ve always been the type of person who never slips up, but today...

I’m not trying to make excuses. It’s just...

(Pause)

I’m starting to realize I might be hitting a breaking point. I have to do well at work, take care of Mom’s health, check our daughter’s homework, and your shifts are so exhausting too. I keep thinking about how to balance it all, but it’s getting harder and harder. Being late today actually panicked me a little—I always thought that as long as I planned everything meticulously enough, I could keep it all under control. But clearly, that’s not the case.

Mom’s situation might be more complicated than we thought. I’m wondering if I should take a few days off to take her to a major hospital for a second opinion. But if I do that, the project...

Never mind, I won’t think that far ahead for now. I just wanted to let you know. I’m not "fine."

# Morning Run Notes

**December 11, 2023 | Morning**

I didn’t finish my planned five-kilometer run today.

When the thought first popped into my head, I actually stunned myself. It’s been three years, and it’s never been like this. Every Monday, Wednesday, and Friday, out the door at 6:30 AM, a fixed route back and forth along Fengtai South Road—5 kilometers, 40 minutes. I even have an alarm set on my phone for it.

But today, as I passed the construction site, I stopped.

I’ve run past that new development many times before, but always in a hurry. For some reason today, I just stood there outside the hoarding and watched. The skeleton of steel and concrete had already risen; workers were busy on the scaffolding, and the tower cranes turned slowly in the morning light. I remembered how much I’ve always loved watching architectural documentaries, seeing how a city grows bit by bit... yet I had never actually stopped to look.

I took out my phone and jotted down a few thoughts. About the design of this complex, about how Hangzhou has changed over the years, and about why I’m always in such a rush.

Then, I didn’t keep running.

I walked to a nearby cafe and sat down. This wasn't on my schedule at all. Usually, I should be back home by 6:50 AM. But I just sat there, drank an Americano, watched the morning mist outside the window, and thought for over twenty minutes.

What was I thinking about? Nothing in particular, really. I was just wondering: why am I always in such a hurry? Why did changing my route and sitting for a while longer make me feel a bit uneasy, yet also... relieved?

Li Xiaolin once asked me why I couldn't change my plans once in a while. My answer back then was that plans allow me to control risks and avoid being a burden to my family. But I realize now that wasn't the reason. It was just my fear of uncertainty.

This tiny change today was actually no big deal. No one was hurt by it. My daughter’s breakfast was still prepared on time. Work won't fall apart just because I’m ten minutes late.

But what does this mean for me?

I think it means I’m starting to believe that stability and flexibility aren't contradictory. I can be a reliable person and still stop to look at a construction site. I can have a plan while leaving room for the unexpected and the surprising. This isn't a risk, nor is it irresponsibility. It’s just... growth, I suppose.

I’m only now realizing that what I’ve been doing isn't changing who I am, but changing how I live.

By the time I headed home, the sky was fully bright. I thought to myself: tomorrow, I might go back to my planned run. But maybe one day, I’ll stop again. And when that happens, I won't feel guilty about it anymore.

Sunday, February 5, 2023  10:15 AM

I’m still playing back what Mom said last night in my head.

"I’m worried about you."

She was sitting on the sofa, watching me hold that blood sugar logbook while scrolling through medical data on my phone. She just looked at me with something in her eyes I’d never noticed before. I thought she was going to ask if I’d found a new treatment plan or how her latest checkup report looked. But she didn't. She just said, "I want you to sit with me for a while. Just like this. You don’t have to do anything."

At the time, I was thinking—sit for a while? I have so much to organize. I haven't printed that new low-glycemic recipe yet, and I still need to cross-check her last physical exam data...

But I put my phone down anyway. I sat beside her. I did nothing.

Twenty minutes. Just sitting there.

Mom’s hand rested on mine. She didn't speak. Neither did I. The only sounds were the TV in the living room and the occasional car horn from the street below. I realized my breathing was slowly deepening. That constant feeling of being wound tight seemed to loosen, just a little.

I suddenly understood something strange. I’ve been trying to "save" her—with data, with plans, with everything I can control. But she doesn't actually need to be saved. She just needs me to be there. That’s it.

Lately, I’ve been terrified of losing control. The insomnia, the racing heart, the mistakes at work... I thought that as long as I planned and managed everything perfectly, I could stop bad things from happening. But Mom’s words showed me another perspective—maybe my "perfect control" is its own kind of losing control. I’m pushing away the very person I’m trying to protect.

I’ve never thought about it like that.

I didn't bring the logbook when I went to see her this morning. We checked Xinyu’s homework together, and then we just sat there, chatting for a while about her younger days. Mom laughed. A real laugh, not just a polite one.

Maybe "stability" isn't what I always thought it was. Maybe it’s not achieved by controlling everything. Maybe... it’s just being able to sit with the people you care about and do nothing at all.

I’m still exhausted. The insomnia is still there. But today feels different.

It’s not because the problems are solved. It’s because I think I finally understand something.

# Work Log - October 2, 2023

**Project:** AI-Driven E-commerce UX Optimization Proposal  
**Participants:** Innovation Department (All), Product Lead, Technical Lead  
**Meeting Time:** Morning of October 2, 2023  

---

## Meeting Feedback Summary

### Core Feedback
1. **Product Lead's Feedback**
   - The proposal framework is "too idealistic" and fails to fully account for the resource constraints of the existing system.
   - Requires a clearer ROI projection and a phased implementation roadmap.
   - Recommended starting with a small-scale pilot.

2. **Technical Lead's Feedback**
   - The compatibility between the current tech stack and the AI modules needs a deep-dive assessment.
   - Data labeling costs and timelines have not been fully calculated.
   - Questioned the technical maturity; suggested adding competitor case studies.

3. **Other Feedback**
   - The timeline is overly optimistic.
   - Lack of contingency plans for failure scenarios.

---

## To-Do List

- [ ] **Technical Feasibility Analysis** (To be completed this week)
  - Map out the compatibility between the existing system architecture and the AI modules.
  - Research 3-5 mature industry cases (competitors or similar scenarios).
  - Estimate data labeling costs and cycles.

- [ ] **Resource Assessment** (By this Friday)
  - Calculate required computing resources and storage space.
  - Evaluate team manpower (Do we need additional algorithm engineers?).
  - Draft a preliminary budget.

- [ ] **Phased Implementation Plan** (By next Monday)
  - Phase 1: Small-scale pilot (User base, functional scope).
  - Phase 2: Conditions and milestones for scaling up.
  - Risk mitigation and rollback plans.

- [ ] **ROI Projection Model** (By next Tuesday)
  - Conservative estimates for user conversion rate improvements.
  - Benchmarking costs against projected gains.
  - Expected returns at 6 and 12 months.

---

## Personal Reflections

The feedback didn't reject the direction; it just pointed out that my plan isn't solid enough yet. To be honest, hearing the words "too idealistic" stung a bit at first—in my previous department, people rarely spoke so bluntly. But looking at it objectively, they’re right. In my excitement, I definitely skipped over some details.

The key question is: **Are these objections real roadblocks, or have they just not seen the possibilities yet?**

I need to answer this with data. Not to prove I’m right, but to truly figure out if this is doable. If the data shows it’s not feasible, I’ll accept that. If the data shows it’s feasible with manageable risks, then I’ll have the confidence to push forward.

This "let the facts speak" approach actually feels more grounding than my previous "play it safe" mindset. I’m not gambling; I’m validating.

I’ll have to put in some overtime next week, but this analysis is worth it.

---

**Next Steps:** Start competitor research tomorrow; discuss data architecture compatibility with the tech team on Wednesday.

# Work Log - November 28, 2023

**Event Record: Innovation Culture Sharing Session - Sharing Flexible Project Management Methodologies**

---

Attended the company-organized Innovation Culture Sharing Session this afternoon. I was invited on short notice to give a 20-minute presentation titled "Preserving Flexibility Within Certainty: Management Practices in E-commerce Platform Projects."

There were about 50-60 attendees, including department managers and core team members. The presentation focused on three main areas:

1. **The Limitations of Traditional Risk Assessment Frameworks** — Over-planning can actually stifle a team's creative space. In the past, we tried to have a contingency plan for every single variable, which resulted in rigid processes.

2. **The Concept of "Stable Flexibility"** — Stability isn't about avoiding all risks; it’s about maintaining room for adjustment in key areas once priorities are clear. For example, in our e-commerce platform iterations, we kept the core architecture stable while allowing teams to iterate rapidly on functional modules based on feedback.

3. **Data-Driven Decision Nodes** — Using clear metrics rather than intuition to determine when to stay the course and when to pivot.

---

**Feedback:**

The reaction after the speech was unexpected. At least eight colleagues approached me privately, saying, "You said exactly what I've been thinking but didn't dare to say." This included two managers from Operations and the Head of Product, all of whom expressed interest in trying similar management styles in their own projects.

My supervisor also validated the session afterward, noting that "this mindset is very valuable and worth promoting internally."

---

**Personal Reflection:**

To be honest, standing there talking about these things, I realized how much I’ve changed over the past few months. It wasn't a sudden shift, but a gradual adjustment—moving from "planning everything out" to "daring to change where it matters."

This philosophy hasn't just changed how I manage projects; it has changed my outlook on life. I just didn't expect this shift to have such an impact on a professional level.

# Diary

**December 4, 2022, 11:47 PM**

I’m lying in bed after a shower. Li Xiaolin is already asleep, but I still can’t get to sleep.

It was the weekend today. I told everyone I was heading out to handle some errands, but I actually visited three different hospitals. Registering, consulting, gathering materials. I wanted to find the most persuasive arguments to get my parents to cooperate with their treatment. I even spent my time on the subway searching for "consequences of high blood sugar patients refusing treatment," hoping to use those cases to "scare" them into it.

Looking back now, I feel a bit ridiculous.

My mother’s blood sugar levels have indeed been unstable lately, and some indicators in her heart function tests were high. I understand what that means. But my parents just won't cooperate; they say, "We’re old now, there’s no need to make a fuss." The more I push, the more they resist. I can feel that sense of powerlessness—I’ve organized so much data and made such detailed plans, yet I still can’t change a thing.

This isn't like work. At work, I create a plan, the team executes it, and the project is delivered on time. The logic is clear, the variables are controllable. But this is different. My parents have their own ideas, my daughter has her own pace of learning, and my mother’s health won't improve just because of how much research I’ve done.

Xiaolin asked me where I went today, and I just said I was taking care of some things. I didn't tell her the truth. I don't know why, but I didn't want her to know I was "comparison shopping" for hospitals. It feels like admitting to it would be admitting that I’m actually panicking—that I’m using more plans to mask my fear of losing control.

I’ve always believed that as long as I’m cautious and meticulous enough, I can protect the people around me. That belief has sustained me for years. But now... I don't know.

Maybe the problem isn't that my plans aren't detailed enough. Maybe this isn't something that can be solved with more rational analysis.

That thought makes me even more uneasy.

Tomorrow goes on. My daughter’s schoolwork, the projects at work, the check-up arrangements at the end of the month... everything is waiting. I should sleep.

But I’m so

Xiaolin, I’ve been thinking about something.

If Mom really needs to be hospitalized for further tests later on, how should we handle our time off? The project I’m working on right now won't be delivered until mid-December, so it’ll be very difficult for me to take a long leave during that period.

I imagine it’s also quite hard for you to adjust your schedule given your role as head nurse, right?

Then there’s our daughter—if we both need to be there for Mom, who’s going to pick her up from school? It might be a bit much for Dad to handle on his own.

I was wondering if we should put a plan together in advance. The doctor mentioned that Mom’s blood sugar needs close monitoring, and she might need a cardiac ultrasound and some other tests. I did some research, and those appointments usually need to be booked ahead of time...

Actually, maybe I’m overthinking things today. But I feel like we should get these details sorted out now, so if anything unexpected happens, we won't be caught off guard.

What do you think?

June 3, 2022  10:30 PM

Something felt off today.

I got a call this afternoon about my mom’s physical. The doctor said her blood sugar levels have gone up again and recommended stricter dietary management. My chest started feeling tight the moment I heard it. After hanging up, I just sat there in the office, staring at the report for nearly ten minutes. Everyone else had left; the office was dead quiet.

Then I made a decision—I was going to figure this out, thoroughly. I ordered medical guidelines and a glucose monitor on my phone. On the way home, I stopped by the supermarket for some low-sugar ingredients. Once I got back, I opened Excel and started drafting a dietary management plan. I listed out what Mom should eat every day, the calorie counts, the carbohydrate percentages... I worked on it until after ten.

Li Xiaolin saw me like this and said, "Don't be so tense. It’s not like Mom has some major illness; her blood sugar is just a bit high. It’ll be fine with some adjustments."

I didn't respond to her directly, just kept my eyes on the screen. After a while, I said, "I don't want things to get worse because of poor execution."

She sighed and didn't say anything else.

Sitting here now, I’m wondering... am I taking care of Mom? Or am I doing something else?

I’ve had this feeling for the past two months. That performance review at work has been weighing on me; I know I’ve probably hit a ceiling, but I don't know what to do about it. So maybe I’m just using other things to fill the void—making plans for Mom, checking Xinyu’s homework, balancing the books... It’s as if as long as I’m controlling *something*, I don’t have to think about the bigger problems.

But Mom’s situation is real. Her blood sugar really did go up. What I’m doing isn't meaningless.

It’s just... I’m a little scared. Scared that if I’m not systematic enough, not meticulous enough, things will spiral out of control. Scared that I won’t be able to take care of her properly. Scared that I’ll slip up somewhere.

I’ve been running for three years now. Usually, it clears my head, but during my last few runs, I realized I wasn't clearing anything—I was just ruminating on the same problems over and over. The more I think, the more exhausted I feel.

Whatever, I should sleep. I have to take Mom back to the hospital for a follow-up tomorrow.

# Memo: Information Regarding Mom’s Blood Sugar Management

**Date:** November 5, 2022, Afternoon  
**Subject:** Mom’s Recent Physical Exam Data & Communication Log  

---

## I. Summary of Doctor’s Advice

Accompanied Mom for her follow-up this morning. Fasting blood glucose: **8.2 mmol/L** (Normal range: 3.9–6.1); two-hour postprandial: **11.5 mmol/L** (Normal range: <7.8).

The doctor’s recommendations:
- An Oral Glucose Tolerance Test (OGTT) is required to rule out Type 2 diabetes.
- Dietary adjustments: Reduce refined carbohydrates and increase fiber intake.
- Exercise: 30 minutes, 3–4 times a week.
- Medication will be necessary if the diagnosis is confirmed.

This is all manageable. The numbers are high, but it’s not the worst-case scenario.

---

## II. Dad’s Exact Words (Needs further reflection)

When I brought this up with Dad this afternoon, he said: **"You always want to control everything. Letting nature take its course is the best way."**

I wanted to push back at the time, but I didn't. I’m still thinking about that sentence.

Possible interpretations:
1. Does he mean I shouldn't be so tense? (But the doctor said we need to take this seriously.)
2. Is he saying I’m too anxious? (Probably, but this isn't anxiety—it’s responsibility.)
3. Does he genuinely not want to cooperate with the treatment? (This is my biggest worry.)
4. Is he expressing some kind of "life philosophy"? (He’s always been like this. He taught me to "follow the rules" growing up, and now he says "let nature take its course." Those two don't seem to align.)

---

## III. Draft of My Arguments (Attempting to persuade him)

Arguments I want to use:
- Statistics: People with poorly controlled blood sugar have a 3x higher risk of cardiovascular events within 5 years.
- Cost considerations: The cost of prevention now < the cost of treatment later (time, money, suffering).
- Real-life case: His colleague, Old Wang, had a stroke last year because he didn't manage his blood sugar.

But I realize these points might be useless with him. Will he even listen? Or will he just keep saying things like "what will be, will be"?

---

## IV. Action Items (Executable Checklist)

- [ ] Monday: Schedule an appointment with the Endocrinology Department for the OGTT.
- [ ] Tuesday: Research diabetes management programs at hospitals in Hangzhou.
- [ ] This weekend: Plan a dietary adjustment scheme with Mom.
- [ ] Monthly: Accompany her for check-ups and track blood sugar trends.
- [ ] Discuss with Li Xiaolin: Who will handle our daughter’s school pickups if Mom needs more care?

---

## V. What if Dad continues to be uncooperative?

I need contingency plans because I can’t guarantee he will change his mind:

**Option A:** Let Mom decide for herself. She is more willing to follow the doctor’s advice than he is.
**Option B:** Ask one of Dad’s old friends (like a former colleague) to talk to him. Sometimes he listens to his friends more than he listens to me.
**Option C:** Intervene forcefully only if serious consequences arise. (But this is too reactive.)

To be honest, I don’t like any of these options. I want a "perfect solution," but it seems there isn't one.

---

## VI. Reflection (I don’t really want to write this, but I will)

I’ve realized I’m doing something specific: I’m turning "worry" into "data" and "plans."

It’s as if I believe that by organizing all the information, listing every argument, and mapping out every scenario, I can "control" the situation. But Dad’s comment hit a nerve—maybe I really am trying too hard to control things.

Mom’s blood sugar won’t drop because of my Excel sheets. Dad’s mindset won’t change because of my statistics.

All I can do is provide information, accompany her to check-ups, and offer gentle discouragement of bad habits. As for the rest... is it their choice to make?

But if I really believe that, why am I still writing this memo?

Whatever. I’ll leave it at this for now and look at it again tomorrow.

During dinner tonight, an idea struck me. I want to write something for my parents—not a formal document, just their story. From Dad’s time as a state-enterprise engineer to now, and Mom’s journey as an elementary school teacher; how they met, how they raised me—all those details I’ve never seriously asked about.

It’s not because I’m afraid of losing them—I’m no longer driven by that kind of fear. It’s because I’ve suddenly realized that life isn't a project that needs perfect planning. It is a series of moments that deserve to be seen, recorded, and celebrated.

I asked Mom if she’d like to talk about her younger days. She was taken aback for a moment, then smiled. She said yes.

This idea makes me feel grounded. Not the "I’ve done the right thing" kind of grounded, but rather—I finally know what truly matters to me.

I’ll start asking Dad some questions tomorrow.

Monday, September 5, 2022. Morning.

I didn't sleep well again last night. I woke up around 3:00 AM, and my chest still felt a bit tight.

The doctor said it’s anxiety. I’ve gone over that medical report repeatedly—the ECG was normal, blood pressure was normal, and all the blood work was within range. He said with total certainty, "Mr. Zhou, there’s nothing wrong with your heart. These are classic symptoms of anxiety." I nodded, pretending to be relieved. But when I got up this morning, I still instinctively reached for my chest.

Why is this happening?

I’ve never been an anxious person. At least, that’s what I always thought. I’m organized, I have a routine; I eat when I’m supposed to eat and sleep when I’m supposed to sleep. I never imagined I’d be going to the hospital for a "psychological issue." But now... I don’t know.

Last Friday, while I was out for a run, my chest suddenly tightened. I was genuinely terrified. In that moment, so many things flashed through my mind—my mother’s blood sugar, my daughter’s grades, that delayed project at work... and then the palpitations started. I stopped and crouched by the side of the road, feeling like I was about to collapse.

The doctor says I’m fine. But how can I fully believe that? I checked the health apps on my phone again last night. Chest tightness and palpitations are indeed listed as symptoms of anxiety. But they’re also symptoms of many heart conditions... I searched again. I didn't put my phone down until after 1:00 AM.

Li Xiaolin asked if I was still thinking about what the doctor said. I told her no. But the truth is, it’s all I’ve been thinking about.

The scariest part isn’t the chest tightness itself. It’s the sudden realization that I might actually be reaching my breaking point. For years, I felt like I had everything under control—work, family, finances, my parents' health. But now? My mother’s blood sugar is getting harder to manage, my daughter’s teacher says her foundation isn't solid, and I actually slipped up on a project at the company. I’m failing in three places at once.

And I don’t even have the time to figure out what to do.

Up at 6:30 every morning. Run, shower, check my daughter’s homework, review my mother’s blood sugar logs, meetings, project reviews, reading my daughter a bedtime story... and then I lie in bed, my head spinning with everything I haven't finished.

I don’t know what anxiety is supposed to feel like. I just know that I’m exhausted. So exhausted that sometimes when I see my daughter get a math problem wrong, I lose my patience. So exhausted that sometimes when I look at my mother’s check-up results, I think, "Can I really take care of her?"

The doctor said I need to relax. How? I don’t even know where to begin making changes.

Maybe I really am sick. Not in my heart, but something else.

It’s 6:30 now. Time to get up for my run. But I don’t want to move.

September 28, 2023, Morning

My conversation with my boss yesterday keeps playing on a loop in my head.

He agreed to my request for flexible working hours. When I told him, "My professional contribution is just as important as my family responsibilities," he didn't push back. He just nodded and said, "I’ve seen your performance over the years; this adjustment won't be a problem."

But sitting here in the office now, it still feels a bit surreal.

I talked about this with my therapist last night. She said, "This means you’re starting to believe that your life is worth redesigning." I didn't respond at the time; I just nodded. But I’ve been chewing on those words all night—*life is worth redesigning.*

Is it, really?

I don't know. Maybe.

Lately, I’ve been wondering why I keep going. Why I keep taking all of this on. Why my existence matters to anyone at all. There are no answers to these questions, but when I said those words yesterday, it felt like something shifted slightly. It wasn't a transformation, not some dramatic moment, just... I said it. I told my boss that I wanted something different.

My daughter asked me last night why I haven’t been reading with her as much as I used to. I told her work has been a bit busy. She just sat on my lap and didn't ask again. Li Xiaolin was in the kitchen; I could hear her sighing.

I don’t know if this adjustment will actually change anything. Maybe it’s just pushing the problem further down the road. Maybe I’ll still regret it ten years from now. But at least... at least I’m not being completely passive. At least I spoke up.

Maybe that, in itself, is a kind of change.

I’m still exhausted. My energy is still low. But this fatigue feels like it has a slightly different texture now—it’s no longer total emptiness, but... a weariness with a bit of purpose?

I’m not sure.

But I’ll keep seeing the therapist. I’ll try this new schedule. I’ll keep reading with my daughter.

Maybe that’s enough.

# February 15, 2023, Morning

6:45 AM. Sitting on a park bench. My coffee is still warm.

Today’s run felt different. Before heading out, I didn’t set a 5km goal on my phone like I usually do, nor did I track my pace. A little over three kilometers in, my legs felt a bit sore and my breathing wasn't quite smooth, so I just stopped. In the past, I would have told myself, "Just one more kilometer," but today... I just stopped. I walked to this park, sat down, and watched the morning mist as it lingered.

Something my therapist said last time keeps spinning in my head: "Stability doesn't come from perfect control." I didn't really get it then—or rather, I understood it but didn't want to admit it. Because hasn't everything I've done over the years been about control? Controlling work progress, controlling finances, controlling my daughter’s study schedule, controlling my own routine...

But now I’m realizing that the more I try to control, the more my body starts to fail. The insomnia, the chest tightness, those inexplicable waves of dread... what are they trying to tell me?

Sitting here, a thought suddenly struck me—maybe I’ve been too conservative.

The moment that thought surfaced, it actually startled me. I’ve never thought that way before. Since I was a kid, my father taught me to "follow the steps," and I always believed that was the right way, the safe way. I chose computer science in college, I never hopped jobs, I bought the house, got married... everything according to plan. I always thought that if I did this, nothing could go wrong.

But what about now? I’ve controlled so much, yet I can’t control my own sleep. I’ve planned so much, yet I can’t plan away where this nameless fear comes from.

Yesterday at home, I watched an architecture documentary for a while. I didn't watch it with the intent to "learn"; I just... watched. My wife asked what I was doing, and I said, "Nothing, just watching." Before, I would have felt this was a waste of time—time that should have been spent with my daughter or organizing financial spreadsheets. But yesterday, I allowed myself to do it. It sounds strange, but allowing myself to waste a little time actually made me feel relieved.

Maybe... stability doesn't come from a perfect plan. Maybe it comes from... I don't know. From accepting that there are things I simply cannot control?

The sky is getting brighter now. Time to head home. My daughter has school today.

But I just remembered—I don't have to check if her homework from last night was perfect. I can trust her, and I can trust her teachers.

The thought makes me a little nervous. But also... a little free.

# March 8, 2022, 6:47 AM

I just closed the job board tab.

I didn't see that CTO position until after 11:00 last night. It’s an e-commerce company in Hangzhou; the salary is 30% higher than what I’m making now, and the team is much larger. I spent half an hour staring at the job description, mapping it against my own experience—project management, system architecture, team building... everything seemed to align. And yet, some things didn't.

Then I asked myself: Can I do this?

The question looped in my head over and over. Can I do it? Do I have the guts?

In the end, I closed the window. I didn't submit my resume.

I’m sitting in the study now. It’s still dark out. My wife and daughter are still asleep, and my parents haven't woken up yet. This is my quietest time. I was going to put on my running shoes like I always do, but I changed my mind today—I’m skipping my usual 5km loop around the park and taking the long route along the river instead. It’s more open there; I can run longer.

I need more time to think this through.

I’ve been with the company for eight years. I went from junior engineer to project manager. During my last annual review, my supervisor said, "Management positions are limited." That sentence felt like a door being softly shut. I always thought that if I just followed the path and did every project well, a new opportunity would eventually come. But now, it seems it might not.

A CTO position, starting over at a new company. It sounds like a breakthrough. But it also means uncertainty. The new company culture, the team integration, the project risks... and if I jump ship, what happens to things at home? My mother’s recent check-up results weren't great; her blood sugar isn't well-controlled. My daughter’s math foundation at school seems to be lagging—the teacher’s feedback last time left me a bit worried.

Can I handle all of this at once?

Maybe I don’t even need this position. My current job has no room for growth, but it’s stable. The salary is enough to support the family and pay the mortgage. I have time to read storybooks to my daughter and time to take my mother for her monthly check-ups. These things matter to me.

But what if it’s always like this? Ten years, twenty years from now—will I still just be the "reliable but unremarkable" Zhou Yu?

I don’t know.

The sky is getting light. Time to head out for that run. I need to go further today.
