August 14, 2023, Afternoon

I’m sitting in the office. The screen is still on, but I can’t process anything.

My father’s funeral was last Friday. I remember standing there in black, staring at the casket. People were crying and talking around me, but their voices sounded so far away. My face was expressionless. I could feel Li Xiaolin looking at me, her hand resting gently on my back, but I couldn't feel any warmth.

One month. Just one month.

My mother passed in mid-July. I was with her for her check-up that day. The doctor said her blood sugar was well-controlled. I remember feeling a sense of relief, thinking I was doing a good job. Then she suddenly said she had a headache, and then... that was it. A brain hemorrhage. It was so fast.

I took her for check-ups every month. I have an Excel spreadsheet recording every piece of data from the last three years. I even charted the trends. But none of it mattered.

My father followed her a week later. The doctor said it was a myocardial infarction. I thought maybe it was because he lost my mother and his heart couldn't take it. But that sounds too literary. Maybe it was just a coincidence. Maybe there was no reason at all.

I told Li Xiaolin I needed a break. When I heard myself say those words, my voice was calm—so calm it didn't sound like me. Then I took a month off. I didn't explain much to my supervisor, just said there was a family emergency. They don't need to know. No one needs to know.

It’s the second week now. I walk around the house, looking at the things—my father’s old newspapers, my mother’s sewing basket, the notebooks she left behind. I found her diary.

I shouldn't have read it.

But I did.

She wrote: "Zhou Yu asked me about my blood sugar levels again today. He is so attentive. But I am so tired. I don't want to be a burden to him anymore."

The date was last November.

I sat on the bed, staring at those words, unable to form a reaction. "So tired." She had been saying she was tired all along, but I thought it was just normal fatigue. I thought my care could change things. I thought my Excel sheets, my regular check-ups, my weekend walks with her—I thought all of it meant something.

But she was still tired. She still left.

All my attentiveness changed nothing.

My daughter asked me why Grandma doesn't come over anymore. I didn't know how to answer. I said, "Grandma went somewhere very far away." She asked how long it would be until she came back. I didn't say anything. Li Xiaolin took over; she knows better than I do how to talk to a child about death. I sat there, feeling like a bystander.

I don't know who I am anymore.

I thought I was reliable. I thought I could take care of my family. I thought that as long as I was cautious enough and planned enough, I could protect them. But I couldn't protect anything. I didn't even see how tired my mother was.

Someone in the office walked by and asked if I wanted to grab lunch. I said no. They left.

How much longer am I going to sit here?

I don't know.

I really don't know.

# Morning Run Notes

I need to write down what my therapist said.

**"Perfection is the enemy; acceptance is the friend."**

I just finished a 5k run and have been sitting on this bench for a while, letting those words circle in my head.

Before, I always felt like I had to do everything perfectly—zero mistakes at work, my daughter’s grades had to be top-tier, my mother’s blood sugar had to be strictly controlled, and the household accounts had to be precise down to the last cent. It felt like if even one thing wasn't perfect, my entire life system would collapse.

But now I realize that this mindset itself is what's destroying me.

I’ve made mistakes at work, my daughter’s studies haven't always been smooth sailing, and my mother’s health has its ups and downs... all of this is normal. It’s not that I am failing; it’s my obsession with "perfection" that is failing.

Acceptance. It’s an unfamiliar word, but perhaps it’s exactly what I need.

Accepting that I am not a superhero. Accepting that there are things I simply cannot do. Accepting that my daughter might need time. Accepting my mother’s aging. Accepting that my career might not have as many possibilities as I imagined.

Acceptance... maybe it’s just about letting go of that tightly wound version of myself.

---

**Reminders:**
- Re-read this the next time I feel anxious.
- Perfection ≠ Responsibility.
- Acceptance ≠ Giving up.

August 3, 2022, 10:15 PM

I stood in the shower for a long time. The water was scalding, but I didn't adjust the temperature.

I still can’t quite process what just happened in my daughter’s room.

The plan was to continue reading storybooks to Xinyu tonight. This is our time—20 minutes every night—and we’ve barely missed a session in three years. But then the teacher’s message popped up on my phone this afternoon: "Needs to strengthen basic calculation practice." I read it once, then read it again. I knew it wasn't a serious issue, but I just... couldn't sit still.

So I changed my mind. I pulled out the math workbook and told Xinyu we were going to do a few problems tonight. She looked a bit disappointed, but she sat down obediently anyway.

By the third problem, she got stuck. A simple two-digit addition, and she couldn't get it right even after a long time. Watching her sit there with a furrowed brow, I suddenly... snapped. I heard myself say, "How can you not know how to do such a simple problem?"

My voice was cold.

Her eyes welled up instantly. I saw her flinch, then she quickly lowered her head to look back at the problem, pretending she hadn't heard me. But I heard it. I heard the words I said, and I saw her reaction.

I froze in that moment for about three seconds before saying, "Okay, let's keep going." But everything had changed. Her hands were shaking as she worked; I could see it.

Later, she said she was tired and wanted to sleep. I didn't push it.

Now, I’ve been standing in the bathroom for twenty minutes.

What kind of father am I?

That question has been circling my mind over and over. I’ve always told myself I’m a reliable person. Reliable at work, reliable in life. I’m never late, I finish tasks on time, and I take care of my family. That is how I define myself. But tonight, during the 20 minutes I cherish most, I said those words to my daughter.

I don't even know where that sentence came from.

My mother’s blood sugar, mistakes at work, never having enough time... all these things piled up, and I became this person. I started demanding that a six-year-old be perfect at simple addition. I replaced patience with irritability.

I can't see my face clearly in the bathroom mirror. There’s too much steam.

I remember the way my wife has been looking at me these past few days. She’s been trying to talk to me, but I always say, "It’s nothing, just a bit tired from work." But it’s not work fatigue. It’s that I’ve realized I’m losing control, and I don’t know how to stop.

I can’t do everything well at the same time. That is my clearest realization tonight.

I can't give my all to work, I can't be fully present for my daughter, and I can't take proper care of my parents. I’ve spread myself thin across everything, and as a result, I’m not enough anywhere. Not a good enough son, not a good enough father, not a good enough employee.

Now, even being "good enough" feels like a luxury.

I need to apologize to Xinyu tomorrow. But I don't know how to explain why I was like that tonight. I don't know how to make her believe that it wasn't because she isn't smart enough, but because I...

I’ll stop here.

Because I don't know how to go on.

I lay in bed, the sky still dark. My phone screen lit up—a message from Li Xiaolin.

I stared at it for a long time before replying.

---

**Li Xiaolin**
Up for a morning run? I’ve warmed up some congee for you.

**Zhou Yu**
Not today.

**Li Xiaolin**
? What’s wrong?

**Zhou Yu**
Just... don't feel like it.

**Li Xiaolin**
That’s not like you. Did something happen?

**Zhou Yu**
Nothing. Just tired.

(A long silence)

**Li Xiaolin**
Zhou Yu, about what you said yesterday... I heard you. About your mom, you need to...

**Zhou Yu**
Don’t.

**Li Xiaolin**
You can't keep going on like this. We could...

**Zhou Yu**
I said don't.

(A pause)

**Zhou Yu**
Leave the congee there. I’m going back to sleep.

---

I put my phone down. The sky was still a dull grey.

Outside the window, I could faintly hear the sound of morning runners passing by. A habit I’d kept for three years, broken just like that.

I don't know why, but I didn't even feel like explaining. Running or not, it didn't seem like it would change anything anyway.

I closed my eyes.

# November 9, 2022, 22:30

I’ve been lying here for nearly 40 minutes, and I still can’t sleep.

Xiaolin has already turned her back to me. I know she’s angry—or rather, disappointed. She asked me what was on my mind, and I told her I was just tired from work. It was a lie. She definitely saw through it. But I don’t know how to explain—how do I tell her that my head is currently filled with nothing but Mom’s blood sugar levels, echocardiogram appointments, and endless "what if" scenarios?

My phone screen lit up again. I’m searching whether a heart ultrasound requires fasting. After checking that, I moved on to complication risks for patients with high blood sugar. Then I started scrolling through discussions on a medical forum... It’s already 1:15 AM.

Mom says, "At our age, why bother with all this fuss?" But the doctor says we need further tests. I’m caught in the middle.

I was late today, too. I’m never late. I was taking Mom to the hospital for tests and got stuck in traffic; I didn't get to the office until 9:18. My supervisor gave me a look—he didn't say anything, but I felt it. I spent the whole morning playing catch-up to ensure the e-commerce project stayed on track. But my heart wasn't in the code reviews at all.

The to-do list is getting longer:
- Mom’s cardiology appointment (Friday at the latest)
- Check the registration systems for Grade A hospitals in Hangzhou
- Time-off plan—what if she needs to be hospitalized? Who will pick up Xinyu from school?
- Report project progress to my supervisor (make up for today’s delay)
- Check Xinyu’s math homework (I haven't looked at it properly for two days)
- Complete Mom’s health data logs for November by the end of the month

I can handle this. I’ve always been able to handle things.

But right now... I’m really not sure.

Planning, lists, rational analysis—these things have always worked. But now it feels like I’m trying to plug an expanding hole with my fingers. Mom’s health won’t magically improve just because I made a detailed medical plan. Work won’t stay problem-free just because I wrote an exhaustive schedule. And my daughter won’t learn better just because I’m anxious.

What exactly am I trying to control?

1:16 AM. Should I even try to sleep? Am I still getting up at 6:30 for my run?

Forget it. I’ll just organize tomorrow’s task list first.

Maybe once that’s done, I’ll finally be able to sleep.

October 8, 2022, past 11:00 PM

Just stepped out of the counseling room. Sitting in my car now, hands still shaking.

I don’t know how to start writing this. It feels like something happened today, but I can’t quite put it into words. When the counselor asked me, "Where did you fail?" it was like a dam breaking. Everything just poured out—the mistakes at work, my daughter falling behind in school, my mom’s blood sugar issues, how I’m exhausted to the point of death every single day... I even used the words "completely worthless." After saying it, I started crying—that kind of desperate sobbing, like my entire body was falling apart.

Then the counselor asked a question that is still ringing in my ears: "Do you believe that everything should be handled perfectly by you?"

I didn’t answer right away. But I knew the answer.

Yes. That’s how I’ve always thought. Excel at work, keep my daughter’s education on track, take care of my parents, keep the house spotless, plan the finances meticulously... every single thing has to be perfect. If even one thing goes wrong, I see it as my failure. And right now, everything is going wrong. So, I am a total failure.

The counselor said it’s not that the external pressure is too great, but that my expectations for myself are too high. He said it very calmly, but those words felt like they shattered something. I cried again, but it was different this time—not out of despair, but more like... being understood? I can’t quite describe it. It just felt like someone finally saw the real me, not the "reliable Zhou Yu" I pretend to be.

I’ve been pretending to be invincible. At the office, at home, in front of everyone. But the truth is, I can’t do anything right.

Wait, that’s not what the counselor said. He said that no one can perfectly fulfill every role simultaneously. It’s impossible. When I heard that, it felt like I could finally breathe properly for the first time.

Maybe the problem isn't that I failed. Maybe my definition of "success" was flawed to begin with.

Sitting here in the car, there’s a light drizzle outside. I’m thinking about the time I spend reading bedtime stories to Xinyu every night. Thinking about walking with Dad. Thinking about Mom’s check-up results. These things aren't "wrong" in themselves, but when I was doing them, my heart wasn't filled with companionship—it was filled with the thought: "I must do this perfectly."

I need to learn to let go of perfection.

It’s easy to say, but I have no idea how to do it. For thirty-nine years, I’ve lived by this standard. To suddenly change...

But at least today, for the first time, I feel like maybe I’m not a failure. Maybe my expectations were just too high.

Maybe this is the beginning.

Xiaolin, I’m sorry.

I shouldn’t have walked away like that just now. I know you were coming from a place of care. But when I heard those words... I don’t know how to describe it, I just felt this sudden surge of irritability and a desperate need to escape. Now that I’ve sat with it for a while, I realize my reaction was unfair.

You’re right, I haven't been myself lately. I’ve been trying to juggle so much—Mom’s checkups, the project deadlines, Xinyu’s schoolwork—but honestly, I think I’ve been going about it the wrong way. I kept thinking that if I just planned things meticulously enough, if I were cautious enough, I could keep everything under control. But what you said... it hit a nerve.

I can’t control everything. I know that’s the truth, but I’m still having a hard time accepting it.

About reading stories with Xinyu—you’re right. It’s been three weeks, and I haven't truly been there for her. I’ve been making excuses in my head—being busy with work, needing to take care of Mom—but none of those are excuses. She needs me, and I’ve been too busy dealing with my own anxiety.

I don’t know what state I’m in right now. Maybe just... exhausted. But that’s not on you. You’re already doing your best.

I’ll make sure to read with Xinyu tomorrow night. No matter what’s going on with work.

I’m sorry again. And thank you for always being here.

September 18, 2023, Night

When Xinyu asked me, "Daddy, are you okay?" the tears just came out.

I didn't even have time to react. My eyes just suddenly burned, and the tears fell. A six-year-old child, asking me with such a serious tone—Daddy, are you okay? It was as if I were the one who needed looking after.

When I held her, she didn't pull away; she just leaned against my chest. I said I was sorry, that Daddy had been too busy lately. She asked, "Are you feeling better now?" I nodded, but honestly, I didn't even know what I was saying.

These past few days, it feels like I’ve been in a very deep place. Waking up, working, coming home, reading with her, going to sleep—it’s all been like a machine spinning. I can’t even remember why I keep going like this. Mom’s blood sugar is up again, Dad’s legs have been hurting lately, Xiaoxiao says I’m becoming more and more silent, and Xinyu’s teacher says she needs more practice on her fundamentals... everything is piling up, and I feel like I’m failing at all of it.

But tonight, while reading *The Little Prince*, I changed things up. Instead of rushing through the pages like I usually do, I paused at certain sentences and asked Xinyu, "Why do you think the Little Prince wanted to leave his planet?" She thought for a moment and said, "Because he wanted to see the world outside."

Then I asked, "Do you think he was scared?"

She said, "A little bit, but he went anyway."

I just sat there, looking at my daughter’s earnest little face, and suddenly something clicked. I’ve been afraid this whole time—afraid of failing, afraid of not being good enough, afraid of not being able to carry all these responsibilities. But when Xinyu asked me, "Are you okay?" she wasn't afraid. She just asked.

Maybe I don’t need to get everything right. Maybe I just need to be here. To just be with her, listen to her, and see something through her innocence.

My tears haven't completely dried yet.

I need to write this moment down. Just in case I start falling again.

# MEMO

**Date:** December 22, 2023
**Subject:** Life Memoir Project – Follow-up Thoughts

---

I had a long talk with my father last night. He told me many stories I’d never heard before—regrets from his youth, career choices, and reflections on certain decisions. I recorded the conversation on my phone and have since transcribed it.

Sitting in the office now, I can’t stop thinking about it.

**Points to Confirm:**

1. **Continuity** – Should I do the same for my mother? Her recent health has me a bit worried; perhaps I should record her stories now, while we can still communicate clearly. It’s not for any specific purpose, just... I don’t want these memories to be lost.

2. **Storage** – Right now, everything is scattered across phone notes and Word docs. I need to create a unified family archive. Maybe a dedicated folder, or perhaps cloud backup. This material is too important to exist in only one place.

3. **Presentation Timing** – My father’s birthday is next March. Maybe I could print the organized memoir or present it to him in some form then. I want him to know that I was listening, and that I remembered.

4. **Potential Expansion** – Will there come a day when I want to leave something like this for Xinyu? Not as a lecture or a roadmap, but just... the traces of my life as a human being. It’s too early to think about that now, but the idea is worth noting down.

**Action Items:**
- [ ] Organize father’s recordings and proofread the transcript
- [ ] Discuss with Li Xiaolin whether to do the same for my mother
- [ ] Research storage solutions for a family archive (cloud backup?)
- [ ] Complete the final version of my father’s memoir by March

---

It’s interesting—I’ve always used Excel to manage finances and checklists to manage work, but this project has made me realize that some things cannot be protected through planning alone. Some things need to be witnessed, recorded, and cherished.

This isn't just a new task. It’s a different way of doing things.

August 16, 2023, 8:30 AM

Xiaolin asked me again if I wanted to go for a run. I told her I was tired.

I’m sitting in my father’s study. His desk calendar is still there, stuck on June 15th. I haven’t flipped the page. I don’t know why, I just haven’t. Looking at that date, it feels like as long as I don’t turn the page, time won’t move forward.

I’ve done this for so many years. Taking care of my father, my mother, my wife, my daughter. I thought this was my purpose. I thought that as long as I was careful enough, planned enough, I could protect them. I could keep everything from going wrong.

But my father died anyway.

I didn’t protect him. I took him for checkups every month, recorded his data, just like I’m doing for my mother now. But he’s still gone.

So everything I’m doing now...

Xiaolin is calling me from downstairs to come eat. I didn’t respond. I know she can hear that I’m not responding.

I don’t know how to go down there. I don’t know how to pretend that any of this still matters. I don’t know how to keep being a good husband, a good father, a good son.

Maybe I never was. Maybe I’ve just been playing a part all along, acting out a person named "Zhou Yu"—someone planned, responsible, and reliable.

But a reliable person should be able to protect his family, shouldn't he?

I pulled the study door shut.

December 13, 2022, 11:47 PM

I just got back from a run downstairs. My daughter is already asleep, and my parents have turned in for the night. Li Xiaolin is in the bedroom on her phone; I didn’t go in. Sitting here in the study, I still feel the need to write down what happened today.

The project review meeting this morning. I proposed a new architectural plan—refactoring the e-commerce platform’s payment module using microservices. It’s not a groundbreaking idea, but for our team, it’s a radical move. The boss paused for a moment, then asked me, "That doesn’t sound like your style."

I didn’t give him a clear answer. I just said I wanted to "try something new" and then pivoted to technical details. Looking back, that response was stiff—very unlike me.

Why did I do it?

That question has been spinning in my head all afternoon. I sat in my office, ostensibly working on project documentation, but I was actually asking myself that over and over. Was it because I wanted to prove I’m not entirely conservative? Or was it because... recent events have made me want to change something?

My mother’s check-up results aren't out yet. The waiting is agonizing. All I can do is record data, research information, and contact doctors, but none of that actually changes anything. Maybe that’s why I acted so out of character in the meeting. It’s as if I needed to prove somewhere—anywhere—that I can still make a change, that I can still control something.

But it’s ridiculous.

What if the plan fails? Refactoring a payment module is no small feat; it involves the entire transaction process. The risk is high. I should know that. I’ve always known that. So why did I propose it?

The moment I spoke up, I felt a strange sense of relief. Like I had broken something. But at the same time, the anxiety deepened—because I realized I was doing something irrational, and I hate irrationality more than anything.

While running tonight, I thought: maybe I’m starting to get tired of this life. Every decision pondered three times, every plan precise to the hour, every risk assessed and avoided. Over the years, this methodology has kept my life stable and made me feel safe. But now...

Now, I don’t know.

My mother’s illness, my daughter’s studies, the glass ceiling at work, the pressure of the mortgage—these aren't things I can solve with a more detailed plan. I’m seeing that more and more clearly. Yet I’m still trying to use the old ways to cope, like using one key to try and open every door.

Maybe proposing that radical plan was my way of rebelling against this sense of powerlessness. It’s childish, I know.

Tomorrow, I have to follow up on the feasibility assessment for the proposal. I need to prepare a detailed risk analysis report. The rational side of me knows exactly what to do. But another voice is saying—maybe just let it play out. See what happens.

This isn't like me.

But maybe this is exactly what I need.

I don't know.

# October 2nd, 11:00 PM

Sitting in the study. My daughter is asleep, and so are my parents. Li Xiaolin is scrolling through his phone. I’m pretending to organize financial spreadsheets, but in reality, my mind is stuck on that meeting, replaying it over and over.

"Too idealistic" and "insufficient technical maturity"—those two phrases are circling in my head.

I have to admit, when I heard them, there was a split second where I thought: *Have I been overrated?* I spent eight years in my old department and never heard such blunt rejection. It wasn't because my ideas were always right; it was because that environment had grown accustomed to my rhythm. I know that. But knowing it and truly accepting it are two different things.

The people in this new team don't know the "reliable Zhou Yu." All they see is someone who proposed an idea that sounds nice but lacks validation. This should be a sobering thought. I had already planned to do a feasibility analysis anyway. I even said so in the meeting. So why do I still feel this... self-doubt?

I think it’s because I’m still learning how to be someone willing to take risks.

In my old department, my role was clear. I knew exactly how to avoid making mistakes. But now? Now I’m in a strange place, working in a strange way. My ideas might be rejected. I might fail. This isn't a new realization—I’ve discussed this repeatedly in therapy. But actually experiencing it is on a completely different level from talking about it in a consulting room.

Still... I didn't back down. I promised to submit a detailed feasibility analysis. I have to give myself credit for that. My reaction in the moment was the right one. Let the data speak, rather than getting defensive with emotions. That is what I can do.

Maybe this is exactly what I need to learn right now: since I’ve decided to change, I have to validate that decision through real setbacks. Not in my imagination, but in the face of actual opposition.

If Mom were still here, what would she say? She always said, "Just do your best." But I understand now that "doing your best" doesn't mean doing everything perfectly. It means actually doing it—actually facing it.

Tomorrow, I keep going. Data doesn't lie. I will build a clear case for this idea. At the very least, I’ll try.

# Memo | Redefining Stability

**Date**: November 28, 2023  
**Location**: Office  
**Keywords**: Stability / Uncertainty / Trust / Failure  

---

## Old Understanding vs. New Understanding

**The Old Definition** (The logic of the past 20 years):  
- Stability = Avoiding risk, following the script, perfect execution, zero mistakes.  
- Metrics: No delays, no failures, no surprises.  
- Essence: Fear-driven.  

**The New Definition** (The truth realized now):  
- Stability = Embracing uncertainty + Trusting the team + Learning from failure.  
- Metrics: Knowing what you are doing, why you are doing it, and how to respond when things go wrong.  
- Essence: Proactive choice.  

---

## Three Core Elements

### 1. Embracing Uncertainty  
It’s not about eliminating risk; it’s about acknowledging its existence. There is no perfect roadmap for AI projects, but we know how to move forward through the fog. This is actually more stable than pretending everything is under control.  

### 2. Trusting the Team  
I used to be the "reliable but uninspired" project manager—I did all the planning and all the risk mitigation myself, which meant no one else had the chance to grow. Now, I’m trying to delegate and let team members learn through failure. The result? They are performing better than I imagined.  

### 3. Learning from Failure  
Failure itself isn't the problem. The problem is failing and then continuing to do the same thing. Every project deviation and every broken plan at home is data. I need to record, reflect, and adjust.  

---

## How This Changes My Management Style

- **Project Management**: Shifting from "predicting all risks" to "building rapid response mechanisms." Prioritizing agile iteration over perfect planning.  
- **Family Decisions**: Shifting from "planning the perfect academic path for my daughter" to "observing her interests and showing up when she needs me." Li Xiaolin was right—my anxiety was hurting the very things I wanted to protect.  
- **Career Choices**: Stability isn't staying at the same company for eight years. Stability is knowing your core value and being able to deliver that value regardless of where you are.  

---

## Moments to Remind Myself

- When the next uncertain project arrives.  
- When my daughter’s grades fluctuate.  
- When my parents' health conditions change.  
- When someone describes me as "not impressive enough."  

In these moments, pull out this memo and remember: Stability was never about perfection. Stability is staying clear-headed amidst chaos and staying committed amidst failure.

# Saturday, December 2, 2023 15:30

Something small happened this afternoon, but I feel I need to write it down.

Xinyu was drawing in the living room, and I was there with her. She was drawing a bird, but it looked a bit "crooked"—the wings were asymmetrical, and the colors bled outside the lines. In the past, I probably would have pointed out these "problems." But today, she asked me: "Dad, why is my drawing different from what the teacher taught us?"

I paused. Instead of immediately correcting or explaining, I took a real look at her drawing.

Then I said, "Because your drawing belongs to you. You see the world differently from everyone else, so your drawing should be different, too."

After hearing that, she went back to her bird. She didn't ask anything else, and she didn't change a thing. She just kept going, doing it her way.

As I sat there, I suddenly felt like crying. Not out of sadness, but... relief?

I realized I was doing something I had never done before—I wasn't mentally calculating whether this way of drawing would "affect her learning foundation," I wasn't wondering "how the teacher would grade it," and I wasn't formulating a "plan for improvement." I was just... with her. Watching her express herself in her own way.

For the past three months, my therapist has been asking me: "Zhou Yu, when you are taking care of others, have you ever considered that maybe perfection isn't the most important thing?" At the time, I would always nod rationally, but I didn't truly understand.

Now I do.

True care isn't about the perfect plans I've made. It’s not the monthly health check-up logs, the checklists for my daughter’s schoolwork, or the "step-by-step" roadmap for life. True care was choosing companionship over correction when she asked, "Why is my drawing different from what the teacher taught us?"

I used to think I was protecting this family—through planning, through control, and through flawless execution. But perhaps I have been missing the most important thing all along.

My wife is cooking in the kitchen, my parents are resting in their room, and my daughter is still drawing her bird. Sitting here, I feel a sense of peace I’ve never known before. It’s not because everything is under my control, but because I have finally let go of the obsession with control.

I understand now that truly showing up is more powerful than any perfect plan.

October 15, 2022 | Evening

My daughter is asleep. I’m sitting in the study, still not quite sure how to process these feelings.

I’ve been replaying what happened this afternoon over and over in my head. Xinyu asked me why I’ve been frowning so much lately, and why I’m sometimes so impatient when I talk to her. My instinct was to brush it off like I always do—tell her Daddy’s just a bit busy with work—but this time, I didn't. I don’t know why, I just suddenly felt so tired of keeping it all inside.

I told her, "Daddy has been feeling a bit sad lately, but it’s not your fault. I love you, and that will never change."

She just hugged me. She said, "I love you too, Daddy."

I sat in her room for a long time.

Later, Mom knocked on the door and asked if I was there. When I came out, she looked at me, but there were no words of reproach. I said to her, "Mom, let’s face this challenge together. It’s not about me taking care of you perfectly all by myself; it’s about us doing it together." She nodded and squeezed my hand.

Thinking about it now, those words don't sound like anything special. But for me, it felt like something finally broke.

All these years, I’ve been trying to be perfect. The perfect employee, the perfect husband, the perfect father, the perfect son. I thought that as long as I was reliable enough, as long as I just toughed it out, I could keep everything under control. But I see now that this need for control was itself a form of losing control.

I can’t carry all the weight. And I shouldn't have to carry it alone.

Maybe vulnerability isn't failure. Maybe saying "I'm tired" or "I need help" is actually a way to find a real connection.

My daughter’s hug, my mother’s hand on mine—those things have more power than all my previous attempts at perfection combined.

I still don’t know what to do next. The issues at work are still there, Mom’s blood sugar is still high, and Xinyu’s studies still need attention. But it feels different now. I’m no longer just white-knuckling it alone in the dark.

Li Xiaolin was right; I should go see a therapist. I’ve been resisting it, thinking it was a sign of weakness. But now I think, maybe it’s just another form of being honest.

I'll deal with tomorrow when it comes. That’s enough for tonight.

- **Colleague C**: "The sharing sessions are a good idea, but we need to make sure they don't turn into 'forced sharing'."

This feedback is invaluable—it shows that the plan truly resonates with people's real needs.

---

## Personal Reflections

To be honest, I was a bit nervous today. Not because I was afraid of rejection, but because I realized I’m doing something bigger—I’m not just fighting for change for myself; I’m trying to establish a new operational logic for the entire organization.

This is very different from how I used to work. Before, my goals were clear: finish the project, don't make mistakes, and don't be a burden to others. Now, I’m thinking: How can we make this organization more human? How can I help more people avoid the exhaustion I felt while trying to balance work and family?

While management requested revisions, their tone was supportive. This gives me confidence. The next two weeks will be busy—transforming ideas into an actionable plan. But this kind of "busy" feels different; it feels more meaningful.

I’ll talk to Li Xiaolin about the results tonight. She supported me from the very beginning, and now we finally have some initial progress.

October 6, 2023, 9:30 PM

Li Xiaolin’s words have been looping in my head.

"You’ve always been a newcomer; you just didn't realize it until now."

I’m sitting in the study. My daughter is asleep, and my parents have retired to their room. My wife is watching TV in the living room, but I know she’s waiting for my reaction. I need to be alone for a while.

I’ve been drowning in regret these past two days. The decision to transfer, that project idea about AI-optimized user experience, giving up everything I built over eight years in my old department... Every time I think about it, my chest tightens. I ask myself: Am I crazy? Why take a risk at this age?

But her words changed something.

I always felt I was "stable" and "mature" in my old department. But in reality, I was just repeating things. Repeating the same processes, the same decision-making styles, the same patterns of success. I mistook that repetition for stability. What she saw was that I’ve actually been doing what a newcomer does all along—I just didn't realize that’s what growth looks like.

So this feeling of being out of my element... maybe it’s not the "I can't do this" signal I thought it was. Maybe it’s the exact opposite.

I’m a bit scared right now. Scared of failing, scared of letting the team down, scared of discovering that I don't actually have that many ideas. But at the same time, there’s this strange sense of excitement. It’s like standing in an unfamiliar place—since I’m already here, I might as well take a good look around.

What happened with my mother last year changed my perspective on a lot of things. I realized I can’t fully control anything. All I can do is show up for the important moments and do my best where I can. I suppose this new project is the same. I don’t know how it will turn out, but I want to try.

Not driven by fear. But driven by a passion for life.

I need to go say thank you to Li Xiaolin. Not because she comforted me, but because she truly saw me.

When I go to the office tomorrow, I’m going to flesh out this idea one more time. Discomfort is a good sign. I have to remember that.

Sunday, July 3, 2022, 11:47 PM

I couldn't fall asleep today.

I’ve been lying in bed for two hours now. Xiaolin fell asleep long ago; her breathing is steady, just like always. I didn’t disturb her.

When I got back from the hospital this afternoon, Xinyu was crying in the living room. She was wearing that pink dress—we were supposed to go to the park. I remember it was the same last week. She asked me, "When will you have time, Dad?" and I said, "This weekend." This weekend, I said, "Next time."

Mom’s blood sugar levels are high again. The doctor said her medication needs adjusting. I need to keep a closer record of her daily diet and glucose levels so I can provide the doctor with accurate data. Xiaolin said, "I can go with Mom," but I refused. I said, "I understand Mom’s situation better."

It sounds reasonable. But now, I’m not sure if it’s true.

Xinyu said, "You always say next time, but next time never comes."

She didn’t look at me when she cried. Then she asked, "Is my body not important, then?"

I said, "Grandma’s health is very important."

She didn't say another word.

After returning from the hospital, I said something to Xiaolin that I’ve never said before: "There isn't enough time." She looked at me for a long while and didn't answer. I know what she wanted to say, but she didn't say it. Maybe she didn't know what to say either.

Now I’m wondering if I’m using taking care of Mom as an excuse to escape something. My career is a dead end, my life is crammed with schedules, my daughter needs me, and my parents need me. I can’t satisfy everyone at once. So I’ve started making choices. And the way I choose seems to be—constantly telling myself, "I understand the situation better," "I need to control this process."

But in reality?

I don’t know.

I need to apologize to Xinyu tomorrow. But I don’t know how to explain it. Maybe I can’t explain it at all.

Maybe I don’t even understand what I’m doing myself.

August 15, 2022, 7:30 PM

Mom’s blood sugar shot up to 8.5 again.

The doctor said her insulin dosage needs adjusting and suggested a week of hospital observation. My mind went a bit blank as I listened. Mom sat beside me looking calm, but I could feel her tension. On the way home, that number—8.5—kept looping in my head. It’s not sky-high, but it’s not low either. The doctor said if it stays like this...

I took the initiative and said, "I’ll do it." Monitoring the levels, giving the injections—I’ll handle everything. My wife gave me a look at the time, and later in the kitchen, she said, "This isn't just your responsibility." I know she’s right, but I snapped back at her anyway—the final call rests with me.

I regretted saying it the moment the words left my mouth, but I couldn't take them back.

While reading to Xinyu tonight, my mind was still stuck on Mom’s management plan. I made a spreadsheet to track daily glucose levels, diet, exercise, injection times, and dosages. I even looked up some resources, wondering if I should overhaul her eating habits. These are things I’m supposed to do.

But honestly, I’m terrified right now.

I’m scared that if I make one wrong move, the consequences will be irreversible. Mom is at that age; I can’t let anything go wrong. It’s a heavy feeling, like something is pressing down on my chest.

My wife asked if I’m overextending myself. I didn't answer. I didn't because I know the answer, but I also know I can’t let go. Who could do it better than me? I need to ensure everything is under control.

Except... it feels like nothing has been under my control lately.

That mistake at work is still rattling around in my head. My daughter’s studies. Mom’s health. There just isn't enough time. I’m carving myself up every day—one person at the office, another at home, and a third at the hospital. There isn't a single place where I can truly relax.

Maybe my wife is right. Maybe I really am taking on too much.

But what else can I do?

I can’t let go.

Xiaolin, I went to the hospital yesterday.

It was about that chest tightness I felt while running the other day. I couldn't quite get it off my mind, so I took the afternoon off to see a doctor. They did an EKG and some blood work, and the doctor said my heart is fine—it’s likely just anxiety.

I know you’ve noticed that I haven’t been myself lately. To be honest, I can’t quite put my finger on it either. There’s that issue with the project at work, my mom’s blood sugar still isn't under control, and Xinyu’s progress at school... I just feel like there’s never enough time, and I’m failing at everything.

The doctor suggested I try to relax or maybe consider counseling. But I still feel like it might just be the recent stress. What do you think? Am I overthinking things?

The truth is, I still get that tight feeling in my chest every now and then. Even though the doctor said I’m okay, I still find myself looking things up online... I know it’s probably not rational, but I just can't stop worrying.

I’m sorry for making you worry. I should have told you sooner.

## Morning Summary

At 10:00 AM, I hosted an internal knowledge-sharing session titled "Risk and Innovation in Project Management." I had volunteered for this presentation and was initially a bit nervous, but it went quite smoothly.

The core content focused on my reflections from working on AI projects over the past six months. I presented a key viewpoint: **True stability isn't about avoiding all risks; it’s about choosing which risks to take after clarifying your priorities.**

This sentence is the result of months of constant reflection. I used to try to keep everything under control, but I’ve realized now that total stability is often an illusion. Instead, it is through evaluated, planned experimentation with new things that we find our true footing.

## Feedback

I received quite a bit of feedback after the session. My supervisor pulled me aside afterward and said, "Your logic was very clear," which caught me a bit by surprise. Two junior engineers in the team also asked me about "how to apply fault-tolerant learning in projects." My advice to them was: first, define what constitutes a "tolerable failure" (such as technical iterations) versus what is "intolerable" (such as data security), and then give the team room to experiment within the former's boundaries.

## Implications for Future Work

This session made me realize that I need to communicate this philosophy more explicitly within my team management. It’s not enough for me to just practice it myself; I need to help the team understand why certain types of "instability" are actually necessary. In next week’s project meeting, I plan to formally integrate this framework into our AI project risk management process.

## Afternoon Schedule

I have two more meetings this afternoon: one with the technical group to discuss optimization plans for the data interface, and another with the product department to coordinate requirement changes.

October 16, 2023, past 11:00 PM

Just stepped out of the study. My daughter has been asleep for a while, and Li Xiaolin is asleep too. The house is very quiet.

I’ve been replaying today’s conversation with my boss over and over in my head.

He said, "What we need isn't a perfect plan, but ideas that are ready to iterate."

The moment I heard that, something hit home. It was as if he saw right through what I’ve been doing all these years—using perfection as a shield. As long as the plan is thorough enough and the execution precise enough, there will be no mistakes, no blame. It’s safe.

I said something to him that surprised even me: "I was always taught to do things the right way."

As I said it, I thought of my father. That phrase, "live life step-by-step," is something I internalized as a child. Do the right thing, follow the established path, don't take risks. I’ve lived by this logic for 39 years.

But then my boss said, "You’re already changing. I can see it."

...

I don't know why, but my eyes welled up when I heard that.

It wasn't because of the praise. It was because I felt seen.

I haven't discussed my decision to transfer roles deeply with Xiaolin. I was afraid she’d worry, afraid she’d think I was being reckless. I haven't even had a proper discussion with myself—I’ve been using logic to convince myself it’s the right move, but deep down, I’m still full of fear. What if I fail? What if the new project doesn't pan out? What if I’m actually not cut out for innovation?

I asked my therapist about these things, and she said, "Failure is information, not a verdict." I nodded at the time, but I didn't truly believe it.

But seeing that my boss notices my change makes me feel... maybe this change is real. Maybe I’m not just lying to myself.

I think this is what it feels like to be validated. Not because I achieved perfect results, but because someone saw me trying. Moving forward amidst the uncertainty.

I have to keep working on the AI project proposal tomorrow. There will be a lot of unknowns. I might make mistakes. But right now, I’m a little less afraid.

Or rather, I’ve learned how to walk alongside the fear.

I wonder what Dad would think if he knew I was changing.

Never mind. I don't need his approval anymore. What I need is... to keep going.

August 15, 2023. Past 11 PM.

I found it.

While sorting through my father’s bookshelf, an old photo fell out. It was them, back when they were young. My father was wearing that deep blue work uniform, and my mother was leaning against his shoulder, smiling so happily. I don’t remember when it was taken.

I just stared at it for a long, long time. Then, in a tiny voice, I whispered, "I'm sorry."

The sound was so faint I could barely hear it myself.

Sorry for what? Sorry for not taking better care of her? Sorry for not seeing how exhausted she was? Sorry that I couldn't change a single thing?

I don't know.

All these years, I tracked her blood sugar levels, took her for check-ups, and walked with her on weekends. I thought that was "care." I thought that as long as I was cautious enough, meticulous enough, I could protect her. I even gave myself a perfect score—look at me, how reliable, how responsible.

But she was still tired. In the places I couldn't see. While I was reading bedtime stories to my daughter, while I was staring at Excel sheets calculating investment returns, she was already so tired that she didn't want to live anymore.

I didn't see it. I just didn't.

I realize now that all my "care" was self-deception. I wasn't taking care of her; I was taking care of myself—nurturing the image of the "reliable son." I needed that identity. I needed someone to look after so that I wouldn't have to think about who I am, or if this is all my life is meant to be.

Now, even that identity is gone.

What have I lost? It’s not just my parents. I’ve lost the entire framework that sustained me for 39 years. I always believed that stability and control were everything. Follow the steps, do your duty, don't be a burden to others. I executed that philosophy flawlessly.

But did it change anything?

No.

It changed nothing. No matter how cautious I am, I cannot prevent tragedy. My philosophy of life is worthless in the face of reality.

...

Xinyu asked me why Grandma doesn't come over anymore. I told her Grandma went somewhere far away. She asked when she’s coming back. I said... I couldn't say it.

How can I tell her that some people just never come back? How can I make her understand that I, her father, can't even protect her from something as fundamental as this?

Xiaolin is downstairs; I can hear her crying. I should go down. But I’m sitting here, looking at this photo, unable to do anything.

I can't do anything at all.

Sunday, September 25, 2022 14:47

I’ve been sitting here in the underground parking lot for almost an hour now.

The morning meeting is still looping in my head. When my boss said, "Performance has been slipping lately," I didn't say a word. I just nodded mechanically. Like a puppet. Then I went back to my desk and stared at the screen for two hours without doing a single thing. My colleagues didn't notice—or they pretended not to. I’m not sure which is worse.

I have failed.

The thought is clear, so clear it feels almost surreal. I used to be the one who kept projects on track, the one my superiors could rely on. And now? Now I’m messing up basic code reviews. A rookie mistake. How could I let that happen?

But work isn't even the worst part.

Last night, while I was reading to Xinyu, she asked me for help with a math problem. It was so simple. But I snapped at her: "How can you not know how to do this?" Her eyes went dark instantly. I saw it, but I didn't apologize right away. Instead, I just kept rushing her to go to sleep. Afterwards, I stood outside her room for a long time, listening to her sob in the dark.

I’m a terrible father.

And then there’s Mom. Her check-up results came back last week, and her blood sugar is up again. I need to learn how to manage her diet, I need to take her for more tests, I need to track the data, I need to... need to what? I’m already tracking it. I record it every month. But clearly, it’s not enough.

I can’t do everything well at the same time.

That’s the core of the problem. I used to think I could. Schedules, checklists, plans—those things used to make me feel like everything was under control. But now I see it was all an illusion. I can’t be a good son, a good father, and a good employee all at once. Every role demands something from me, but my time and energy are finite. I start dropping the ball in one place, and then everything else starts to crumble.

The ultimate irony is that I can’t even quit. The mortgage, my daughter’s education fund, Mom’s medical bills... it’s all on me. I’m trapped. I can’t quit, but I can’t keep going like this either.

I’m stuck in a dead loop.

My wife has started to notice lately. She asked if work was getting to me. I just said, "It's nothing, I'm just a bit tired." I didn't tell her how I really felt. How could I? She’s already exhausted from her shifts at the hospital, on top of looking after our daughter and our parents. I don’t want to add to her burden.

So I just sit here, alone in the car, in the parking lot.

I don’t know what to do next.

 I really don't.

**The Perfection Trap — After my talk with Li Xiaolin**

Just now, sitting in the living room, she asked me what’s been going on lately. Instead of my usual "Nothing, just a bit tired from work," I actually stopped and thought about it.

**Three Missed Connections:**
- Deep conversations with her over the last 8 years. We talk about bills, our daughter’s grades, and our parents' checkups. We haven't truly talked about our fears or dreams.
- Quality time with Xinyu. I read her bedtime stories every night, but my mind is always on tomorrow’s project risks. She doesn't need a perfect father; she needs a father who is actually present.
- Honesty with my father. I’ve always lived life his way—following the rules, making no mistakes. But I’ve never asked him if he ever imagined a different life for himself.

**The Core Issue:**
Perfection ≠ Responsibility

I always thought that keeping everything under control, planning every detail, and never letting anyone down was how I took care of this family. But in reality, I was just hiding—avoiding uncertainty, and in turn, avoiding real connection.

**What I see now:**
- True companionship is about being present, not about perfect arrangements.
- I cannot protect everyone from every risk, nor should I try to.
- Letting go of the "Perfect Manager" obsession might actually make me a better husband, father, and son.

**Next Step:**
Not adding another plan, but learning how to breathe amidst the uncertainty.

The career transition has already begun. Now, I need to learn how to let that transition happen at home, too.

September 10, 2022, 6:00 PM

I’m sitting in the office; everyone else has left. I haven’t turned on the lights—I’m just sitting here in the dark.

What happened at the tutoring center this afternoon... I still haven’t processed it.

Xinyu was working on math problems, and I was waiting beside her. I watched her frown, struggling with a single problem for ten minutes only to get it wrong. In the next class over, there was a little boy whose pen was flying across the paper; he finished a whole page in no time. I just sat there, staring at that boy, and then... the tears started falling.

I didn’t make a sound. The tears just kept flowing. The tutor probably saw, but she was smart enough to pretend she didn't. I ran to the restroom and stayed there for ten minutes.

I’m 39 years old. I shouldn’t be crying at a tutoring center.

Yesterday, I emailed Xinyu’s teacher with a list of her mistakes, asking if they reflected deeper learning issues. The teacher replied today, saying she’s only in first grade and progress takes time. I looked at those words, but all I could think was—should I hire a one-on-one private tutor for her?

It’s ridiculous. I know it’s ridiculous. She’s a first-grader making normal progress, and here I am thinking about private tutors.

Mom’s blood sugar is high again. This month’s checkup results were even worse than last month’s. I need to do some research and learn how to help her manage it. I need to discuss her diet with her. I need to...

A bug popped up in the project last week that I missed during review. That’s rarely happened in the last eight years. I spent the whole afternoon trying to fix it. My colleagues didn’t say anything, but I know.

There isn't enough time.

I wake up at 6:30 every morning, go for a run, drop Xinyu off at school, go to work, pick her up, help her with homework, read her stories, go for walks with Dad, check Mom’s health data... These are all the right things to do. These are things I *should* be doing. But added together, I feel like I’ve been split into four pieces, and not one of them is whole.

At work, I’m no longer that "reliable" person. I’m not that person at home, either.

Li Xiaolin asked me what was wrong lately. I said nothing, just a bit tired from work. She looked at me with that look... she knew I was lying. I knew it too. But I didn’t know how to tell her. What would I even say? That I cried at a tutoring center? That I feel like I’m failing at everything?

My old way of handling project risks was to list the problems, analyze the causes, and create a plan. But this time... I can’t list them. Or rather, I’ve listed them, but there is no plan.

The problem is: I cannot be a good son, a good father, a good husband, and a good employee all at once.

And I always thought I could.

I always thought that as long as I worked hard enough, stayed rational enough, and planned well enough, I could do everything right. Since I was a kid, my father taught me to live life step-by-step. I’ve always done that. But now...

Now I’ve realized that you can follow every step and still fail.

I don’t know what to do.

# Counseling Session Summary

**Date**: October 8, 2022, Afternoon  
**Location**: Counseling Room

---

## Key Dialogue

**Counselor**: "You just said you’ve failed. Where exactly is the failure?"

**My Response**: Mistakes at work, my daughter falling behind in school, my mother’s blood sugar still being high, not taking care of anyone properly...

**Counselor’s Observation**: "I noticed you used 'I' a lot—I failed, I didn't do well, I can't handle it. But have you ever wondered what kind of expectations lie behind all those 'I's?"

---

## Core Insight (Important)

The counselor said something that made me stop in my tracks:

> "You are trying to perfectly fulfill every role simultaneously—the perfect employee, the perfect father, the perfect son, the perfect husband. This is inherently impossible. Your anxiety doesn't stem from how much pressure you're under, but from the fact that your expectations of yourself are fundamentally contradictory."

I had never thought about it that way.

I always thought the problem was that I wasn't working hard enough or wasn't capable enough. But actually... the problem is that the standards I've set for myself cannot be met at the same time.

---

## Cognitive Shifts Needed

- [ ] Stop measuring myself against "perfection"
- [ ] Acknowledge that there are things I cannot do, or cannot do perfectly
- [ ] Anxiety comes from **expectations**, not from **pressure**
- [ ] Letting go of a role is not the same as a dereliction of duty

---

## Food for Thought

What if I don't pursue perfection?  
What if I allow myself to be "not good enough" in certain areas?  
What would I lose? What would I gain?

---

**Note**: Continue this topic in the next session. This discovery is important.

# December 15, 2022, 9:30 PM

As I closed the study door, I could still hear the TV droning downstairs, and my mother’s snoring drifted in from the room next door. Li Xiaolin is probably still tidying up the kitchen. I’m sitting here, staring at this blank page, not knowing where to begin.

That one thing she said keeps circling in my head: "You can't control everything."

I know she’s right. I also know it sounds like the most ordinary cliché. But when she said it, I felt like something inside me had been pierced. I didn't even know how to respond, so I ran. I cut her off with, "It’s nothing, I’m just a bit tired from work." I knew exactly what I was doing, but I just couldn't help it.

It’s been three weeks since I’ve properly sat down to read a storybook with Xinyu. When she mentioned that, my first instinct was to defend myself—to say I’m trying to juggle so many things. But as the words came out, even I could hear how hollow they sounded. It was as if I were saying: I have more important things to do. And all the while, Xinyu was just sitting there on the sofa, waiting for me.

Mom’s blood sugar went up by another 0.3 this week. I’ve organized all her test results into an Excel sheet, color-coding every single indicator. The doctor said this slight fluctuation isn't cause for concern, but I’m still wondering if I should adjust her diet plan. I even looked up three research papers online. When Li Xiaolin watched me doing all this, she had a strange look in her eyes.

At work, the project is still slightly delayed. Not by much, but that’s rare for me. I’ve been working overtime to catch up, but my mind keeps drifting to Mom’s heart function check-up next week. I spaced out twice during meetings. My supervisor didn't say anything, but I could feel it.

I’ve been trying to fix all of this with more planning. More detailed schedules, more medical knowledge, more frequent checks. It’s as if I believe that if I’m just cautious and meticulous enough, I can prevent the worst from happening. But now, I’m starting to doubt that logic.

Maybe that’s the problem. I’ve always believed in living life by the book, believing that through rationality and planning, I could protect everything I hold dear. My father taught me to be this way, and I’ve always followed suit. But now, things are slipping beyond my plans. My mother’s health, Xinyu’s performance at school, the stagnation at work, and this indescribable sense of dread.

I can’t solve this with a spreadsheet.

Sitting here, I suddenly realize I might be wrong. Not about any specific decision, but about something more fundamental. Maybe I shouldn't be trying to control everything. Maybe that itself is the problem.

But if I let go of that, what do I have left?

I have to take Mom to the hospital tomorrow.

Xiaolin, I wanted to say I’m sorry.

When you nudged me at the dinner table earlier, I didn't react. I knew you were trying to comfort me, but

Xiaolin, I’ve been thinking about this for a long while, and I feel I need to be honest with you.

When you said earlier that I was trying to control everything, I was a bit angry at first. But later, while I was running downstairs, I realized you were right. I’ve been pretending that I can handle everything at once—work, taking care of my parents, being there for Xinyu, managing the finances—but the truth is, I reached my limit a long time ago. I just wouldn't admit it.

I’m not trying to make excuses for myself. I know I haven't been doing well enough. That feeling of being "fragmented" is something I’ve been feeling for the past two months, but I kept running away from it. Telling you that I was "just a bit tired from work" was a lie to you, and to myself.

What I’m most sorry for is how I reacted earlier. When you tried to hug me and I went stiff—that wasn't your fault. That was my own disappointment in myself turning into a defense mechanism. I realize now that doing that was hurting you, even though you were just trying to help me.

I’ve been thinking that maybe we need to sit down and have a real talk. Not a rushed one, but a serious look at our priorities. Or… I might actually need to talk to a professional. I know that sounds heavy, but I think I need help.

Do you have some time in the next few days? This weekend? I really want to talk things through with you.

I’m sorry. And thank you for not giving up on me.

Xiaolin, I’m sorry.

I shouldn’t have turned and walked away like that just now. I know you were trying to reach out to me, but I... I just couldn't. I wasn't pushing you away. It’s just that in that moment, I felt like I was falling apart, and I was afraid that if I let you touch me, I would completely break down.

I’m sitting in the study now, and I’ve been reading Mom’s diary for a while. I read that one sentence over and over again: "Yu'er is too tired." Every time I read it, it feels like a physical sting. I keep thinking about how all these years, I took her for her checkups every month, logged her blood sugar levels, and walked with her on weekends. I thought that was what it meant to take care of her. But I never saw that she was actually worrying about us, too. I was even busy planning investments, calculating education funds...

Am I a failure as a son?

I need to ask you this. You don't have to answer me right away. I just... I need to know if that's how you see me, too.

I have to go pick up Xinyu from school in a bit. I’ll pull myself together. But right now, I just need a little time.

Can you understand?

Xiaolin, I’ve been thinking for a long time before finally working up the courage to send this to you.

I heard everything you just said. I know I haven’t been... myself lately. The insomnia, the constant running, the endless searching for all that random stuff... you’ve seen it all. I wanted to defend myself, but the moment I turned away, I realized I had no excuse at all.

You’re right. I’m losing control.

I don’t want to be like this anymore. I’m going to see a therapist this Friday, just like you suggested. I know it’s nothing to be ashamed of, even though I still feel a bit... I don't know how to put it. But I trust you. I trust your judgment.

Thank you for not giving up on me. Truly.

I’m still exhausted, and I’m still scared. But at least... at least I know you’re there.

Goodnight.

# Friday, December 22, 2023 – Sunny

I didn't sleep well last night. It wasn't insomnia, but a strange kind of alertness—lying in bed, my mind kept replaying the image of my father reading the memoir.

I remember that moment so clearly. He was wearing his reading glasses, his hands shaking slightly, and he stopped when he reached that one page. It was the story I wrote about his younger days on the construction site, and that regret he’s mentioned so many times but never truly had recorded—missing that business trip to Beijing, losing that opportunity. I’d heard him tell it at least five times before, and every time, it went in one ear and out the other. But this time, I had actually written it down.

His eyes welled up. I’ve never seen him like that.

"I always thought I was just teaching you how to live a conventional life," he said. Then he paused for a long time. "I never imagined my story mattered so much to you."

I didn't know what to say. We just sat there, him still holding the notebook. Eventually, I patted him on the shoulder, and he patted my hand. No more words were needed.

Thinking back now, I finally understand what I’ve been doing these past few months. It wasn’t about changing him, or "fixing" anything. I was just seeing him. Truly seeing him. Seeing his stories, his choices, his regrets, and why he has always taught me to live "by the book." It wasn't a shackle; it was the greatest love he could give. He used stability to protect me, just as I am now using my presence to watch over him.

It sounds a bit sentimental, but I truly believe now: stability and change are not opposites. I’ve always been a stable person, and that’s fine. But stability doesn't mean stagnation. Stability is courage with a foundation. I can change when it comes to the things that matter—being there for my father, for my daughter, even rethinking my work. But I won't lose myself in the process of changing. That’s what I understand now.

Li Xiaolin asked me why I didn't sleep last night. I couldn't put it into words. Some things haven't quite settled yet. But I know this time is different. This isn't just another plan or another goal. This is real.

Tomorrow I still have to check Xinyu’s homework. This weekend, I might go for another walk with my father. Life is still the same routine. But I feel like I’ve changed.

I haven't become someone else; I’ve finally become myself.

# Project Initiation Record - AI E-commerce UX Optimization

**Initiation Date**: Oct 28, 2023  
**Project Duration**: 3 months (Nov - Jan)  
**Core Objective**: Optimize e-commerce recommendation paths via AI algorithms to increase conversion rates by 15%+

---

## Project Configuration
- **Core Team**: 2 Engineers (1 Frontend, 1 Backend)
- **My Role**: Project Manager + Product Design
- **Tech Stack**: Python + React + Existing Database Architecture
- **Budget**: Controlled within existing resources

---

## Core Innovations
This isn't just a simple stack of recommendation algorithms. The keys are:
1. **User Behavior Layering**: Differentiating between browsing, favoriting, and purchasing stages for targeted pushes.
2. **Real-time Feedback Mechanism**: Fine-tuning algorithm weights after every user action, rather than relying on periodic batch updates.
3. **Explainability**: The backend allows us to see *why* a specific product was recommended to a user, facilitating easier iteration.

The idea originated from a paper I read recently, but I've improved its practical utility.

---

## Risk Registry
- **Technical Risk**: Algorithm performance falls short of expectations → Requires POC validation by mid-November.
- **Resource Risk**: Learning curve for the two engineers → Requires intensive technical syncs from me during the first two weeks.
- **Business Risk**: Higher recommendation rates don't necessarily guarantee higher conversion → Need to define clear success metrics.
- **Timeline Risk**: Tight delivery schedule before the Lunar New Year → 2-week buffer reserved.

---

## Milestones
| Timeline | Goal | Acceptance Criteria |
|----------|------|---------------------|
| Nov 10 | Data Prep + Algo Prototype | Basic recommendation flow operational |
| Nov 30 | Canary Testing | Effectiveness validated via small-scale traffic |
| Dec 20 | Full Launch | Stable monitoring data |
| Jan 15 | Review & Iteration | Completed documentation and knowledge transfer |

---

## Personal Notes
When I heard the project was approved in the meeting room just now, I spaced out for a moment. It wasn't excitement; it was more... a sense of grounding? Or weight? A bit of both, I suppose.

In the past eight years, I’ve handled many projects and delivered them all on time, but few have made me feel, "This is what I actually want to do." This one is different. I’m not just executing a plan; I’m validating an idea.

But because of that, I feel a weight of responsibility. Not the "I can't fail" kind, but a sense of being "accountable to the idea itself." If it fails, I need to understand *why*, rather than making excuses.

I need to talk to Li Xiaolin tonight. Also, weekends will likely be busy over the next three months; I’ll need to rearrange my time with my father and daughter. Still... I think it will be worth it.

If Mom could see what I’m doing now, she’d probably say, "You still have to give it a try."

---

**Next Step**: Kickoff meeting with the two engineers on Monday

July 12, 2023, 6:00 AM

I didn’t get up.

The alarm went off. I heard it. But I didn’t move. I just lay there, watching the ceiling slowly brighten. Li Xiaolin has already left for the hospital; my father should be downstairs with Xinyu while she eats breakfast. Everything is as usual. Only I am not.

It’s been three years. I’ve kept up my morning runs for three years. Every day at 6:30, rain or shine. It was my last... what? Sense of order? Self-discipline? I don’t even know anymore.

What was it all for? To stay healthy? To live longer? To prove I still had some control? It all seems meaningless now.

My mother was very careful with her health, too. I remember her blood sugar levels perfectly. Every month’s check-up data is in my notebook. I paid more attention to them than she did. I thought that by doing this, I could...

Never mind.

I give up. I’m not running today. I don’t know about tomorrow, either. Those checklists, those deadlines, all those things I used to prove I was alive—they all suddenly seem absurd. I lie in bed, imagining myself just staying here forever. No one would say anything. Everything would still go on.

Li Xiaolin asked me again last night if something was wrong. I said no, just tired. That’s the truth. But not the whole truth. It’s not my body that’s tired; it’s... my whole self. My whole way of living.

I’ve never missed a day of work. I’ve never let anyone down. I’ve lived my life by the book for 39 years. I chose my major by the book, joined this company by the book, got married by the book, bought a house by the book, and took care of everyone by the book.

I did a good job. Everyone says I’m reliable.

But I failed to protect my mother. I didn’t even see how tired she was.

It’s light out now. I can hear Xinyu downstairs reciting her multiplication tables. Her voice is so crisp and clear. I should get up. I should go be with her. I should check her homework. I should...

I’m still lying here.

Maybe today, I just shouldn't.

April 10, 2022, 7:00 PM

I stood in the bathroom for half an hour while showering. I didn’t even wash; I just stood there, letting the hot water pour over me.

What happened this afternoon is still looping in my head. Xinyu was doing her math homework and got a simple addition problem wrong. Her homeroom teacher’s feedback last week said she needed more practice on the basics, and I was already feeling irritable when I saw that message. Then she made another mistake, and I couldn't help myself. I snapped at her, "How can you get such a simple question wrong?"

The light in her eyes just went out.

I regretted it instantly, but the words were already out. She didn’t cry; she just kept her head down, and that look on her face hurt me more than if she had cried. I sat with her and went through it again later, and I was much kinder, and she was cooperative, but the damage from that moment couldn't be undone.

Li Xiaolin saw it. She didn’t say anything, but I could feel her watching me.

So I hid in the bathroom.

What is the real problem?

It’s not that she got a math problem wrong. It’s normal for a first-grader to make mistakes. The problem is, why was I so mean?

I’m wondering if I’ve started failing in every area of my life.

At work, my boss mentioned in the annual review that management positions are limited. When I heard that, it felt like the assumptions I’ve held for the past eight years just shattered. I thought that if I just followed the path, I’d steadily move up. And the result? I’m still just that "reliable but unremarkable" guy.

And at home? My mom’s blood sugar has been high again lately. I take her for checkups every month and keep track of the data, but it feels like I’m not actually solving anything. She stays with us to help look after Xinyu, and I should be kinder to her, but sometimes I let my work frustrations bleed into my tone with her.

And now, I’m losing my temper with my daughter.

I’ve always felt like I was doing okay in all my roles—employee, son, father, husband. But now I realize that maybe I’m not actually doing well in any of them. I’ve just been "executing a plan" to deal with everything, and the moment the plan goes off track, I start to crumble.

That isn't truly "doing well." It’s just an illusion.

There isn’t enough time. There isn’t enough energy. My schedule is packed every day—running, working, spending time with my daughter, being there for my parents, managing the finances... I want to do every single thing right, but I’m realizing now that it’s simply impossible.

Maybe the problem isn’t the work itself, or the family itself. The problem is that I can’t be perfect at all of it at once. And yet, I’ve been trying to be.

The water in the shower has gone cold. I still haven’t found an answer.

I have to go to work tomorrow. I have to keep being a reliable person.

But today, I wasn't.

Lin, I spoke with my supervisor today about my working hours. He agreed to try adjusting the project schedule to give me more time with my daughter. Nothing too major—just pushing some meetings back and reducing the frequency of overtime on Wednesdays and Thursdays.

My therapist said this is a significant step. She told me it means I’m starting to believe that my life is worth redesigning. I was a bit stunned when I heard that, but thinking about it later, she’s right. Before, I was just passively enduring everything, feeling like work, family, and my parents were all mandatory burdens. Now, I’m actively making adjustments instead of constantly giving in.

It’s not some grand gesture, but it feels different.

I’m still reading with Xinyu tonight as usual. I want to see if I can keep up this pace.

How are things on your end?

Just got back from a run. My legs are still shaking.
