My wife asked me what was wrong. I said, "I'm tired." She looked at me, and I could feel her wanting to say something, but in the end, she stayed silent. I didn't have the energy to explain, either. My eyes must have looked hollow; I didn't even know what I was thinking.

Lately, I’ve been dwelling on a strange question. I’ve always considered myself a rational person—someone who makes plans, assesses risks, and follows the rules. This approach works well at work. But when it comes to my parents and their stubbornness, all my data and logic fail. They just won’t listen. I brought out the materials on blood sugar management and used every persuasive tactic I could think of, but in the end, my mother just shook her head.

At that moment, I realized that perhaps the entire system I’ve relied on for over thirty years is flawed.

So, this week at the office, I proposed that radical project plan. It’s completely unlike me. My colleagues were surprised. I was surprised myself. During the meeting, I kept saying things like "Why not try it?" and "Maybe we should take a little risk." My boss gave me a very strange look. I knew what he was thinking: *This isn't Zhou Yu.*

But I just wanted to prove to myself that I’m not entirely conservative. I don't have to follow the script.

But now, I’m starting to regret it. What if the plan fails? What if I mess everything up? I have a mortgage, a daughter, and parents. Can I really afford to take this risk?

The insomnia, the over-exercising, the aggressive work proposal, the obsession with my parents... none of this is like me. Or rather, these are all signs of me trying *not* to be myself.

I don't know what I'm doing.

Maybe I really am just too tired.

I have to take my mother for her check-up tomorrow. I’ve already prepared the new blood sugar management plan. But now, I’m a little afraid to see her. Afraid she’ll reject me again. Afraid of being powerless once more.

The sun is about to come up. My daughter has school today. I should try to get some sleep.

But I know I won't.

November 7, 2022, 11:47 PM

I was late today. For the first time in eight years.

Fifteen minutes. I was with Mom for her checkup; her blood sugar was high again. The doctor mentioned needing an angiogram, and my mind just went blank—I completely lost track of time. It wasn't until I was behind the wheel that I realized it was already 9:15.

Project review meeting. When I burst into the conference room, my supervisor was discussing risk assessment. Everyone glanced at me. I sat down, still catching my breath.

Then he asked for my thoughts on the new proposal. I should have provided a detailed technical evaluation and listed potential risks, just as I always do. But my mind was still on Mom’s results, on my dad saying, "She’s at this age, why put her through all this?" I was thinking about how I’d take time off if she needs to be hospitalized, and who would check my daughter’s homework...

I heard myself say: "The proposal is worth considering."

That was it. No risk assessment. No alternative plans. Like a complete novice.

My supervisor looked at me and asked, "Zhou Yu, are you okay?"

I said I was fine, just a bit tired today. But I knew he could tell. In that moment, a crack appeared in my professional image.

I’m sitting in the study now. My daughter has been asleep for a while. My wife is downstairs watching TV. I should go join her, but I don't know how to start the conversation.

I always thought that as long as I was cautious enough and planned well enough, I could protect everyone in this family. No mistakes at work, financial planning in place, regular checkups for my parents... everything under control. But today I realized that this "control" is actually an illusion.

Mom’s blood sugar can spike out of nowhere. I can be late. I can’t be a perfect project manager and a perfect son at the same time.

Maybe I’ve never truly been in control of anything.

Am I living for security, or am I actually living?

I don’t have an answer right now. Just this feeling of being trapped—unable to control the situation, yet unable to escape it.

I have to take Mom back to the hospital for a follow-up tomorrow. There’s a major project delivery the day after. My daughter needs to review for her math quiz this weekend.

I should sleep. I still have to get up at 6:30 for my run.

But I’m not sure if I can go for that run tomorrow like I usually do.

# Saturday, November 5, 2022, 14:30

Just got back from the hospital.

Mom’s test results are out. Her blood sugar levels are even higher than last time. The doctor said her medication needs adjusting and ordered a battery of further tests—fundus exam, kidney function, neuropathy screening. A long list of items. My hand shook slightly as I held the lab slips, my mind automatically running the numbers: how much time these tests would take, the approximate costs, who would look after my daughter during the recovery period...

Dad said something while standing next to me that I’m still playing back in my head: "You, you’re always trying to control everything."

I didn't respond right away. I just stood there, looking at his face, then down at the slips in my hand. Is he right? Or... am I just doing my duty?

After getting home at noon, I opened that Excel spreadsheet—*Mother’s Health Management Plan*. I spent two hours organizing it: medication schedules, dietary advice, follow-up cycles, emergency contacts. It’s very detailed. But looking at the grid now, a thought suddenly hit me: Will Mom actually follow this? Will Dad push her to? Or did I already know, even as I was making it, that it might be useless?

Then why did I do it?

The question makes me feel a bit panicked.

I’ve always felt that as long as I’m cautious and thorough enough, I can minimize any risk. It’s like that at work—I create detailed project plans, so projects rarely slip. It’s like that with my finances—I manage investments and insurance with Excel, so the family accounts are clear. But Mom’s blood sugar... no matter how much research I do or how much medical knowledge I cram, I can’t change her insulin secretion.

Data doesn't explain the problem. Statistical probabilities can't change reality.

What if Mom’s condition worsens? What if she needs to be hospitalized, or even... I don't dare think further. Is that my responsibility? Can I carry it? Or are some things simply beyond my control, and I have to learn to accept that?

But I don't know how to accept it.

This feeling is foreign to me. I’m used to having a plan, a strategy, a clear next step. Now, all I have is anxiety and a potentially useless Excel sheet.

Xiaolin asked me where I was this afternoon; I told her I was at the hospital for Mom’s checkup. She glanced at me but didn't press further. I know she sensed something, but I don’t want to explain. Or rather, I don’t know how.

Work starts again tomorrow. The project needs to keep moving. My daughter needs help with her homework. My parents need looking after.

What am I supposed to do?

December 18, 2022, 11:47 PM

Just took a shower, but my heart is still racing a bit.

Something went wrong during my run today. I was planning on doing 5k, but just past the 3k mark, things started feeling off—my heart was pounding, I couldn't catch my breath, and my face was drenched in sweat. I sat on a park bench for a long time, feeling a bit dazed. This has never happened before. I’ve been running for three years, and I’ve always been steady, with a clear rhythm and a fixed schedule. Today, it felt like something was crashing around inside me.

While sitting on that bench, I said something very strange to myself: "Maybe I’ve been too conservative." It startled me. Where did that even come from?

Lately, my mind has been cluttered with all this mess. Mom’s blood sugar, her heart check-up, Dad’s uncooperative attitude, Xinyu’s schoolwork... all these things feel like problems I have to solve, yet they seem unsolvable. I’ve done so much research and made so many plans, but reality just won't follow my lead. Mom is still resistant to going to the hospital, and Dad keeps insisting, "It’s fine, stop making a fuss." All I can do is repeat the same advice over and over, then wait for them to ignore it.

Work is a bit of a mess, too. The last project was delayed, and I’ve been obsessing over how to fix it. I’ve been working overtime these past few days trying to get back on track. But at the same time, my head is spinning with Mom’s test results, Xinyu’s study progress, next month’s family finances...

Running has always been my way to relax. Just me, no distractions, a time when I don't have to think so much. But today, even that was gone. My body was running, but my mind was still racing. Racing too fast.

Maybe I really have been too conservative. For all these years, I’ve just followed the plan, doing everything by the book, never taking risks. Choosing this company, getting married, buying the house... every decision was based on rational analysis. But what has rational analysis protected me from? Can it protect my family? Clearly not.

I’m a little scared right now. Scared of losing control over things. But then I realize—I never really had control over anything to begin with.

My wife has been watching me lately. She knows something is wrong, but I keep telling her, "It’s nothing, just a bit tired from work." It’s not exactly a lie, but it’s not the whole truth either. I just don’t know how to talk to her about all this.

I have to take Mom for her physical tomorrow. I’ve already made a list of questions for the doctor.

But right now, I just want to sit here and think about nothing at all.

# December 31, 2023, 8:00 PM

Li Xiaolin just asked me what my plans are for the New Year. I didn't open an Excel spreadsheet like I used to, nor did I list out a set of goals. I simply said, "Let’s just experience the new year together."

She looked a bit surprised. Perhaps she’s used to my old way of doing things—the detailed planning, the precise deadlines, the life where every step is calculated. But I really don't want that anymore.

Sitting here in the living room, Xinyu is watching a New Year's Eve cartoon, my parents are resting in their room, and my wife is in the kitchen tidying up. It’s a very ordinary scene, but I suddenly realized that this is all I want. Not a perfect plan, not seamless execution, just this—being here together.

A thought keeps circling in my head: I haven't changed; I’ve just become more real.

It sounds like I’m making excuses for myself, but when I really think it through, I realize it’s the truth. Those changes—no longer making a schedule every night, starting to tell Xinyu stories instead of just checking her grades, actually listening to my father when we go for walks—these don't mean I’ve become a different person. It’s just that I’ve finally stopped that excessive control, that way of using perfect planning to escape uncertainty.

I have always been a stable person. That hasn't changed. But I understand now that stability doesn't mean rigidity. Stability can encompass change. Stability can mean having the courage to take risks on things that truly matter, while remaining rational about the things that don't.

Back in November, I was excited, feeling like I’d suddenly had an epiphany. Now that December is ending, what I feel is more of a deep sense of contentment. Not because I’ve succeeded at anything, but because I’ve finally stopped trying so hard. I can let go of the role of the perfect project manager, and I can let go of the expectations of being the perfect father and the perfect son.

I just need to be here. Present. Real.

Tomorrow is the New Year. I don’t know what will happen, and I don’t want to plan it out in advance. The feeling is a bit foreign, but it’s also a bit like freedom.

I dug out a box of old photos today. I was originally looking for pictures of my daughter when she was little to make an album for her, but the further I dug, the further back I went, eventually stumbling upon photos of my parents in their youth.

There’s one of my father in his work uniform, standing on a construction site with a radiant smile. It was the 1990s, back when he was still an engineer at a state-owned enterprise. I’ve never heard him talk about what his dreams were, but looking at this photo, I can feel the passion he had then. And after that? After that, it was just "following the steps." A good job, a steady paycheck, providing for the family, and retiring on time. I always assumed that was the life he wanted.

But looking at this photo today, I suddenly wondered—was he truly content, or did he once have other ideas that he eventually let go of?

I’ve read so many stories to my daughter about protagonists who persist in chasing their dreams. But in reality, the people around me—including myself—all seem to be making a kind of silent compromise. Not that compromise is inherently bad. It’s just... I’m only now realizing that the "steady path" my father taught me might not have been everything he truly wanted to pass down.

Recent changes at work have shown me something different. I’m starting to believe that stability and passion aren't actually opposites. You can be both cautious and passionate. You can take care of your family while also doing things that make you feel alive.

I’ve decided to start a project. Not a work project, but a "Life Memoir." I want to record my parents' stories, my own story, and perhaps my daughter’s. Not to complete a task, but... to celebrate these lives themselves.

The idea gives me a strange sense of mission. Not a heavy burden of responsibility, but a warm, "want-to-do" kind of feeling.

Li Xiaolin asked me what I was doing, but I didn't explain right away. Some things are hard to articulate just yet. But I know this isn't an escape. This is me finding a new way to understand myself, my family, and this life I’ve been living.

Maybe my father was the same way back then—looking back at the path he’d traveled on some quiet night, and then making a choice. I’m only now beginning to understand that it might not have been a compromise, but a form of maturity.

I called Xiaoxiao. "I think I might have psychological issues," I said, and then I hung up.

The way my boss looked at me... I know what he was thinking. *Zhou Yu, you should just stay in your comfort zone.*

I’m tired. Truly exhausted.

It’s not a physical tiredness; it’s—I don’t know how to describe it. It’s like I’m being torn apart between two places. On one side is everything I’ve always believed in (stability, planning, rationality); on the other is this voice that’s starting to doubt (maybe none of this can save anyone).

If I really do have psychological issues, who can I even take care of? Xinyu needs me to study with her, my mother needs me to take her for checkups, and Xiaoxiao needs me to be the "reliable one." But right now, I can’t even take care of myself.

It’s quiet outside. Everyone in the house is asleep. I’m sitting in the living room, looking at those spreadsheets I organized in Excel—investment plans, insurance schemes, the kid’s education fund. These things used to give me peace of mind. Now, they just feel like a mockery.

The doctor said I’m fine.

But I know something is wrong.

Where exactly is the problem?

# MEMO

**Date: August 10, 2023, 4:45 PM**

**Key Observation - Father**

I’ve been thinking about what Father said last night. He said, "Living is too exhausting." I asked him if it was a physical exhaustion or a mental one, but he didn't answer; he just stared out the window.

I need to record a few things; maybe it will help make sense of what’s happening:

**Symptom Checklist:**
- Significant loss of appetite over the last two weeks; barely eats breakfast.
- Walking distance is shortening; started saying his legs felt weak the day before yesterday.
- Irregular sleep patterns; sometimes wakes up as early as 3:00 AM.
- Becoming less talkative; answers everything with a "yeah" or "it’s nothing."
- His eyes look hollow when he watches his granddaughter, not like before.

**To-Do:**
- Accompany him to the hospital on Monday for a comprehensive check-up, not just blood pressure and blood sugar.
- Ask him if he’s had any chest tightness or dizziness lately.
- Try chatting with him tonight to see if I can understand what’s on his mind.
- Maybe I should ask Mom...

Wait.

Mom is gone.

I forgot while I was writing this. It only hit me halfway through.

**—**

This list is useless.

I know it’s useless. The "exhaustion" Father is talking about isn't a problem that can be solved with a checklist. Everything I’ve done for 39 years—recording, planning, preventing—none of it changed anything. Mom still left. I took her for check-ups every month, I logged her blood sugar levels; I thought I was taking care of her, but I didn't see how tired she really was.

Now Father is saying he’s tired, too.

I don’t know how to help him. I don’t know how to tell my daughter why Grandma isn't coming back. I don’t know how to tell Li Xiaolin how I feel right now, because I can’t even articulate it to myself.

I want to delete this memo. But I don’t know what deleting it would change, either.

**—**

15 minutes until I get off work. I should go home. My daughter is waiting for me to read her a story.

I’m not sure if I can sit by her side and read for 20 minutes like I usually do tonight. I’m not sure if my voice will sound strange.

Maybe I should tell Li Xiaolin and let her take over for me. But then again, I don’t want to. I don’t want to give up this time.

It’s the only thing I have left that I can still do right.

Xiaolin, there’s something I need to tell you.

I was too harsh with Xinyu today. She got a math problem wrong, and I raised my voice, asking her, "How do you still not get something this simple?" — you know I’m rarely like that. She burst into tears immediately.

I’m still thinking about it now. It’s not just because she cried, but because of how I acted. That wasn't me.

Work has been a bit chaotic with the recent adjustments, and I might be a little distracted. But that’s really no excuse to take it out on a child. She’s only in first grade. At the parent-teacher meeting, the teacher mentioned she needed more practice with the basics, and I just started... I can’t quite explain it. It’s like I found something in her that I felt I had to "fix."

I hid in the study later, but I actually just stood in the bathroom for a long time. You didn't see me.

I know this sounds contradictory. I’ve always felt like I was doing okay, that I could handle everything at home. But today I realized that maybe I’m under more pressure than I thought. By pushing her, I was actually pushing myself.

I really regret it.

I’m going to have a proper talk with Xinyu tomorrow, and my attitude will be different. But I wanted to tell you this first. I don’t know how to describe it, it’s just... I feel like something is shifting, and I haven’t quite figured it out yet.

Sorry for bringing all this up so late.

Xiaolin, are you asleep?

I can't sleep. I’ve been lying here for over two hours.

I was just thinking about Mom’s heart exam results again... I keep replaying what the doctor said in my head. He said, "It’s okay for now, but we need to monitor it regularly." I know that’s just standard doctor-speak, but I just...

Never mind, maybe I’m overthinking it.

It just feels like everything has been going downhill lately. Mom’s blood sugar is still out of control, Dad isn't cooperating with his medication, I’ve fallen behind on that project at work, and Xinyu’s teacher mentioned last time that her fundamentals need more work... It feels like I’m failing at everything.

Do you think I’m being too sensitive?

I know you have to get up early tomorrow. No need to reply. It’s just... when I can’t sleep, my mind starts racing. It feels like everything is going wrong at the same time, and I can't fix any of it.

Sometimes I wonder if I could have kept everything under control if I had just been more careful or planned things out better. But now I’m realizing... it feels like nothing is actually within my control.

What do you think?

# Re-evaluating Discomfort

**Date**: October 6, 2023, 21:30  
**Source**: Conversation with Li Xiaolin tonight

---

## Old Mindset
Discomfort = I made the wrong decision  
(Transferring might have been a mistake; I should go back to my comfort zone)

## New Mindset
Discomfort = I am learning something new  
Discomfort = A signal of growth

---

## Conversation Snippet

**Me**: "I’ve felt a bit out of place in the new department this week. It feels like I’m starting everything from scratch."

**Li Xiaolin**: "That just proves you’re doing something truly different. If you adapted instantly, you’d just be doing the same old thing in a new location. Discomfort is exactly what breakthrough feels like."

**My Reaction**: A five-second silence. Then it hit me—she’s right.

---

## Why This Matters

In the past, I would interpret "discomfort" as a "warning sign" and trigger my risk-aversion mode.  
Now, I need to interpret it as a "learning signal."

This isn't just wordplay. It fundamentally changes how I view anxiety.

---

## When to Revisit This

Next time I feel career anxiety → Read this.  
Next time I want to quit a new project → Read this.  
Next time I feel "incompetent" → Read this.

**Reminder to self**: Discomfort isn't a precursor to failure; it’s a precursor to growth.

---

## Addendum

My mom once said that the most interesting moments in teaching are when students ask questions she hasn't considered. She feels "uncomfortable" then, too. But she says those are her most lucid moments.

I am in one of those moments right now.

When I woke up this morning, Xiaoyu was still asleep. I glanced at my phone—6:32 AM.

I had planned to go for a 5km run, but for some reason, I just wasn't feeling it today. The weather wasn't great either, all gray and misty. So, I just took a stroll along Qiuju Path. I didn't follow the set route or keep track of the time.

By the time I finished my shower, you were already up. While we were having breakfast in the kitchen, it suddenly hit me: I didn't stick to the plan today, yet I actually felt better for it.

It sounds a bit strange, doesn't it? I've always felt that if you plan something, you have to see it through, otherwise it’s just a waste of time. But today... I just let go a little. Maybe it was because that "must-complete" pressure wasn't there.

I'm starting to wonder if I've been keeping things too tight. You’ve told me before that I’m too serious, that I insist on doing everything by the book. Back then, I’d always defend myself, saying that’s the only way to get things done right. But now... I’m not so sure.

What do you think? Am I overthinking this?

Xiaolin, I wanted to share something with you.

Today’s workshop is over. To be honest, I was a bit nervous before it started, wondering if I could keep up with the pace of this new project. Remember how I used to always say, "It’s too risky"?

But something unexpected happened today. The design team proposed a "rapid iteration, learn-as-you-go" approach. My gut reaction was still that it felt too risky. However, instead of shooting it down right away, I took a moment to think and suggested a middle ground—we could iterate and test non-core features quickly, but we had to maintain strict control over the core issues.

The team actually agreed, and they even gave me a round of applause.

Sitting there, it suddenly clicked for me. Maybe stability and growth aren't actually opposites. I don't have to abandon my principles, but I don't have to be paralyzed by "perfection" either.

I know this might sound a bit sudden. But I feel different now—like I’ve found a way forward that isn't just a binary choice between "sticking strictly to the plan" or "taking massive risks."

What do you think? Am I overthinking this?

June 29, 2023, around 3:00 PM

Sitting in Mother’s room. Her diary is still in my hands.

I don’t know what to do.

"Yu'er is too tired; I can tell."

Just that one sentence. Written on May 15th. I remember going to the hospital that day in a rush, carrying a physical exam report, discussing blood sugar levels with her. I didn't even sit down; I just stood by the bed, showing her the data on my phone. She said back then, "It’s fine, don't come so often if you’re busy with work." I thought she was just trying to reassure me.

Now I understand. She was aching for me.

And me—I always thought I was "taking care" of her. I remembered every monthly check-up clearly. That Excel spreadsheet has all her blood sugar levels, blood pressure readings, and doctor’s advice. I even showed it to Li Xiaolin once, saying, "Look how meticulously I’m managing Mother’s health." I was proud of it.

It seems so ironic now.

What use was all that data? Could I stop her stroke by keeping records? Could I protect her with a perfect health file?

I’m realizing that every time I went to the hospital, I was in a hurry. Truly. I would bring a checklist—what to check, what to ask, what to record. Like managing a project. But I never really sat with her. I never really listened to her. I didn't even notice the look in her eyes when she said "you're too tired"... that look of... what? Now I’ll never know.

I am a failure of a son.

That sentence keeps looping in my head. I think over and over: what if I had stayed a little longer that day? What if I had asked "how are you feeling lately" instead of "what’s the blood sugar reading"? What if I could have seen her exhaustion instead of just those damn numbers?

My whole life—I’ve always believed that through planning, through control, through perfect execution, I could protect the people I care about. That I could keep everything under control. But it was all a lie. I lied to myself.

When Mother was in the ICU, I was even thinking about project deadlines. I was wondering whether I should take time off. And then I hated myself. I hated my rationality. I hated that I couldn't stop thinking about that goddamn work.

Now she’s gone. All the medical forms are useless. All the records are useless.

I didn't see how tired Mother was. My presence, my perfect "care," might have actually been a burden to her.

I don't know how to forgive myself.

# Saturday, Nov 18

**Unplanned Weekend: A Butterfly Moment with Xinyu**

Around 10:30 AM, in the community garden. Xinyu spotted an orange butterfly resting on a Chinese rose. I was about to urge her to come back and check her homework, but I stopped myself. I knelt down and watched it with her for about ten minutes.

She asked me why butterflies drink nectar. Instead of giving her the answer directly, I asked her what she thought. She said, "Maybe it's hungry." We just sat there as she made up a story—about how the butterfly had to fly home to bring food to its babies.

No photos, no keeping track of time. Just watching.

Later, when my wife called us in for lunch, Xinyu was a bit reluctant to leave. I told her the butterfly would come back and we’d watch it again next weekend. She nodded.

—

This weekend didn't go according to plan. No pre-prepared homework checklists, no schedules. But these ten minutes felt more vivid than many of my previously "well-arranged" quality time sessions.

It’s a strange feeling. Like I finally gave myself permission to just stop.

Marking this down. A reminder to myself that this actually happened.

My transfer application was officially approved today. Deciding to head home early this afternoon isn't about escaping; it’s about confirming priorities. My mother taught me to be grounded, and my father taught me to follow the rules, but I understand now—true groundedness is choosing to be present even amidst uncertainty. The new project will be busy, but "busy" can no longer be an excuse for being absent. Changing storytime from 20 minutes to 30 minutes seems like a mere 10-minute difference, but those 10 minutes represent my choice.

I’m taking a gamble. I hope I’m right.

September 11, 2023. Past 11 PM.

The lights in the consulting room felt a bit cold. I was sitting on that gray sofa—I’ve lost track of how many times now. My therapist said something, a very simple sentence.

"You’ve been through a lot. This is normal."

That was it.

I don’t know why, but in that moment, my throat just tightened. It felt like something was suddenly stuck there, and no matter what, I couldn’t swallow it down. I kept my head low, and my fingers started to shake—actually shaking, I could feel it. My shoulders were trembling too, very slightly, but I could feel it. For seven or eight minutes, I didn't say a word. I just sat there.

Then I heard myself say, in a voice that sounded like a stranger’s: "I don’t know why I should keep living."

The voice didn’t sound like mine. It was raspy. Like it was being squeezed out from somewhere very deep.

I didn't cry. I was just... broken. Like a machine that suddenly had a part come loose and started leaking air.

My therapist didn't speak immediately. I was grateful for that.

Now I’m sitting at home. My daughter has been asleep for a while. I read her a storybook earlier, and she asked me why I didn't seem very happy today. I told her Daddy was just a little tired. She went to sleep obediently. I don’t know how tired I am. It’s not a physical tiredness. It’s something else.

Li Xiaolin is in the bedroom. Lately, she’s been looking at me often, with something in her eyes I can’t quite read. Maybe it’s worry. Maybe it’s disappointment. I don’t want her to see me like this.

My parents are in this house, too. They’re in the room next door. I can hear them snoring. I’m supposed to be taking care of them. I’m supposed to be taking care of everyone.

But as I sit here now, I can’t think of a single reason.

It’s not that I can’t think of one. It’s that... I really can’t find one.

My notebook is on the desk. It’s full of lists. What to do tomorrow. What to do the day after. My daughter’s schoolwork. My mother’s check-up. Project deadlines. The mortgage. Insurance. The education fund.

Everything is there. Very neat. Very orderly.

But I don’t know why I keep filling out these lists.

I really don’t know.

# Flexible Work Arrangement - Preliminary Proposal

**Date**: October 20, 2022 (Morning)
**Status**: Just returned from the supervisor's office

---

## Key Takeaways

The supervisor mentioned the possibility of "flexible work arrangements" today. I need to carefully think through what this means and how to implement it.

---

## Potential Flexible Arrangement Options

1. **Remote Work Days**
   - Proposal: Remote on Mondays and Wednesdays to reduce commute time.
   - Advantage: Can handle family emergencies (mother’s check-ups, daughter’s school runs).
   - To Confirm: Will this affect team coordination? Do our project management tools support this?

2. **Flexible Schedule**
   - Proposal: Core hours from 10:00 to 16:00, with flexible start and end times.
   - Benefits: Can drop my daughter off at school in the morning and attend my mother’s medical appointments in the evening.
   - To Confirm: Will this impact salary? How will performance evaluation standards be adjusted?

3. **Project Reallocation**
   - Can current projects be partially transferred to other PMs?
   - Or can we reprioritize project urgency?
   - Need to discuss specific feasibility with the supervisor.

---

## Priority Re-evaluation Framework

| Item | Frequency | Priority | Current Conflict |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mother’s monthly check-up | Once a month | High | Frequent leave requests |
| Checking daughter’s homework | Every weekend | High | Weekend overtime |
| Work deadlines | Variable | High | Frequent high stress |
| Walking with father | Every weekend | Medium | Tight schedule |
| Reading to daughter | 20 mins every night | Medium | Often too late/too tired |

**Potential Adjustments**:
- Mother’s check-up: Fix for Tuesday afternoons; request 1-2 hours off in advance.
- Daughter’s tutoring: Wednesday evenings or Saturday mornings (to avoid work conflicts).
- Work deadlines: Communicate with the team earlier to build in buffer time.

---

## Specific Questions for Further Discussion with Supervisor

- [ ] Is there a trial period for the flexible arrangement? How long?
- [ ] Will salary be affected?
- [ ] Will performance evaluation criteria change?
- [ ] What are the specific requirements for remote work?
- [ ] Impact assessment on project delivery?
- [ ] Is a written agreement required?

---

## Impact on Family Financial Planning

**Calculations Needed**:
- If salary is adjusted, impact on monthly expenses.
- Whether pressure from the second mortgage will increase.
- Whether the daughter’s education fund plan needs adjustment.
- Potential increase in mother’s medical expenses (need latest check-up results).

**Current Assumption**: Salary remains unchanged; time flexibility is exchanged for improved work efficiency.

---

## Action List

**This Week**:
- [ ] Organize the specific timelines for current projects.
- [ ] List specific dates of leave taken for family reasons over the past 3 months.
- [ ] Calculate commute time saved by working remotely.
- [ ] Discuss the feasibility of this plan with my wife.

**Next Week**:
- [ ] Formally discuss the above questions with the supervisor.
- [ ] Prepare a written version of the specific flexible work proposal.
- [ ] Communicate potential changes with team members.

---

## Personal Reflections (Brief Notes)

This is an opportunity. It might not be the perfect solution, but it’s a step in the right direction.

Need to evaluate this calmly—don't be overly optimistic, but don't give up either.

Build the framework first, then move forward step by step.

# October 28, 2023, 11:00 PM

The project has been officially greenlit.

During this morning's meeting, the Director announced the official launch of the AI optimization plan. Two engineers have been assigned with a three-month timeline. It’s smaller in scale than I initially expected, but that actually brings me a sense of relief. With just two people, I can clearly see the impact of every single decision.

I thought I’d be ecstatic. After all, this is the first thing to truly materialize since my transfer. But sitting in that conference room listening to the announcement, what I felt wasn't the rush of "getting it done." It was something heavier—a sense of responsibility, or perhaps, commitment. This project is real now; it’s no longer just an idea in my head. It will either succeed or fail, and that outcome will say something about me.

I’m a little scared.

But there’s also a... different kind of fulfillment. Not the "I finished a task" kind, but the "I am doing something I actually want to do" kind. There is a world of difference between the two.

I left work early this afternoon. I didn't stay late at the office, nor did I grab drinks with colleagues to celebrate. I just suddenly wanted to go home.

While reading stories to Xinyu, I extended our time from 20 minutes to 30. She fell asleep halfway through, her head resting on my shoulder. I just sat there, motionless. I thought to myself—if I were still in my old position, I’d probably still be in a meeting right now. And I would have missed this moment.

Is this the life I want? What does the life I want even look like?

I don’t know. But I know I don’t want to miss these moments anymore.

Dad has been getting a bit winded lately when he walks; Mom says his blood pressure is high again. I didn't tell him about the project launch. While walking with him over the weekend, I remembered how he taught me from a young age to "follow the steps." Back then, I thought that was the safest way. Now... I’m still afraid, but I’m starting to understand that following a plan and taking risks might not be opposites. Perhaps true stability is simply knowing what you are doing, why you are doing it, and being willing to live with the consequences.

That is what I am doing now.

Rationally, I accepted this idea long ago. But today, when the project became official, when I chose to go home early instead of grinding away, when I watched my daughter fall asleep on my shoulder—that was when my body and heart truly began to believe this was right.

I’m still scared. But alongside the fear, I feel... alive.

It’s a strange feeling.

Xiaolin, aren't you up yet?

I just got back from a run. Today was a bit strange—while I was running, I couldn't stop thinking about that UX case study from the workshop. My brain was automatically brainstorming solutions. Usually, running is my time to zone out, but today I was stuck on this. I even had to stop halfway through to jot down a few notes so I wouldn't forget them.

To be honest, I'm not quite used to being like this. But I don't hate it either.

Oh, and I didn't follow my usual routine of checking the financial data just now. Instead, I went straight to helping Xinyu get breakfast ready. She has to bring a craft project to school today. I figured that data doesn't necessarily have to be reviewed on a Friday; maybe I've been a bit too rigid with my processes.

Do you think this is okay? I mean, these changes I'm going through. Sometimes I'm not even sure if I'm making progress or just letting my guard down.

I’ve been thinking a lot about what you told me the other day—the part about how "companionship isn't about perfect planning." I think I'm finally starting to understand what you meant.

The workshop starts next Monday. I'm a bit excited, but also a little nervous. I wonder what I'll learn.

Thank you for always being there.

Xiaolin, I’m still at the office, but I wanted to tell you something.

The news about the project approval just came out. I sat at my desk and stared at it for a long time. It wasn’t excitement, exactly; it was more like… how do I put this? My eyes felt a bit stinging. I didn't join the colleagues in celebrating; instead, I just really wanted to go home.

When you said "I'm proud of you," I finally realized what this means to me. It’s not just about the project succeeding; it’s that… I did something the old me wouldn't have dared to do. It might sound a bit dramatic, but this really is the first time I’ve looked at such massive uncertainty and said, "I’m going to try." In the past, I would have calculated every risk and chosen the safest path. But this time was different.

I’m a little scared now, to be honest. I don’t know if this project will work out, but at least I’m no longer that person who just follows a script. When Mom passed away, I think I finally understood—perfect plans don’t change anything; what matters is whether you actually step up and do it.

I’ve decided to leave work early. The project details can wait until tomorrow. I want to spend more time with Xinyu—I’ll read her stories for 30 minutes today, not 20. And I want to go for a walk with Dad.

Your support… it really means everything. I’m still figuring out this "new me," and sometimes I feel unsure. But having you there gives me the courage to keep going.

I should be home around 5:30.

June 2, 2022, past 10 PM

I closed the study door. My daughter is asleep; my wife is downstairs watching TV.

I got my mother’s physical exam report this afternoon. Her blood sugar levels have climbed again. The doctor’s words are stuck in my head: "This is a trend, not a temporary fluctuation."

Xiaolin suggested maybe she just hadn't been watching her diet lately, but I corrected her, insisting it wasn't temporary. She glanced at me and didn't say anything else. I know my tone wasn't great, but I couldn't help it.

I’ve been thinking about this all afternoon. I sat in the living room without even changing my clothes, numbers spinning in my head—blood sugar levels, frequency of check-ups, potential medication costs, long-term care expenses. I even opened Excel, thinking I’d re-plan the family’s medical reserve fund. But in the end, I didn't change a thing; I just closed it.

While Mom was downstairs cooking, she asked if I wanted some fruit. I said no. Her voice sounded the same as usual, but now, any sound she makes just reminds me of that report.

To be honest, I don’t know exactly what I’m anxious about. On the surface, it’s Mom’s blood sugar, but it feels like more than that. When the doctor said "This is a trend," I suddenly thought about my job. Eight years—from junior engineer to project manager, and then I just stopped there. Maybe that’s a trend too, rather than the temporary plateau I’ve always assumed it was.

I’ve always felt like I could control everything—project timelines, family finances, my parents' health, my daughter’s upbringing. Make a plan, list the tasks, execute step-by-step. But Mom’s report feels like a reminder: some things simply cannot be controlled. They follow their own logic, no matter how cautious you are.

I’ve been worried about Xinyu’s schoolwork lately, too. Last week, her teacher said she needed to strengthen her fundamentals, so I started checking her homework constantly and hovering over her progress. Xiaolin says I’m overdoing it, but I can’t let go. It’s as if I believe that if I’m diligent enough, I can guarantee she won't fail. But that’s probably just an illusion.

Tomorrow, I’m taking Mom back to the hospital for a follow-up. I need to record the new data and discuss a diet plan with the doctor. I need to do what a good son should do.

But sitting here now, looking at these words, I don’t know what I’m actually afraid of.

Maybe it’s just the fear of losing control.

# June 15, 2023, 18:45

The doctor just said a lot of things. I wrote them down:
- Stroke, right middle cerebral artery occlusion.
- Admitted to the ICU; vital signs are currently relatively stable.
- Prognosis is difficult to determine; a 72-hour observation period is required.
- Family members should be mentally prepared.

I typed it into my phone’s notes app, word for word. I didn’t cry while the doctor was speaking, nor did I ask many questions. I just listened and recorded. When the nurse asked if I understood, I said yes.

When I called Xiaolin, my voice sounded calm to my own ears. I said, "It’s a stroke. She’s in the ICU. The doctor said it’s hard to tell the prognosis right now." That was it. She asked how I was doing; I said I was fine and needed to handle these things first. She wanted to say more, but I told her I had to hang up.

I’m sitting in the corridor outside the ICU now. There’s another family member crying across from me. I’m watching her and feeling strange—why can’t I cry?

I remember taking Mom for her checkup just last month. Her blood sugar was a bit high again, so I noted it down in a spreadsheet, planning to adjust her diet this month. I thought that as long as I kept these indicators under control, I could prevent any complications. I even calculated a five-year health management plan in Excel.

That plan is useless now.

I keep wondering, what if I had noticed a sign earlier? What if I had taken her for one more walk last week? What if I...

No. There’s no point thinking like that. That’s how strokes are; no "what ifs" can change anything.

The insurance card is in my bag. I haven't sent the email to request leave yet. Dad doesn't know. Xinyu is still at school.

I need to make a list. I need to know what to do next. That way, I won't have to keep thinking about questions that have no answers.

The doctor said the next 72 hours are critical. 72 hours. I can wait here for 72 hours. I can record the results of every test. I can...

Actually, I can’t do anything.

I always thought I was protecting them. Regular checkups, tracking data, financial planning, managing everything. I thought that was enough. I thought that if I were just cautious enough, perfect enough, I could stop bad things from happening.

I realize now that it was all self-deception.

The doctor is coming back in. I’m going to ask about Mom’s current status.

# Monday, December 18, 2023, Evening

I just got back from the office, and my chest still feels a bit tight. Not the painful kind of tightness, but... how should I put it? It’s like something is slowly surfacing.

After the morning meeting ended, I sat alone in my office for a long time. Looking at that proposal—the "Caregiver Support Program"—it felt a bit surreal. Did I really write this? When did I become the kind of person who makes these sorts of suggestions to management?

I could feel the atmosphere in the room shift the moment I said it: "Competitiveness isn't about overtime hours; it's about mental health and life satisfaction." After the words left my mouth, even I was a bit stunned. It didn't sound like me. Or rather, it *is* me, but a version of myself I’ve never truly voiced before.

Two colleagues came to see me this afternoon, saying they had similar thoughts. One mentioned his mother hasn't been well lately, but he never dared bring it up with the bosses. The other said her daughter’s school often has events that she’s never been able to attend. They just talked to me like that, as if they were looking for some kind of validation. I realized then that maybe I’m not the only one struggling.

Li Xiaolin asked me how my day was. I wanted to tell her everything, but I didn't know where to start. In the end, I just said, "It was okay, quite productive." She gave me a look—that look where she seems to know I’m holding something back, yet chooses to trust that I’ll tell her when I’m ready.

While reading a bedtime story to Xinyu, she asked me why I seemed a bit different today. Even a six-year-old can sense these things. I told her, "Daddy did something a little brave today." She asked what it was, and I said, "I changed some people's minds." She nodded, as if she understood perfectly.

I’m sitting in the study now; it’s already dark outside. I’m thinking that this proposal isn't just about workplace policies. It’s actually saying: we can be different. We can take care of ourselves and others at the same time. That’s not weakness; it’s being real.

I used to think stability meant staying still. Now I understand that true stability is knowing who you are and having the courage to change what isn't right.

Maybe this is what I’ve been doing these past few months. Not running away from myself, but finally finding who I really am.

# March 25, 2022, 11:45 PM

I shut the study door. My daughter is asleep, my wife is watching a TV drama, and my parents have retired for the night.

Usually, at this hour, I’d be checking tomorrow’s schedule or flipping through financial statements. But tonight, I did something I’ve never done before—I created a new Excel sheet and password-protected it.

I named it "Risk Scenario Analysis."

In reality, I was just calculating one thing: if I quit my job right now, how long could my family survive?

When the numbers finally appeared before me, I stared at them for a long time. 18 months.

18 months. It’s a concrete number, something I can count on my fingers. It’s not "maybe one day" or "we’ll see later." It’s the sum of our current accounts, the mortgage, the education fund, and my parents' medical expenses—the exact amount of time we can stay afloat.

18 months.

To be honest, seeing that number triggered two simultaneous emotions. One was... how should I put it? It felt like seeing an escape hatch. If I truly can’t take it anymore, if staying at this company means being ground down by that "reliable but unexceptional" label, I have an 18-month buffer. 18 months to find a new job, to do something different, to...

But the other feeling was stronger. It was fear.

What does 18 months mean? It means my life isn't nearly as stable as I thought. it means everything I’ve meticulously planned—the mortgage, the education fund, my parents' healthcare, my daughter’s tutoring—is built on a foundation that could snap at any moment. It means I cannot afford a single mistake.

My boss's words are still ringing in my ears: "Management positions are limited."

Eight years. For eight years, I’ve never missed a late-night shift, and my projects have never been delayed. Everything I’ve done was to prove I’m trustworthy. And the result? Reliable but unexceptional. Like a well-oiled machine—useful, but easily replaceable.

I didn't tell Xiaolin about this spreadsheet. She’s been trying to talk to me about my career lately, asking if I’m truly exhausted or if I’ve thought about changing jobs. I always just say, "It’s nothing, just a bit tired from work." That look in her eyes... she knows I’m avoiding the subject.

But how can I tell her? How can I say, "I’m calculating how long we can survive if I quit"? It sounds too... too desperate. Besides, she trusts my judgment; she trusts that I can take care of this family. I can’t let her know that I’m actually doubting whether I really can.

My mother’s recent check-up showed some fluctuations in her data again. My daughter is falling behind in math this semester; her teacher says she needs extra help with the basics. New variables are cropping up in my projects at work. Any of these things would be fine on their own, but when they happen all at once... it’s like a system starting to show multiple points of failure.

I always thought that as long as I followed the plan, as long as I was cautious and rational enough, I could keep all risks under control. But now I realize that some things are beyond control.

18 months.

I locked that number behind a password. No one knows what I’m thinking. I continue to wake up on time, continue to go for my runs, continue to read bedtime stories to my daughter, and continue to take walks with my parents. Everything looks the same as always.

But I know something has changed.

I no longer believe in the word "stability."

July 6, 2023, 9:30 AM

I found it. Mom’s diary.

It was in her cabinet, tucked behind a yellowed ledger. I was actually looking for her medical records—her blood sugar check-up is due this July—and then I saw that sentence.

"Yu-er is too tired."

Just those five words. Written in pencil, the handwriting a bit shaky. Dated November of last year.

I read it over and over again. Every time I did, it felt like a sharp prick.

I should have noticed. I accompanied her to her check-ups every month; I logged her blood sugar, her blood pressure, even every pill she took into an Excel sheet. I thought I was being so meticulous. But I never saw that she was tired. I never even asked her, "Mom, are you okay?"—not in a way that truly meant it.

What was I busy with back then? Planning to buy a second apartment. Discussing education fund allocations with Li Xiaolin. Checking Xinyu’s homework, worrying that her first-grade foundation wasn't solid enough.

I was taking care of everyone. But I wasn't taking care of anyone.

My wife tried to lean in and hold me yesterday. I pulled away. I couldn't let her touch me. Because if she did, I would break down, and then I’d have to comfort her, telling her, "It’s okay, we have to be strong." I just don't have the strength anymore.

I always thought I was reliable. I had plans for everything; I never worked late into the night; my projects were never delayed. I kept everything under control. I thought that was how you protected your family.

But Mom was still tired. And Mom is still gone.

No matter how cautious I am, it doesn't change a thing.

The things I’ve believed in for 39 years—following the rules, doing your job, not being a burden to others—they are worthless in the face of reality.

What if I had noticed sooner? What if I had cared less about investments and more about her? What if...

But there are no more "what ifs."

I don't even know how to explain it to Xinyu. She asks me why Grandma isn't here anymore. I tell her, "Grandma got sick." She asks, "Will Grandma get better?"

I can't say it.

I can't say it.

# December 15, 2023, 7:00 PM

Xinyu took the initiative to drag me to her room to draw today. I originally planned to sit beside her and offer some "suggestions"—you know, that typical fatherly guidance. But then she asked me, "Dad, do you want to hear the story from my dream?"

I put down my phone.

She drew some wavy lines on the paper and said it was a river where the fish could sing. She told me that in her dream, she was walking along the river and heard her mother’s voice, but when she turned around, her mother had turned into a bird and flown away. She spoke with such earnestness; I noticed how her eyes squint whenever she’s deep in thought.

I didn't say "This is a great drawing" or "You should add more detail." I just sat there and listened. I asked her what songs the fish sang, and why her mother turned into a bird.

Later, she said, "Thank you for letting me be part of your world."

No, wait. I was the one who said it. I said, "Thank you for letting me be part of your world."

She looked a bit confused, but she smiled.

Sitting there, something suddenly clicked. For years, I’ve been obsessed with how to "take good care" of her—checking grades, planning her curriculum, making sure her foundation is solid. But today was the first time I truly *saw* her. Not as a subject to be educated, but as a person with her own dreams and thoughts.

I’ve always been afraid that I’m not doing enough. Afraid that her foundation isn't strong enough, afraid that I’m not giving her enough companionship. But maybe real companionship isn't any of those things. Maybe it’s just sitting there, listening to her dream stories, without judging, without trying to improve anything... just being there.

I thought back to a conversation I had with my therapist. She told me that care isn't about perfect planning. I understood the words back then, but today, I actually felt it.

Xinyu fell asleep during bedtime story time. I’m sitting by her bed now, looking at her face. She’s still smiling.

I don’t know if this is the "change" I’ve been looking for. But it feels different. It feels real.

Xiaolin, I wanted to tell you something.

Just now, while I was reading a story to Xinyu, she asked me, "Daddy, do you not want to read to me anymore?" I was a bit stunned for a moment.

I realized that I’d been thinking about Mom’s test results all evening and wasn't really there with her at all. She was talking about the little bunny in the story, but my head was completely filled with blood sugar levels and those lab reports.

I know it’s not a huge deal, but... I feel so guilty. I feel like I can’t get anything right lately—I was even late for work today, and now I’m distracted in front of my daughter. I always thought I could handle all of this, but right now, it feels like everything is falling apart.

Do you think... I’m still a decent father?

I’m not looking for empty comfort. I just... I need to know that I haven't completely messed everything up.

# November 1, 2023, 6:30 AM

It’s still dark out. I’m sitting on the living room sofa with a cup of coffee in my hand; it’s still warm.

What happened yesterday morning keeps looping in my head. For three years, I’ve never broken that fixed 5km route. Qiuju Lane, the east side of the lake, then back to the start. Precise as a computer program. But yesterday, I just... didn't follow it. The weather was nice, and as I ran, I drifted into an unfamiliar neighborhood. Eventually, I just slowed down and walked, like I was out for a stroll.

Li Xiaolin asked me why I got back so late. I told her I didn't stick to the plan today, and that it actually felt better.

She gave me a look but didn't press further. I could sense what she was thinking, though—this isn't like me.

And she’s right. This isn't like me.

I’ve always believed that persistence is the ultimate virtue. Sticking to the plan, sticking to the route, sticking to the things that have already been proven. That’s how you avoid mistakes. But yesterday, on that unfamiliar path, it suddenly hit me—over these past three years, have I been running, or just completing a task?

It’s been nearly a month since I transferred to the new project. Every day, I’m thinking about how to prove myself, how to avoid making mistakes. But maybe... maybe that’s the problem. I’m approaching something new with the same old mindset. The same caution, the same "perfect" plan.

But this new project is different. No one is giving me the standard answers. I have to feel my way through, to learn by trial and error. The feeling makes me both excited and terrified.

My therapist said last time that I need to learn to stay calm amidst uncertainty. I didn't really take it to heart then. But yesterday morning, on that stretch of Qiuju Lane I’d never run before, I think I started to get it. Deviating from the plan isn't a loss of control, nor is it a failure. It’s just... another possibility.

I’m just not used to it yet.

Last night, while Li Xiaolin was checking Xinyu’s homework, I watched from the side. Our daughter was complaining that the math problems were too hard. I remembered what her teacher said last time—"she needs more foundational practice." I started getting anxious again, wondering if I should sign her up for a tutoring class. But Xiaolin pulled my sleeve, signaling me not to interfere. She said, let the kid try it on her own.

I sat there and said nothing. But I was thinking that maybe my "perfect" planning for Xinyu is the exact same thing as my obsession with that 5km route.

It’s 6:30. Time for my run. But today, I don’t know where I’ll end up.

Maybe that’s okay.

I’m sitting in the study. My tablet is still on, displaying the blood sugar management plan I spent two hours putting together. The screen has dimmed, but I haven't turned it off.

I don’t know how to write this.

I still haven’t processed what happened this afternoon. Or rather, I see it all too clearly—so clearly that it scares me.

I went to my parents with the data to try and persuade them. A 40% increase in risk over five years, a downward trend in cardiac function indicators—I even made a comparison chart: last year’s values versus this year’s, with warnings highlighted in red. My voice grew more urgent, sounding less like I was helping them and more like I was lecturing them. I could hear my tone shifting from "according to medical guidelines" to "you must do this."

My mother cried. She said, "The way you talk, it’s like we’re already dying."

It didn't register at the time. I was still thinking about the next data point, still trying to figure out how to make them understand the research paper I’d found. Then my father stood up, said, "Stop trying to educate us all the time," and walked out of the living room.

I sat there, holding my tablet, and suddenly realized—what am I doing?

I’ve always felt like I was "protecting" them. I track every one of my mother’s check-up results, I research treatment options, I build plans. For years, this method has worked for me. I use it at work, for family finances, even for raising my daughter. As long as I’m meticulous enough and rational enough, I can keep things under control.

But today, my rationality failed me in front of them.

Worse, I realized that I’m afraid. I’m afraid of my mother’s blood sugar levels, afraid of the information I found on complications, afraid that I can’t stop something from happening just by creating a perfect plan. So I became more and more desperate, more and more determined to use data to convince them—as if their agreement with my plan would give me back a sense of control.

But they refused. They weren't refusing treatment; they were refusing my approach. They were refusing my "education."

I’m sitting here now, and I don’t know who I am anymore. I always thought of myself as the rational, reliable one, the person who protects loved ones through planning. But maybe I’m just using rationality to mask my own fear.

Li Xiaolin asked me what was wrong. I said nothing, just that I was a bit tired from work.

I was lying to her. I’m also lying to myself.

I don’t know what to do next.

I talked about how, for the past eight years, I’ve been chasing perfection—perfect project plans, perfect risk management, perfect family arrangements. I even laid it out for her like a list, as if I were doing a project post-mortem. She listened intently, without interrupting.

Then I said the hardest part: I’ve missed out on so much. Deep conversations with her, because I was always busy solving problems instead of truly listening. Quality time with Xinyu, because I turned "being there" into checking homework and recording grades. Honest talks with my father, because I was always just executing the "step-by-step" approach he taught me, rather than telling him what I actually thought.

Xiaolin didn't blame me. Instead, she took my hand. She said, "You’ve realized it now, so there’s still time."

We decided that starting next month, we’ll set aside one weekend every month where we plan absolutely nothing. No checking off lists, no organizing bills, no reviewing schoolwork. Just... being together. I don’t know how strange that sounds, but to me, it feels like learning a new language.

When I was reading to Xinyu tonight, I didn't spend the time mentally assessing her level of comprehension like I usually do. I just sat there, listening to her laughter and watching her point at the pictures in the book. That was enough.

Perfection itself is a trap. I’m only just realizing that now.

As I said that, I saw some colleagues nodding, and it was like a light went on in some of their eyes.

What surprised me most was that after the presentation, several colleagues told me, "You said exactly what I’ve been thinking but was too afraid to say." Even the manager from the neighboring department came over to ask if we could discuss this approach to project management together. I never imagined I could do something like this—to be honest, I used to be the kind of person who just "did my job and didn't cause trouble," never one to proactively share my ideas.

Sitting here now, I realize that without your patience during that time, and without you constantly encouraging me to think about my true professional needs, I’d probably still be stuck in the same spot. You know what? The feedback from those colleagues didn't feel like a sense of achievement so much as a confirmation—a confirmation that I wasn't taking a gamble; I was simply responding to my own authentic thoughts.

June 15, 2023. Late night, 23:47.

I’ve been sitting on this hospital corridor bench for three hours now.

Mom is still in the ICU. The doctor said they need to observe her for 72 hours. I don’t know what that means—or rather, I know, but I refuse to admit it.

Earlier, when I was shutting down my computer at the office, my hands were steady. When I told my colleagues, "My mother has been hospitalized, I need to take leave," my voice was calm. I even remembered to email my supervisor about the project handover. What the hell is wrong with me? Looking back now, what was I even doing?

On the drive to the hospital, my head was full of checklists. Who to notify, how long to take off, how to file the insurance, what to do about the funeral arrangements... it was like I was conducting a project risk assessment. My mother might be dying, and I’m thinking about procedures. What kind of person am I?

Dad is at home now, staying with Xinyu while she sleeps. Xiaoxiao said she’d take the day off to come to the hospital tomorrow. I haven’t cried. Not once.

This isn't right.

I always thought I was protecting them. Accompanying Mom to her checkups every month, logging her blood sugar data, planning the finances, managing everything... I thought if I did all that, I could stop bad things from happening. I thought if I were cautious enough, thorough enough, I could keep life running according to plan.

And now?

One stroke, one phone call, and it all falls apart. My "perfect management" is useless. I can’t change her blood vessels with an Excel spreadsheet. I can’t stop Death with a project plan. I can’t control anything.

Everything I’ve done for the past 39 years... it was all based on a lie. I thought I was in control.

The doctor said, "We need to see how the recovery progresses." I can’t take it in. I’m thinking, if she... if she really... then what should I do? I’ve never thought about that question. Or rather, I’ve been deliberately avoiding it.

Sitting here, I suddenly feel like a stranger to myself.

Where is that "reliable Zhou Yu"? The one who never works overtime but never makes a mistake? The one who keeps everything organized and in order?

Where is he?

He can’t do a single thing right now.

thought
# Thursday, October 20, 2022, 11:47 PM

I just couldn't sleep again. I’ve been lying in bed for over two hours, replaying today’s conversation with my boss over and over in my head.

During the meeting this afternoon, I finally stopped hiding. When he asked why the recent project was delayed, I was going to make up an excuse like I usually do—system issues, staffing changes, that sort of thing. But this time, I didn't. I told the truth: I haven't been in a good headspace lately, there are things going on at home, and my focus at work has definitely slipped.

As I said it, that familiar tightness in my chest returned. I expected him to frown, to say something like "that’s no excuse." But he didn't. He just nodded and said, "I appreciate your honesty." Then he said everyone goes through hard times and asked if I needed some flexibility with my work arrangements.

I was a bit stunned.

It wasn't the reaction I expected. I’ve always assumed the workplace is a certain way—that you have to be perfect, you have to be reliable, and the moment you show vulnerability, you’re looked down upon. But his reaction was completely different. He even mentioned the possibility of remote work or adjusting my project load.

Lying here now, I’m wondering—have I been misreading people all along? Is my attitude toward my wife, toward my colleagues, all built on this assumption that "I have to carry it all alone"?

My mother is in the next room; her blood sugar was a bit high again today. My daughter’s tutoring classes continue this week. The projects at work still need to be done. None of that has changed. But it feels like something has.

The act of coming clean felt like putting down a heavy stone. I don't know what happens next, or if this will really change anything. But at least in that moment, I wasn't carrying it by myself.

Maybe I don't have to be perfect.

The thought is foreign, but not entirely unfamiliar.

Tomorrow will go on as usual. But maybe... maybe I can try a different way.

# Caregiver Support Initiative - Formal Proposal

**Date**: Monday, December 18, 2023
**Time**: 14:30 - Immediately following the proposal meeting
**Location**: Company Conference Room / Personal Calendar

---

## 📌 Event Log

**[Formal Proposal Submission]**
- Time: 2023-12-18, 14:00-14:25
- Participants: 3 Management Members + Me
- Status: Submitted; positive reception

I need to mark this clearly on my calendar. Not to show off, but because this truly changed something.

Before this, I would never have proactively proposed something like this to management. I would do my projects, finish my tasks, and go home. But this time is different. I realized that if I’m the only one making these changes, the impact is far too small.

The core of the proposal is simple—establishing a support system for employees with family caregiving responsibilities: flexible hours, mental health resources, and regular "Caregiver Sharing Circles." It’s not a perk; it’s recognition. it’s saying: you don’t have to choose between your career and your family.

---

## 📅 Key Next Steps (Follow-up Required)

**[Management Decision]**
- Expected: By December 25, 2023
- Note: They said they would provide feedback by year-end. I believe they will give it serious consideration.

**[First Caregiver Sharing Circle]**
- Planned Date: Monday, January 15, 2024
- Time: 18:00-19:00
- Location: TBD (Likely virtual)
- Target Attendance: 8-10 people
- My Role: Facilitator + Sharing my story

I’ve thought about this date repeatedly. It can’t be too early (need to give management time to decide), but it can’t be too late (momentum might fade). Mid-January is just right.

**[First Follow-up Meeting]**
- Date: January 8, 2024
- Purpose: Confirm management's decision and discuss implementation details.

---

## 💭 Reflections in the Moment

Sitting in that conference room, I felt calm. It wasn't nervousness or excitement. I just knew exactly what I was saying and why I was saying it.

They asked a few questions—costs, implementation difficulty, whether it would affect project timelines. I answered them all. I used data, logic, and the trust I’ve built over the past eight years.

But the most important moment was the last question. The Director asked me: "Zhou Yu, where did this idea come from?"

I paused for a moment. I could have talked about work-life balance or employee retention rates. But I told the truth: "I have a six-year-old daughter, a mother with high blood sugar, and many colleagues just like me. We are all trying to do our jobs well while taking care of our families. If the company can understand this, rather than making us feel guilty or forced to choose between the two, we will perform even better."

They nodded. No one disagreed.

---

## 🔄 To-Do List

- [ ] Draft the agenda framework for the Sharing Circle
- [ ] Reach out to colleagues who might be interested (maintain privacy)
- [ ] Prepare my own sharing content (regarding the changes over this past year)
- [ ] Discuss the plan with Li Xiaolin—she should know
- [ ] Proactively follow up if there is no feedback by December 25

---

## 📝 Personal Notes

This isn't something the "old me" would have done. The old me would have thought: this is too risky, it might look unprofessional, it might hurt my promotion chances.

But now I understand. True professionalism isn't about hiding your humanity; it’s about honestly admitting that we are all people with families, responsibilities, and limitations. A good organization doesn't ask employees to ignore these things; it acknowledges them and finds a way forward together.

The proposal might be rejected. But at least I tried. At least I spoke up.

This feeling, more than any promotion, feels like real progress.

March 20, 2022. Night.

I asked my dad a strange question while we were out for a walk today.

I asked him if he ever regretted spending his entire life working at a state-owned enterprise.

He paused for a long time, then said, "Following the path is the safest way." Just that one sentence.

I don’t know why I asked. Maybe it’s something I’ve been mulling over for months, and it just reached a breaking point and slipped out. During my last annual review, my supervisor said, "Management positions are limited," and those words have been stuck in my throat ever since. I always thought that if I followed the rules, I’d get a steady promotion. But it feels like there’s something wrong with the rules themselves.

Dad’s answer made me feel uneasy. Not because he said anything wrong, but because I suddenly realized—I’ve been repeating his life. He taught me to "live by the book," and I did exactly that. I chose a practical major in college, joined a stable company after graduation, and haven't changed jobs in eight years. "Reliable but unremarkable." That evaluation feels like looking in a mirror.

I am a steady provider. I support my family, my parents, and my daughter. But lately, I feel these roles are crushing against each other. My mom’s blood sugar issues, Xinyu’s adjustment to school, the pressure at work—it’s all happening at once. I keep adding items to my checklist, but there’s never enough time. Sometimes, while reading a bedtime story to Xinyu, my mind is still on work, or I’m mentally calculating next month’s mortgage.

Am I living my own life? Or am I just repeating a pre-verified template?

Dad said "following the path is the safest way." But what does "safe" even mean? It means no surprises, but no joy either. It means everything goes according to plan, even if the plan itself might be wrong.

I’m feeling a bit tired right now. Not physically, but a different kind of exhaustion. It’s the fatigue of realizing you might be trapped, without knowing how to break free.

Maybe I can’t keep living the way my father did. But I don’t know any other way to live.

# October 22, 2023, 7:00 PM

Just got back from my walk. My father is sitting on the sofa watching the news; I poured him a glass of water.

To be honest, nothing special happened today. We just took a lap around the building and talked about my internal job transfer. But sitting here in the study now, I still feel... how should I put it? Like there’s something heavy in my chest.

My father said something that I keep chewing over. He said, "Being nervous just means you’re doing something that actually matters to you."

It sounds simple, but for me, it felt like a door opening. I grew up hearing him talk about "following the steps"—those words are practically etched into my bones. Choosing a major, finding a job, getting married, buying a house; that’s how I’ve lived my life. Stable, controlled, error-free. That has always been my underlying logic.

But today, he changed his tune. He said being nervous is a good thing.

When I asked him why, he paused for a long time. Then he said that when he was young, there were times he wanted to break through, too, but in the end... he didn't. He said it very quietly, but I caught something in his voice. A sense of regret, I suppose.

Sitting there, I suddenly realized that I am doing something he might have wanted to do but didn't dare to. It’s a strange feeling. It’s not a burden, but more like... being handed something.

I’ve overthought this transfer a thousand times. My therapist helped me work through it, and my wife supports me. Rationally, I know that stability and growth aren't opposites. But physically, I still wake up in the middle of the night thinking, "What if I fail?"

Maybe it’s exactly this nervousness that proves it’s real. It’s not an escape, not an impulse—it’s because I truly care.

During our walk today, I realized what I was actually doing. I wasn't just checking off a task; I was spending time with an old man. Not because "weekend walk" was marked on my calendar, but because I wanted to hear him speak. I can’t say exactly when this shift happened, but it just feels different now.

From "I have to" to "I want to."

I’m still a bit scared. The identity shift feels foreign—moving from "the reliable project manager" to "the innovator willing to explore." These two identities haven't fully merged yet. I still subconsciously want to control risks and avoid failure. But my father’s words made me realize that perhaps this nervousness is itself a form of proof.

Proof that I am living, not just following a plan.

Onward tomorrow. The first meeting for the new project is on Tuesday. I’ll still be nervous, but the feeling is different now.

September 22, 2023, 8:30 PM

Li Xiaolin is already asleep. Xinyu is in bed, too; I just finished reading her the final chapter of *The Little Prince*. My parents are in the living room watching TV—the volume is turned way down, like a low hum in the background.

I’m sitting in the study with my laptop open, but I’m not writing about project deadlines. I’m thinking about what I said to Xiaolin earlier.

I always thought I was protecting them—by controlling everything, by planning every detail, by ensuring nothing unexpected could happen. But today I realized that wasn’t protection. It was fear. I was using control to escape that feeling of powerlessness.

Xiaolin said, "You don’t need to do everything right. You just need to be with us."

When I heard that, I almost burst into tears. I didn't cry at the office, and I didn't cry while running, but when she said those words, I felt something inside me shatter—not a painful break, but more like something rigid finally loosening its grip.

I thanked her. I told her I was grateful she didn't give up on me during the hardest times. She said, "Well, you didn't give up on us either."

I never realized that admitting vulnerability would require so much courage. More courage than it takes to pretend to be strong.

Now I’m thinking maybe I should write something for my father. Not a formal eulogy, but... his story. He taught me to live "step by step," but he never told me what lay beyond that routine. Maybe I should record his life—not for any grand purpose, but just so it doesn't disappear.

The thought brings a new sensation. It’s not that feeling of emptiness. It’s... a bit like responsibility, but different. More like... companionship.

I still don’t know what comes next. Work is still the same. The mortgage is still there. Xinyu’s homework still needs checking. But I know now that none of those are the most important things. What matters most is that while these things are happening, I am truly here. Not just pretending to be, but actually present.

My energy is still low. But it feels different.

I’ll still wake up at 6:30 tomorrow morning. I’ll still go for a run. But I think, maybe while I’m running, I’ll allow myself to stop and look at the sky for a while.

# Morning Run · Changing Routes

**Type**: Personal Time

**Notes**:

I didn't stick to the usual Binjiang Road route today.

I stopped at the five-kilometer mark to look at that new real estate development under construction. There were renderings posted on the hoarding; the glass curtain wall design is quite interesting—it uses a layered, terraced approach so it doesn't feel too overbearing. It reminded me of a documentary I watched about urban renewal in Hangzhou; the design philosophy is very similar.

I stood there for over ten minutes. I wouldn't have done that before.

Afterward, I went to Dawn Cafe, ordered an Americano, and took a seat by the window. I jotted down a few lines in my notebook:

*"Stability is not the same as being static. Changing routes is not a loss of control. Seeing something new, stopping, and reflecting—that is also a form of planning."*

A bit sentimental, perhaps. But that’s truly how I felt.

I got home at 7:20, still in time to read to Xinyu. She told me about school today and said the house she drew in art class was posted in the hallway. I saw the photo; she did a good job.

My wife asked where I went this morning and why I was back later than usual. I told her I took a different path and looked at a construction site. She just smiled and didn't ask further.
