I always thought I was just too weak, and that’s why I was so anxious, why I lost my temper with Xinyu, and why I made mistakes at work. But the truth is... I’ve been demanding a perfection that doesn't exist. I set my expectations for myself far too high.

I know I’ve been avoiding you these past few days whenever you tried to talk to me. I kept saying, "It’s nothing, I’m just tired." But in reality, I was rejecting everything about myself. I felt like I was failing in every area of my life.

The feeling of being understood today... it was different. The therapist didn't tell me what I "should" do; she just said that it’s okay to accept imperfection. I don’t need to be perfect in everything.

I know this might sound like I’m making excuses for myself. But I want you to know what I’ve realized. I need to learn how to let go of some things. Not to abandon my responsibilities, but... to accept that I have limits.

I’m sorry for how I’ve been acting toward you and Xinyu lately. I’m going to take things slow and adjust.

I need your patience.

# Work Log - November 8, 2023

**Project**: E-commerce Platform AI-Assisted Decision Module  
**Participants**: Design Department, Core Technical Team  
**Location**: Company Meeting Room  
**Time**: 9:00 AM – 12:00 PM

---

## Workshop Summary

Today, we held a requirements review workshop for the AI project. The discussion primarily focused on the "rapid iteration, fault-tolerant learning" development strategy proposed by the Product Design Department.

**Core Issues**:
- The Design Department suggested adopting an MVP model, allowing for rapid trial and error on non-core features.
- The Technical Team expressed concerns regarding the tight timeline.
- There is a need to balance the speed of innovation with quality control.

**My Position**:
My initial reaction was that this pace carries significant risk. However, after thorough discussion, I proposed a compromise:

1.  **Core Layer** (Data Security, Transaction Logic): Maintain a zero-defect standard; all changes must undergo a full review process.
2.  **Functional Layer** (Recommendation Algorithms, UI/UX): Allow for rapid iteration and establish a fault-tolerant learning mechanism, with weekly reviews.
3.  **Monitoring Mechanism**: Set up alerts for key metrics, with immediate rollbacks in case of anomalies.

**Team Feedback**:
- The Design Department approved of this framework, noting that it preserves room for innovation while maintaining a safety baseline.
- The Technical Team found it acceptable but requested clear criteria for rollbacks.
- Senior management tentatively agreed with this approach and suggested I refine the proposal into an execution document.

**Next Steps**:
1. Complete the detailed tiered quality standards document by this Friday.
2. Coordinate with the Tech Lead on specific monitoring metrics and alert thresholds.
3. Submit the final proposal to senior management for approval next Monday.

**Personal Reflections**:
This solution isn't the "absolute stability" I initially wanted, nor is it "blindly aggressive." I feel we’ve found an acceptable balance. The key will be strict adherence to the tiered standards during execution—there can be no compromise there.

---

**Recorded by**: Zhou Yu  
**Date/Time**: 2023-11-08 14:00

Walking out of the meeting room after today’s project management sharing session, I still felt a bit of a surreal buzz.

A few colleagues told me, "You said exactly what I’ve always wanted to say but never dared to," and even the manager from the neighboring department asked if we could chat more about this approach. To be honest, standing on that podium, it finally clicked—sometimes the most stable way forward is learning how to navigate through uncertainty.

Reflecting on the changes over the past three months, from the initial nerves to now being able to share my thoughts so openly, a subtle shift seems to have happened. It’s not that I suddenly became incredibly confident; it’s more that I’ve started to believe that beyond doing my job well, I can have a voice; and beyond taking care of my family, I can still explore. These two things aren't mutually exclusive.

While reading to Xinyu tonight, she asked, "Daddy, are you happy today?" It made me realize that I’ve been showing up for her in a different way lately—it’s no longer a "checklist" style of parenting, but actually listening to her stories.

So grateful for the team’s trust. And grateful for every small change during this time.

**Notes:**
She asked me why her drawing looked different, and I told her that’s exactly what makes her so special.

After picking her up from school this afternoon, we didn't jump straight into homework as usual. She said she wanted to paint, so I sat with her in the living room. No goals, no pressure to make it look like anything specific. I just watched her scribble away with her markers, every now and then asking, "Daddy, look at this!"

Her little figures were all wobbly, and her sun was green. It suddenly hit me—this is what companionship is really about. It’s not about sitting there checking if she’s following the teacher’s instructions; it’s just being there with her, seeing how she imagines the world.

A very simple afternoon, but it felt different.

qa_items.question: If you hadn't "lectured" your parents with those cold data charts during that dinner in early December, but had tried listening to their perspective on life instead, do you think the atmosphere that night would have been different?
qa_items.answer: To be honest, I’ve spent a long time reflecting on this, and I still feel terrible about it. At the time, I thought that as long as I showed them that comparison chart—the one showing a "40% increase in risk over five years"—they’d understand I only had their best interests at heart. Instead, I ended up turning the whole atmosphere into something like a courtroom debate. If I had just put down my tablet and listened to why my mom was so resistant, maybe she wouldn't have cried and said I was cursing them to die, and my dad wouldn't have stormed off in a rage. I was so obsessed with having a sense of "control" that I forgot what they needed wasn't a doctor, but a son who understood their fears.

His mother’s tears and her retort—"You make it sound like we’re on our deathbeds"—made Zhou Yu realize clearly for the first time that his "rational management" and "data-driven persuasion" paled in comparison to his parents' "life wisdom" and "equanimity toward death." This realization triggered a deep-seated desire for control within him, followed by a profound sense of frustration.

qa_items.question: Sigh, I’ve been thinking—if you had let Xiaolin take your aunt for her checkup when you canceled the park plans that day, and stayed behind to be with Xinyu instead, do you think the crack in her trust would have ever happened?
qa_items.answer: Well... I’ve thought about it over and over since then. At the time, I always felt like I was the only one who truly understood my mom’s situation, so I insisted on doing everything myself. But in the end, it just made Xinyu feel like I was "lying" to her. That kind of disappointment—of "always next time, but next time never comes"—really hurts. If I had just let Xiaolin step in back then, even if it was just to sit with Xinyu in the park for a little while, she might not have cried and asked me if her health didn't matter anymore. Ultimately, I was so obsessed with controlling every responsibility that I ended up pushing the person closest to me away.

qa_items.question: Zhou Yu, you’ve always been such a steady hand, but ever since you were late for the first time in your career late last year, it’s like you’ve become a different person. qa_items.answer: It’s embarrassing to admit, but being 15 minutes late that day felt like the collapse of my entire belief system. I’ve always felt that as long as I planned meticulously enough and controlled every single variable, I could be the pillar of this family. But that day, I realized I couldn't even balance a punctual meeting with caring for my sick mother. That overwhelming sense of failure made me realize that the "sense of control" I took such pride in was actually just an illusion. It left me feeling incredibly anxious and frustrated, so when Xiaolin tried to show she cared, I ended up coldly pushing her away—simply because I couldn't face my own incompetence.

Now, I feel that being a true pillar isn't about one person stoically carrying the entire weight; it’s about having the courage to admit when I can't handle it all. Learning to face those imperfections together with Xiaolin and my parents—that authentic connection is what actually makes me feel more grounded.

qa_items.question: Zhou Yu, I remember ever since you got distracted while telling that story back in March, you seem to have been wrestling with the idea of "family responsibility." Now that nine months have passed, how do you feel your understanding of that responsibility has changed?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? These past few months have been a real turning point for me. At first, I thought responsibility meant "absolute control"—that to be competent, I had to manage everything like a computer program, from my mom’s blood sugar and my daughter’s homework to the household bills, without a single slip-up. But then I realized that trying to carry it all alone in pursuit of "perfection" actually pushed me further away from Xiaolin and the rest of the family. Now, I’m slowly starting to understand that true protection isn't about being a lone superhero; it’s about learning to drop the "I'm fine" act and facing those uncertain challenges together with my family, even if it means showing a little vulnerability.

When his wife suggested he relax, Zhou Yu defended his actions by claiming he didn't want to "worsen the situation through poor execution." This reflects a shift in his understanding of "responsibility"—from one of companionship and support to one of absolute control and flawless execution.

Zhou Yu told his mother, "Let's face this challenge together," rather than his previous stance of "I must take perfect care of you"—a shift that reflects his identity transformation from "bearing the burden alone" to "sharing it together."

When his wife asked him directly, "What are you avoiding?" Zhou Yu responded with a cold dismissal—"I told you, it's nothing"—and turned toward the wall, refusing further communication. It was his first explicit rebuff of her concern.

qa_items.question: Looking back at your emotional journey—from those moments of self-doubt in the shower, feeling like you were "failing in every role," to the sense of peace you’ve found after taking this long leave—what do you think is the biggest change in yourself between then and now?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? I think the biggest change has been learning how to "let myself off the hook." Back then, I always felt like some kind of omnipotent "system administrator." I felt I had to handle everything—my work, my daughter’s education, my parents’ health—with absolute precision. If even the slightest thing went wrong, just like it said in the evidence, I’d hide in the bathroom feeling like my entire life was a failure. Later, through my therapist’s guidance, I realized I was actually chasing a perfection that doesn't exist. Now, I’m still the same Zhou Yu who likes to have a plan, but I’ve accepted that I’m just an ordinary guardian. I have limits to my energy, and there are things I simply cannot do. This "imperfection" has actually made me feel much more at peace.

qa_items.answer: To be honest, my confidence has been going through a cycle—from firm belief to near collapse, and now to a process of re-evaluation. At the beginning of the year, I could still keep my life organized with nothing more than Excel sheets and schedules. But as my mother’s blood sugar fluctuated and my daughter began to struggle with her studies, I realized that my logic of "as long as I follow the plan, nothing can go wrong" had completely failed. That sense of being pulled in every direction by work and family made me feel utterly useless for a while; I’d even find my mind wandering while reading bedtime stories to my daughter. Now, however, with the help of my therapist, I’m trying to accept the reality that I’m not a superhero and I can’t handle everything perfectly. This idea of "letting myself off the hook" has actually allowed me to finally catch my breath.

Now, I still make lists, but it’s more about giving myself some peace of mind rather than forcing life to follow my script to the letter. Some things, you just have to learn to accept and protect.

qa_items.question: I’ve noticed a pattern with you: the moment a problem crops up, you immediately switch into "fix-it mode," frantically gathering information and mapping out solutions. While being this "reliable" is a great habit, does it ever make it hard for you to just... let go and relax?
qa_items.answer: You’re spot on with your observation. Honestly, sometimes I do feel quite exhausted. Take a while back when my mom’s blood sugar was fluctuating—my first instinct wasn’t to feel sad, but to immediately open my laptop, dig through medical data, compare hospitals, and I even made an Excel sheet to track everything. This "problem-solving" instinct does give me a temporary sense of security, but just as you said, it makes it very hard for me to stop and actually process my emotions. Even when I was reading a storybook to my daughter, my brain was automatically calculating leave days and caregiving schedules. It was in that moment I realized this need for "control" is actually quite a heavy burden.

evidence.statement: Upon receiving his mother's medical report showing elevated blood sugar, Zhou Yu immediately shifted gears. Instead of changing his clothes first, he went straight into "action mode," informing his wife in a calm but heavy tone and taking the initiative to state that he would "learn how to manage this."

Upon receiving his mother's medical report showing elevated blood sugar levels, Zhou Yu immediately shifted his daily routine—instead of changing his clothes first, he went straight into 'action mode,' informing his wife in a calm but heavy tone and taking the initiative to say he would 'learn how to manage this.'

While reading the story, Zhou Yu subconsciously began calculating emergency plans—such as how much leave he would need if his mother were hospitalized and who would look after his daughter—indicating that he had already shifted into "problem-solving mode" to cope with the anxiety triggered by his mother's health crisis.

While reading the story, Zhou Yu subconsciously began calculating emergency plans—such as how to take time off if his mother were hospitalized and who would look after his daughter—indicating that he had already shifted into "problem-solving mode" to cope with the anxiety triggered by his mother's health crisis.

choices.text: Sigh, you’re right. Sometimes I really hate this about myself. Like last time when that company project suddenly went sideways—I was wound so tight even after I got home. I was sitting there watching a movie with my wife, but my hands were glued to the notes app on my phone, scribbling away and listing out a dozen potential risks and remedies. When she asked me what the movie was even about, I couldn't say a single word. In that moment, I felt so incredibly exhausted. I’ve realized that I’ve lost the ability to be "in the moment." No matter what I’m doing, my subconscious is always rehearsing the next crisis management drill.

Can you feel it? Honestly, it was at its worst last year when my dad had his surgery. At the time, I didn't even have the heart to comfort my mom. Instead, I spent the night photocopying a dozen sets of medical records to pull strings and find experts, and I even taped every single post-op recovery milestone onto the fridge. It wasn't until one day, when my dad told me he didn't need me to do all that—he just needed me to sit by his bed and talk to him—that I realized how cold my "problem-solving" instinct really was. That sense of frustration—of trying to control everything while completely neglecting the emotional side—still haunts me to this day.

You’re absolutely right—this habit has even become a psychological burden for me. I remember one time the whole family went on a trip; it was supposed to be relaxing, but because the forecast mentioned a chance of rain, I actually stayed up all night researching indoor routes and backup transportation. I ended up printing out a five-page "Extreme Weather Contingency Guide." Once we got there, I spent the entire time checking my watch and obsessing over the route; I couldn't even focus on the scenery. While my family was having a great time, I was mentally calculating evacuation plans in case the rain got worse. That inability to let go of control—that constant need to manage everything—really makes me feel quite lonely.

It was in that moment I realized this need for "control" is actually a heavy burden.

qa_items.question: I remember last year you were going all out to manage your mom’s blood sugar—messing around with Excel sheets, digging through medical guides, and even getting into some pretty heated arguments with the family. How come lately you’ve stopped bringing up all those numbers and just seem content to sit and keep her company?
qa_items.answer: I’m honestly a bit ashamed to admit it, but I used to think I could keep everything under control with that "systematic" management approach of mine. Instead, it just made the atmosphere at home incredibly tense. Later, my mom told me that she didn't need me to be some cold, detached administrator; she just wanted me to sit with her for a while. That’s when I realized those data points and schedules were actually just tools I used to escape my own anxiety, but they ended up pushing the people closest to me away. Now, I still care about her health, but I’m much more willing to put down my phone and the logbooks just to truly be there and chat with them. Honestly, it feels much more grounding this way.

evidence.statement: Zhou Yu purchased medical guides and a blood glucose monitor all in a single day, and began crafting a detailed Excel spreadsheet for dietary management. This reflects his "over-systematized" approach to his mother's health crisis, as he attempts to regain a sense of control through data and planning.

Zhou Yu stopped bringing medical data and blood sugar logs to see his mother, turning instead to "real conversation"—a concrete manifestation of his transformation from a "perfect manager" to a "genuine companion."

Zhou Yu purchased medical guides and a blood glucose monitor all in a single day, and began crafting a detailed Excel spreadsheet for dietary management. This reflects his "over-systematized" coping mechanism in the face of his mother’s health crisis—an attempt to regain a sense of control through data and planning.

choices.text: Well, that was before I figured out the trick. I eventually realized that my aunt was really pushing back against my "command-and-control" style of management, so I quietly "automated" all the monitoring and logging. I got her a full set of smart wearables and sensor scales; the data syncs automatically to my phone, so I don't have to grill her or keep a manual log anymore. Now, when we're together, I never breathe a word about the numbers—it's all about keeping the peace at home. As long as the data in the background doesn't trigger an alarm, I just stay quiet and enjoy her company.

Don't even get me started. Last year I was just panicked and felt like I had to follow the experts' playbook to the letter. Later, a distant relative from my hometown introduced us to a very experienced doctor of Traditional Chinese Medicine. After taking my aunt to see him a few times, he told us that the biggest mistake with chronic illnesses like this is trying to "force" it. Following his advice, I ditched all those complicated metrics and switched to a gentler routine of dietary therapy and daily walks. Now I’ve realized that as long as she stays calm and in good spirits, her blood sugar levels stabilize on their own. I don't have to obsess over those numbers every single day anymore.

It’s embarrassing to admit, but I used to think I could keep everything under control with my "systematic" management style. Instead, I just ended up making the atmosphere at home incredibly tense. My mom eventually told me that she didn't need me to be some cold, detached administrator; she just wanted me to sit with her for a while. That’s when I realized all those data points and schedules were actually just tools I used to escape my own anxiety, and they were pushing the people I love most away. I still care about her health, of course, but now I’m more willing to put down my phone and the logbooks just to truly be there and talk with them. Honestly, it feels much more grounding this way.

Actually, it’s because I signed my mom up for a local senior health support group. I used to think I had to micromanage everything myself, which ended up putting a real strain on our relationship. Now, she goes there every day to practice Tai Chi and swap low-oil, low-salt recipes with people her own age; she’s actually much more self-disciplined than when I was hovering over her. I’ve realized that some things are better handled in a professional social environment. Now, I just focus on spending quality time with her on weekends and being a good daughter, instead of acting like a taskmaster all the time.

qa_items.question: I remember how you used to lose your cool whenever Xinyu couldn't solve a problem—you’d even scrap story time just to squeeze in extra tutoring. How is it that you’re like a different person now? You actually have the patience to play those "useless" games with her, and you’ve stopped nagging her about her schoolwork.
qa_items.answer: Sigh, I’m ashamed to admit it, but I used to think I should manage her like a "project," chasing efficiency and focusing on building a "solid foundation." I later realized that all it did was make her afraid of me. I remember one time I was too harsh, and seeing her cowering and trembling like that made me spend a long time reflecting in the bathroom alone. Now I feel that what she needs isn't a supervisor constantly staring at her mistakes, but a father who can truly play and laugh with her. Even if it’s just building blocks or chatting about the little bunnies in a story, as long as she’s happy, that’s the only kind of companionship that truly matters.

evidence.statement: Zhou Yu replaced the nightly twenty-minute storytime he so cherished with math tutoring. When his daughter struggled to grasp a concept, he snapped at her, "How can you not understand such a simple problem?" Afterward, he spent twenty minutes alone in the bathroom, reflecting on his actions.

Zhou Yu shifted away from the task-oriented parenting style of "checking homework before reading stories" and instead engaged in "purposeless play" with his daughter. Sensing the change in his mood, she remarked, "Daddy, you seem so happy today."

I eventually realized that all it did was make her afraid of me. I remember one time I was too harsh, and seeing her cowering and trembling like that made me spend a long time reflecting in the bathroom alone. Even if it’s just building blocks or chatting about the little bunnies in a story, as long as she’s happy, that’s the only kind of company that really matters.

To be honest, it was because I noticed she’d been complaining about headaches lately, but the checkups didn't show anything physically wrong. It wasn't until the doctor suggested I look into her psychological stress that I realized I had been pushing her too hard. On the way home from the hospital that day, Xinyu gripped the hem of my shirt so tightly and wouldn't let go; that’s when I realized she had developed this unconscious mix of dependency and fear toward me. Since then, I’ve tucked away all those tutoring materials and replaced them with the picture books and Lego sets she loves. Even if we’re just sitting on the rug zoning out or chatting about her school lunch, as long as it helps her relax, I feel the change is worth it.

Actually, I attended a lecture on "parent-child empathy" a while back, and the expert's advice really struck a chord with me. I used to always worry about her falling behind, but now I realize that if a child doesn't feel a sense of achievement in their learning, any amount of tutoring will be twice the effort for half the result. So, I’ve started trying to spend time with her after dinner first—doing some crafts she’s good at, or going for a stroll downstairs to look at the plants. Seeing her gradually open up and share fun stories from school has made me realize that protecting her curiosity is far more important than obsessing over a few wrong answers.

It all started when I took her to that outdoor summer camp. I noticed that Xinyu was incredibly confident when it came to teamwork and hands-on problem-solving, but the moment she sat back down at her desk, she became withdrawn and quiet. That night in the tent, she whispered and asked me if getting good grades was the only way to be "excellent." It really broke my heart. After we got back, I had a change of heart—there’s no point in banging our heads against the wall over those dry math problems. Now, we often play Aeroplane Chess or do puzzles together. I guide her thinking through play; she doesn’t push back, and I can stay calm enough to actually see her shine.

qa_items.question: You used to always brush things off by saying, "It’s nothing, just tired from work," trying to shoulder every bit of pressure on your own—even pushing Xiaolin away when she tried to help. But now, Xiaolin tells me you’ve actually started opening up to her, even bringing up "what if" scenarios like, "What if we hadn't bought the house?" What brought about this change in you?
qa_items.answer: It’s embarrassing to admit, but I used to think a man had to be the pillar of the family, unbreakable and stoic. I ended up turning myself into a machine that only ran by the schedule. I eventually realized that sense of "control" was just an illusion; I had overextended myself so much that I felt like I was being torn apart by work and family. It wasn't until Xiaolin pointed out that I was actually running away that I realized—instead of stumbling around in the dark alone, it’s better to lay out all those "what-ifs" I was too afraid to touch and just talk them through. This feeling of no longer fighting a solo battle is honestly so much lighter than pretending to be strong.

However, when she pushes further, pointing out his attempt to control everything, he finally admits, "I feel fragmented." This marks the pivotal moment of his transition from latent anxiety to an overt crisis.

choices.text: It’s mainly because I’ve started practicing meditation and deep reading. I used to feel that communication couldn't solve any practical problems and would only add to the other person's mental burden, so I simply chose to stay silent. Later, after reading about the power of "transparent communication" in a few books, I tried opening up about the professional confusion I’d been bottling up during a walk. To my surprise, Xiaolin’s advice was far more objective than my own aimless brooding. This process of reaching a consensus through honest dialogue made me realize that admitting one's vulnerability is actually a more advanced form of courage.

It was actually quite accidental. I was taking my parents on a road trip to the outskirts when we got a flat tire halfway there. As I was sweating buckets trying to fix it on my own, Xiaolin and my parents were right there beside me, quietly handing me tools and holding the flashlight. In that moment, it suddenly hit me: I’d always thought I was the one shielding them from the elements, but they had been supporting me in their own way all along. On the drive back, I took the initiative to bring up those "Plan Bs" for the future. I realized that as long as we’re in this together, there’s nothing to fear, even if life throws us a curveball.

To be honest, it was a recent reunion with some old classmates that really struck a chord with me. Seeing my old buddies talk about how they scrimped and saved just to stay in Shanghai, only to miss out on so much time with their families, made me realize that I’ve fallen into that same trap of "overcompensating." After I got back, Xiaolin and I sat down to review our family expenses over the past few years. We realized that much of what we called "security" was actually built on overextending ourselves, both physically and emotionally. Rather than waiting until the day I truly burn out to start regretting things, I’d rather take the initiative now to shed those unnecessary burdens and choose a more relaxed way of living.

It’s embarrassing to admit, but I used to think a man had to be the pillar of the family—unbreakable and all that. I ended up turning myself into a machine that only lived by the schedule. Later, I realized that sense of "control" was just an illusion; I had filled my life so full that I felt like I was being torn apart by work and family. It wasn't until Xiaolin pointed out that I was actually running away that I realized: instead of stumbling around in the dark alone, it’s better to lay out all those "what-ifs" I was too afraid to touch and just talk about them. This feeling of no longer fighting a solo battle is honestly so much lighter than pretending to be strong.
